topics.title: Motivation and Introduction to Optimization in Engineering Practice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET638 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Motivation and Introduction to Optimization in Engineering Practice

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Optimization** is the mathematical discipline concerned with finding the *best* (most favorable) element from a set of available alternatives, where *best* is quantified by a prescribed criterion. In engineering practice, it is the systematic process of selecting design parameters, operating conditions, or resource allocations to either **minimize** a cost function (such as material usage, energy consumption, time, or error) or **maximize** a performance function (such as efficiency, profit, reliability, or throughput), while simultaneously satisfying all physical, economic, and regulatory constraints imposed by the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> *Optimization is the act of obtaining the best result under given circumstances. In engineering, every design, planning, scheduling, or control decision can be formulated as an optimization problem in which a measure of performance (objective) is either maximized or minimized subject to constraints.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you are planning a **road trip from Kochi to Delhi** (approximately **2,600 km**). You have multiple route choices: NH44, NH48, or a mix. Your goals might be:
- Shortest distance
- Minimum fuel cost
- Least travel time
- Best scenic value
- Minimum toll charges

You cannot minimize *all* of these simultaneously — improving one may worsen another. **Optimization** is the formal mathematical machinery that helps you:
1. Quantify your preference into a single numerical score (e.g., total cost in INR).
2. List all available routes (the *feasible set*).
3. Systematically search and identify the route giving the lowest total cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Translation:** Replace the road trip with designing a gear, a power system, a chemical reactor, or a manufacturing schedule. The *route* becomes a vector of *design variables*, the *cost* becomes the *objective function*, and the *traffic rules / road width limits* become the *constraints*. Optimization finds the best design.

## 1.3 Why Optimization is Indispensable in Engineering

Modern engineering systems are characterized by:
- **Multi-variable complexity:** A Boeing 747 has over **6 million** parts designed by thousands of engineers.
- **Conflicting objectives:** Lighter aircraft (less fuel) vs stronger aircraft (more safety).
- **Scarce resources:** Limited budget, material, time, and energy.
- **Stringent performance demands:** Modern IC engines target thermal efficiency above **45%**.
- **Sustainability mandates:** Carbon footprint reduction, zero-waste goals.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Students:** If a problem involves a *choice*, a *best*, a *minimum*, a *maximum*, a *least cost*, or a *highest output* — **it is an optimization problem in disguise.**

## 1.4 Fundamental Terminology (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Decision / Design Variable | $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ | Quantities the designer can control |
| Objective Function | $f(\mathbf{x})$ | The performance criterion to be minimized or maximized |
| Constraints | $g_j(\mathbf{x}), \ h_k(\mathbf{x})$ | Restrictions arising from physics, economy, or safety |
| Feasible Region | $S$ | The set of all $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying every constraint |
| Optimal Solution | $\mathbf{x}^{\ast}$ | The point in $S$ yielding the best value of $f$ |
| Global Optimum | $\mathbf{x}_{g}^{\ast}$ | The overall best solution over the entire feasible region |
| Local Optimum | $\mathbf{x}_{l}^{\ast}$ | The best solution in a small neighborhood of $\mathbf{x}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Mathematical Model of an Optimization Problem

The canonical (standard) form taught across all KTU 2024 optimization modules is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find} \quad & \mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \in \mathbb{R}^n \\
\text{that minimizes} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p \\
& x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

### Logical Breakdown — The *Why* and *How* Behind Each Component

- **Decision Vector $\mathbf{x}$:** Every problem has controllable inputs. The dimension $n$ defines the *degrees of freedom*. A higher $n$ ⇒ exponentially harder problem (*curse of dimensionality*).
- **Objective Function $f(\mathbf{x})$:** The scalar metric of *goodness*. If the engineering goal is *maximization* (e.g., profit, efficiency), we convert it to minimization via $\min\{-f(\mathbf{x})\}$ — this is a **KTU-standard reduction**.
- **Inequality Constraints $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$:** These represent upper / lower limits (e.g., stress $\le \sigma_{allowable}$, temperature $\le T_{max}$).
- **Equality Constraints $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$:** These represent physical laws or fixed specifications (e.g., energy balance, Kirchhoff's laws).
- **Bound Constraints $x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)}$:** Side limits on variables (e.g., diameter must be positive, voltage must be in $[0, 400]$ V).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** A problem with no constraints is called *unconstrained*; with only bounds it is called *bound-constrained*; with general $g_j$ and $h_k$ it is called *generally constrained*. KTU Module 2 onward deals with all three classes.

## 2.2 Classification of Optimization Problems

Optimization problems are classified along **five canonical axes** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Axis | Class A | Class B | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nature of variables | Continuous | Discrete / Integer | $x \in [0, 5]$ vs $x \in \{0,1,2,3,4,5\}$ |
| Nature of $f, g, h$ | Linear (LP) | Nonlinear (NLP) | $3x + 5y$ vs $x^2 + \sin(y)$ |
| Number of objectives | Single (SOO) | Multi (MOO) | Minimize cost only vs Minimize cost AND weight |
| Determinism | Deterministic | Stochastic | Fixed load vs Random wind speed |
| Convexity | Convex | Non-convex | Bowl-shaped $f$ vs multi-modal $f$ |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1 Cheat Sheet)

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Use | Units (typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard LP form | $\min\ c^{T}\mathbf{x} \ \text{s.t.}\ A\mathbf{x} \le b$ | Production planning | Cost in INR |
| Standard NLP form | $\min\ f(\mathbf{x}) \ \text{s.t.}\ g(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ | Optimal reactor design | Depends on objective |
| Convex set condition | $f(\lambda\mathbf{x} + (1-\lambda)\mathbf{y}) \le \lambda f(\mathbf{x}) + (1-\lambda)f(\mathbf{y})$ | Guarantees global optimum | Dimensionless |
| Optimum condition (unconstrained) | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{\ast}) = \mathbf{0}$ | Necessary for interior optimum | Vector of derivatives |
| Multiplier rule (Lagrange) | $\mathcal{L} = f(\mathbf{x}) + \lambda^{T}h(\mathbf{x})$ | Equality-constrained problems | Scalar Lagrangian |
| KKT stationarity | $\nabla f + \sum \mu_j \nabla g_j + \sum \lambda_k \nabla h_k = 0$ | General constrained problems | Vector equation |
| Feasibility condition | $g_j(\mathbf{x}^{\ast}) \le 0, \ h_k(\mathbf{x}^{\ast}) = 0$ | Final check at optimum | Inequality / equality |
| Curvature test | $\mathbf{v}^{T} H(\mathbf{x}^{\ast}) \mathbf{v} > 0 \ \forall \mathbf{v} \ne 0$ | Confirms local minimum | Hessian matrix test |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Optimization underpins virtually every modern engineering discipline:

- **Mechanical:** Topology optimization reduces aircraft bracket mass by **30–50%** while preserving stiffness.
- **Electrical:** Optimal power flow (OPF) in a 1000-bus grid saves millions of units of electricity annually.
- **Civil:** Structural optimization minimizes cement use in high-rises, cutting **embodied carbon by up to 40%**.
- **Chemical:** Reactor yield maximization in ammonia synthesis raised conversion from **15% (1910) to 70%+ (modern)**.
- **Industrial / Production:** Inventory models (EOQ) reduce holding cost by **20–35%** in supply chains.
- **Computer Science & AI:** Loss-function minimization is the heart of every neural network training loop.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting a Maximization Problem to Standard (Minimization) Form

A KTU favorite: the *profit maximization* problem. Let the engineering decision yield profit $P(\mathbf{x})$ we wish to **maximize**.

**Step 1.** Write the original problem as posed in the physical world.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{maximize} \quad & P(\mathbf{x}) = 10 x_1 + 8 x_2 \quad \text{(INR profit from two products)} \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 2 x_1 + x_2 \le 40 \quad \text{(raw material, kg)} \\
& x_1 + 3 x_2 \le 30 \quad \text{(labour hours)} \\
& x_1, x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Recognize the canonical form. KTU (and globally) requires *minimization* for uniformity of theory (KKT conditions, duality, software solvers like CPLEX, Gurobi, MATLAB all assume $\min$).

**Step 3.** Apply the algebraic identity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max P(\mathbf{x}) \quad &\equiv \quad \min \big[ -P(\mathbf{x}) \big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** Substitute. The transformed standard form is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) = -10 x_1 - 8 x_2 \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 2 x_1 + x_2 \le 40 \\
& x_1 + 3 x_2 \le 30 \\
& x_1, x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.** Verify the equivalence. If the original optimum was $P^{\ast} = 120$ INR, the new problem returns $f^{\ast} = -120$. The optimal *decision* $(x_1^{\ast}, x_2^{\ast})$ is identical. This is a **rigorously invertible transformation** — no information is lost.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Many students forget to flip the inequality when multiplying by a negative number during transformation. Always re-verify the constraint direction after every negation.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Unconstrained Optimum (First-Order Necessary Condition)

**Claim:** If $f(\mathbf{x})$ is differentiable and $\mathbf{x}^{\ast}$ is a local minimum in the interior of the feasible region, then:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{\ast}) = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 1.** Taylor-expand $f$ around $\mathbf{x}^{\ast}$ for a small perturbation $\mathbf{d}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}^{\ast} + \mathbf{d}) = f(\mathbf{x}^{\ast}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{\ast})^{T} \mathbf{d} + \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{d}^{T} H(\mathbf{x}^{\ast}) \mathbf{d} + \mathcal{O}(\Vert \mathbf{d} \Vert^{3})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Since $\mathbf{x}^{\ast}$ is a local minimum, $f(\mathbf{x}^{\ast} + \mathbf{d}) \ge f(\mathbf{x}^{\ast})$ for all small $\Vert \mathbf{d} \Vert$.

**Step 3.** Therefore the first-order change must be zero for both $+\mathbf{d}$ and $-\mathbf{d}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{\ast})^{T} \mathbf{d} &= 0 \quad \text{and} \quad -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{\ast})^{T} \mathbf{d} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** The only vector $\mathbf{g}$ satisfying $\mathbf{g}^{T}\mathbf{d} = 0$ for *every* direction $\mathbf{d}$ is the zero vector itself. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{\ast}) &= \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.** This is the *stationarity* condition. It is **necessary** but not **sufficient**. Sufficiency requires the second-order test: $H(\mathbf{x}^{\ast})$ (Hessian) must be positive definite.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Engineering Domains to Optimization Classes

The following matrix links each major engineering domain to the optimization class it most commonly employs. This is a KTU-recommended study aid for Module 1 questions.

| Engineering Domain | Typical Objective $f$ | Typical Variables $\mathbf{x}$ | Typical Constraints | Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Structural Design | Minimize weight | Cross-section, thickness | Stress $\le \sigma_{y}$, deflection $\le \delta_{max}$ | NLP, Convex |
| Power System OPF | Minimize fuel cost | Generator outputs | Power balance, line limits | NLP, Large-scale |
| Production Planning | Maximize profit | Units of each product | Raw material, labour, time | LP |
| Chemical Reactor | Maximize yield | Temperature, pressure, residence time | Equilibrium, kinetics | NLP, Non-convex |
| Network Routing | Minimize latency | Path assignments | Bandwidth, topology | Integer / Combinatorial |
| Machine Learning | Minimize loss | Weights $\mathbf{w}$ of the model | None (or norm-based regularization) | NLP, Non-convex |
| Inventory Control | Minimize total cost | Order quantity, reorder point | Storage capacity, demand | LP / Stochastic |
| Aerospace Trajectory | Minimize fuel | Thrust profile, angle of attack | Dynamic equations, bounds | Dynamic / NLP |

## 3.4 Important Reduction: Equality Constraint to Two Inequalities

Any equality constraint $h(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ can be written equivalently as the pair:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h(\mathbf{x}) \le 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad -h(\mathbf{x}) \le 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This conversion is used in algorithms that can only handle inequality forms (e.g., cutting-plane methods).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Canonical Optimization Process Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete decision-engineering workflow from problem identification to validated optimal solution. It is a **block-level functional architecture** (as mandated for complex conceptual topics).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Real-World Engineering Problem]) --> stepB[Identify Decision Variables x]
    stepB --> stepC[Define Objective Function f]
    stepC --> stepD[List Constraints: g and h]
    stepD --> stepE[Build Mathematical Model]
    stepE --> stepF{Is Model Convex?}
    stepF -- Yes --> stepG[Apply Deterministic Solver]
    stepF -- No --> stepH[Apply Heuristic / Metaheuristic]
    stepG --> stepI[Obtain Candidate x star]
    stepH --> stepI[Obtain Candidate x star]
    stepI --> stepJ[Validate Feasibility]
    stepJ --> stepK{Constraints Satisfied?}
    stepK -- Yes --> stepL[Validate Optimality]
    stepK -- No --> stepM[Refine Model or Solver]
    stepM --> stepE
    stepL --> stepN{Optimality Confirmed?}
    stepN -- Yes --> stepO([Implement Optimal Design])
    stepN -- No --> stepP[Re-examine Problem Assumptions]
    stepP --> stepB
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- *Top-down* flow shows the *engineering* sequence: understand → model → solve → validate → deploy.
- *Feedback loops* (stepM, stepP) emphasize that optimization is **iterative**, not a one-shot calculation.
- The *convexity branch* (stepF) is critical — convex problems guarantee *global* optima; non-convex problems may trap the solver in a *local* optimum.

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Optimization Techniques

The following Mermaid tree presents the taxonomy of methods introduced in KTU 2024 Module 1 and expanded in Modules 2–5.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Optimization Techniques] --> classB[By Variable Nature]
    rootA --> classC[By Equation Nature]
    rootA --> classD[By Objective Count]
    rootA --> classE[By Constraint Type]

    classB --> leafB1[Continuous]
    classB --> leafB2[Discrete Integer]
    classB --> leafB3[Mixed Integer]

    classC --> leafC1[Linear LP]
    classC --> leafC2[Nonlinear NLP]
    classC --> leafC3[Quadratic QP]

    classD --> leafD1[Single SOO]
    classD --> leafD2[Multi MOO]

    classE --> leafE1[Unconstrained]
    classE --> leafE2[Equality]
    classE --> leafE3[Inequality]
    classE --> leafE4[Bound only]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common Module 1 question asks you to *classify* a given problem. Walk through the five axes above systematically. Mentioning the *conjugate axis* (e.g., the problem is *nonlinear + constrained + multi-objective*) earns full marks.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: The Optimization Engine in an Engineering Design Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[Conceptual Design] --> blockB[Mathematical Model]
    blockB --> blockC[Optimization Algorithm]
    blockC --> blockD[Optimal Parameters]
    blockD --> blockE[Detailed CAD / CAE]
    blockE --> blockF{Performance Meets Spec?}
    blockF -- Yes --> blockG([Manufacture])
    blockF -- No --> blockH[Update Constraints or Objective]
    blockH --> blockB
```

This figure is a **functional architecture** that maps the *role* of optimization within the larger engineering design cycle, in line with KTU outcome-based education (CO) mapping.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define optimization in the context of engineering design. List the essential components that constitute a mathematical optimization problem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Optimization is the mathematical methodology of finding the most favorable (minimum-cost or maximum-performance) value of an objective function from a set of feasible alternatives, subject to prescribed constraints.

**Essential components (2 marks):**
1. **Decision / Design Variables** $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ — the controllable inputs.
2. **Objective Function** $f(\mathbf{x})$ — the scalar performance metric to be optimized.
3. **Constraints** $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ and $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ — the physical, economic, and regulatory restrictions.
4. **Feasible Region** $S$ — the set of all admissible $\mathbf{x}$.
5. **Optimal Solution** $\mathbf{x}^{\ast}$ — the point in $S$ that yields the best value of $f$.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Distinguish between constrained and unconstrained optimization problems. Give one engineering example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Unconstrained | Constrained |
|---|---|---|
| Form | $\min\ f(\mathbf{x})$ | $\min\ f(\mathbf{x})\ \text{s.t.}\ g, h$ |
| Feasible region | Entire $\mathbb{R}^n$ | Restricted subset $S \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| Optimum condition | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{\ast}) = 0$ | KKT conditions |
| Difficulty | Easier | Harder; needs active-set, penalty, or barrier methods |

**Examples (1 mark each):**
- *Unconstrained:* Minimize the air drag of a freely falling sphere's cross-sectional geometry with no restrictions.
- *Constrained:* Minimize the mass of a truss bridge subject to stress $\le 250$ MPa and deflection $\le 5$ cm.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Every Part B question in a Module test offers an **internal choice**. You must attempt **one** of the two alternatives. Each alternative carries sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a) Discuss the motivation for applying optimization techniques in modern engineering practice, with at least four domain-specific examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The motivation for optimization in engineering is rooted in three universal pressures: (1) **resource scarcity**, (2) **performance intensification**, and (3) **sustainability mandates**. Modern systems can no longer afford *trial-and-error* design — the search space is too vast and the cost of suboptimality too high.

**Domain-specific examples:**

1. **Aerospace Structural Design (7 marks: each example ≈ 1.5):** Modern wing ribs are designed using *topology optimization*, which iteratively removes low-stress material to create organic, bone-like geometries. The result is up to **40% weight reduction**, directly translating to lower fuel burn and higher payload. Example: Airbus A320 family wing brackets.

2. **Power System Operation:** The *Optimal Power Flow* (OPF) problem dispatches generators every 15 minutes to minimize total fuel cost across a national grid while satisfying Kirchhoff's laws and line thermal limits. India's national grid saves **several thousand crore INR per year** through OPF.

3. **Chemical Process Engineering:** In an ammonia or petroleum refinery, optimization of reactor temperature, pressure, and catalyst feed ratio directly influences conversion percentage. Historical improvements in ammonia synthesis (Haber-Bosch) efficiency from **15% to 70%+** owe much to systematic optimization.

4. **Manufacturing & Supply Chain:** The *Economic Order Quantity* (EOQ) and *Vehicle Routing Problem* (VRP) minimize inventory holding cost and transportation cost respectively. Companies like Amazon and Flipkart rely on these to deliver next-day at minimum cost.

5. **Civil Engineering:** Optimal design of reinforced-concrete beams minimizes cement and steel usage while meeting IS-code strength and serviceability limits, reducing embodied carbon and project cost.

6. **Machine Learning & AI:** The *training* of every neural network is an optimization problem — minimizing the loss function over millions of parameters using gradient-based methods (SGD, Adam).

7. **Biomedical Engineering:** Optimization is used in designing prosthetic limbs (minimize mass + maximize strength), radiation therapy planning (minimize dose to healthy tissue + maximize tumor dose), and drug dosage.

**Conclusion:** Optimization is no longer a niche mathematical luxury; it is the **backbone of competitive, sustainable, and innovative engineering**.

---

**(b) Formulate the general mathematical model of an optimization problem. State clearly the meaning of each term and discuss the classification of optimization problems along five standard axes. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The general mathematical model is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find} \quad & \mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \\
\text{to minimize} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p \\
& x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Meaning of each term (3.5 marks):**
- $\mathbf{x}$: vector of $n$ design variables (controllable inputs).
- $f(\mathbf{x})$: objective function (scalar to be minimized).
- $g_j$: $j$-th inequality constraint.
- $h_k$: $k$-th equality constraint.
- $x_i^{(L)}, x_i^{(U)}$: lower and upper bounds on $x_i$.
- The feasible set $S = \{\mathbf{x} \mid g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0,\ h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0,\ x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)}\}$.

**Five classification axes (3.5 marks):**
1. **Nature of variables** — continuous vs discrete vs mixed.
2. **Nature of functions** — linear (LP) vs nonlinear (NLP) vs quadratic (QP).
3. **Number of objectives** — single (SOO) vs multi-objective (MOO).
4. **Determinism** — deterministic vs stochastic.
5. **Convexity** — convex (guaranteed global opt) vs non-convex (may have local traps).

---

### Question B (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the role of optimization in the engineering design cycle. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Conceptual Design
        |
        v
Mathematical Model  <-------+
        |                   |
        v                   |
Optimization Algorithm      |
        |                   |
        v                   |
Optimal Parameters          |
        |                   |
        v                   |
Detailed CAD / CAE          |
        |                   |
        v                   |
Performance Check           |
   |            |           |
  Yes           No----------+
   |
   v
Manufacture
```

**Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Conceptual Design:** Initial idea and rough specifications.
2. **Mathematical Model:** Translate physical problem into $f$, $g$, $h$ formulation.
3. **Optimization Algorithm:** Solver (LP, NLP, heuristic) processes the model.
4. **Optimal Parameters:** Best values of design variables emerge.
5. **Detailed CAD / CAE:** Optimal parameters implemented in CAD; FEA/CFD verifies physics.
6. **Performance Check:** Compare simulated performance with specifications.
7. **Feedback:** If specifications are not met, the model is refined (constraints tightened, objective re-weighted) and the loop repeats.
8. **Manufacture:** Once validated, the optimal design is fabricated.

Optimization thus acts as the **central nervous system** of modern design — providing the quantitative link between idea and implementation.

---

**(b) Classify the following real-world problems and identify the optimization class they belong to. Justify your answer. (7 marks)**

**(i)** *Minimize the surface area of a cylindrical can of volume 500 ml.*

**(ii)** *Allocate 5 different raw materials to 3 products to maximize profit with limited material availability.*

**(iii)** *Find the shortest route visiting 25 cities exactly once.*

**(iv)** *Design a control policy for an autonomous vehicle under random wind disturbances.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Cylinder design (1.75 marks):** 
- Variables: radius $r$ and height $h$ — **continuous**.
- Objective: $A(r, h) = 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r h$ — **nonlinear** in the variables.
- Constraint: $V = \pi r^2 h = 500$ — **nonlinear equality**.
- **Class: NLP with equality constraint.**

**(ii) Raw material allocation (1.75 marks):**
- Variables: $x_{ij}$ = amount of material $i$ used in product $j$ — **continuous**.
- Objective: total profit $\sum p_j x_{ij}$ — **linear**.
- Constraint: $\sum_j x_{ij} \le a_i$ for each material $i$ — **linear inequality**.
- **Class: Linear Programming (LP).**

**(iii) Travelling Salesman (1.75 marks):**
- Variables: binary order $x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$ — **discrete / integer**.
- Objective: minimize total distance — **linear** in $x_{ij}$.
- Constraint: each city visited exactly once — **integer equality**.
- **Class: Integer Programming / Combinatorial Optimization (NP-hard).**

**(iv) Autonomous vehicle under random wind (1.75 marks):**
- Variables: control input $u(t)$ — **continuous, time-varying**.
- Objective: minimize expected energy / tracking error — **stochastic**.
- Constraint: dynamics + bounds — **stochastic / dynamic**.
- **Class: Stochastic Dynamic Optimization / Optimal Control.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 1 Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert maximization to minimization** (1 mark lost in any standard form question).
> 2. **Confusing *necessary* and *sufficient* optimality conditions** — $\nabla f = 0$ is necessary but not sufficient; the Hessian positive-definite test is the sufficiency condition.
> 3. **Omitting units or dimensions** when stating engineering objectives — always mention physical units (INR, kg, m, MPa, etc.).
> 4. **Miscounting the number of decision variables** — students often ignore bound constraints while counting $n$.
> 5. **Confusing *local* with *global* optimum** in non-convex problems — for non-convex $f$, multiple local optima can exist.
> 6. **Skipping the feasibility check** — an "optimal" point that violates a constraint is *infeasible* and earns zero marks in design interpretation.
> 7. **Not drawing the block diagram** when asked — diagrams carry **2–3 marks** in KTU's marking scheme.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly):**

- **Definition:** Optimization = best of many, quantified mathematically.
- **Five Core Components:** Variables, Objective, Constraints, Feasible Set, Optimal Solution.
- **Standard Form:** Always $\min\ f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $g \le 0, h = 0, x^{(L)} \le x \le x^{(U)}$.
- **Max → Min Trick:** Replace $P$ with $-P$; keep the variables identical.
- **First-Order Necessary Condition (unconstrained):** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{\ast}) = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Second-Order Sufficient Condition:** $H(\mathbf{x}^{\ast})$ is positive definite.
- **Five Classification Axes:** Variable type, function type, objective count, determinism, convexity.
- **Convexity is the Holy Grail:** Convex problems have a *unique* global minimum; non-convex problems are *hard*.
- **Engineering is Multi-Objective by Nature:** Most real problems are MOO — trade-off curves (Pareto fronts) are the answer.
- **LP, NLP, IP** are the three pillars of Module 1 terminology.
- **Optimization is Iterative:** Always check feasibility, then optimality, then physical realizability.
- **Reduction Rule:** $h(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ can be replaced by $h \le 0$ and $-h \le 0$.
- **Units Matter:** Never present a numerical optimum without units in engineering answers.
- **KKT Preview (Module 2):** Stationarity, primal feasibility, dual feasibility, complementary slackness.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Motivation and Introduction to Optimization in Engineering Practice**

*Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels 1–4: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Single Variable Functions and Optimality Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET638 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Single Variable Functions and Optimality Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Optimization Problem in a Single Variable

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework for *Optimization Techniques*, the single-variable unconstrained optimization problem is formally stated as:

> **Find $x^* \in \mathbb{R}$ that minimizes (or maximizes) $f(x)$ over all $x \in \mathbb{R}$**

Mathematically expressed as:

$$
\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}} f(x) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \max_{x \in \mathbb{R}} f(x)
$$

where $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is a real-valued **single variable function** and $x^*$ is called the **optimal point** or **stationary point** (also referred to as the *optimum*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In Module 1, the focus is restricted to functions of **one independent variable** with **no constraints** on $x$. Multivariable extensions (Module 2 onwards) and constrained problems (later modules) build directly upon these foundational optimality conditions.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Optimal Points

A function $f(x)$ may possess several different kinds of "best" points. KTU examiners frequently test the precise definitions:

| Type of Optimum | Notation | Formal Definition |
|-----------------|----------|-------------------|
| **Global (Absolute) Minimum** | $f(x^*) \le f(x), \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$ | Strictly lowest value of the function across the **entire real line** |
| **Local (Relative) Minimum** | $\exists \, \delta > 0 \mid f(x^*) \le f(x)$ for all $x \in (x^*-\delta, x^*+\delta)$ | Lowest value in a small **neighborhood** of $x^*$ |
| **Strong Local Minimum** | $f(x^*) < f(x)$ for all $x$ near $x^*$ (excluding $x^*$) | Strict inequality in the neighborhood |
| **Global Maximum** | $f(x^*) \ge f(x), \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$ | Strictly highest value across the entire real line |
| **Local Maximum** | $\exists \, \delta > 0 \mid f(x^*) \ge f(x)$ in $(x^*-\delta, x^*+\delta)$ | Highest value in a small neighborhood |
| **Infimum / Supremum** | $f^* = \inf f(x)$ or $f^* = \sup f(x)$ | Greatest lower / least upper bound (may not be attained) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Every global optimum is a local optimum, but the converse is FALSE.**
> This is one of the most commonly tested distinctions in KTU examinations. A function can have many local minima but only one (or none) global minimum.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Hiker

Imagine you are a **hiker on a mountain range** and you want to reach the lowest valley (minimization) or the highest peak (maximization):

- **Global optimum** = the absolute lowest valley of the *entire mountain range*. You must survey the whole range to confirm.
- **Local optimum** = the bottom of *any one valley*. From a vantage point inside that valley, every direction goes up — but a deeper valley may exist elsewhere.
- **Stationary point (where $f'(x)=0$)** = the spot on the slope where the ground is perfectly **flat under your feet** — you are neither climbing nor descending.
- **Inflection points** = mountain passes where the slope is zero *but* the ground curves differently on each side (like a saddle tangent to the trail).

This analogy is helpful because it captures the **two fundamental questions** KTU Module 1 asks:
1. **Where** should we look for optima? *(Answer: at stationary points, $f'(x^*) = 0$)*.
2. **How do we distinguish** a maximum from a minimum? *(Answer: by examining $f''(x^*)$ — convexity / concavity).*

---

## 1.4 The Concept of Convexity (Single Variable)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convexity is the *single most important property* that distinguishes a global minimum from a mere local minimum.**

A function $f(x)$ is called **convex** on an interval $I$ if for every $x_1, x_2 \in I$ and every $\lambda \in [0,1]$:

$$
f\bigl(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2\bigr) \le \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)
$$

Geometrically, the **chord** (straight line) joining any two points on the graph lies **above** the curve. Equivalently, for **twice-differentiable** functions:

$$
f''(x) \ge 0, \quad \forall x \in I \iff f \text{ is convex on } I
$$

A function is **strictly convex** when $f''(x) > 0$ for all $x \in I$. **Strictly convex functions have a unique global minimum**, which is *also* the unique stationary point. This is the **global optimality theorem** for convex single-variable functions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A strictly convex function can have **at most one** local minimum, and that minimum is automatically the **global** minimum. This property is heavily exploited in engineering design optimization (e.g., minimum cost, minimum loss, minimum energy problems).

---

## 1.5 Necessary vs. Sufficient Conditions

The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes a clear distinction:

- **Necessary Condition**: A property that *every* optimal point *must* satisfy. *(If $x^*$ is a local min/max, then $f'(x^*) = 0$ — always.)*
- **Sufficient Condition**: A property that *guarantees* $x^*$ is optimal. *(If $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$, then $x^*$ is a local minimum — guaranteed.)*

> [!WARNING]
> A common student mistake in KTU exams is to treat the necessary condition as sufficient. $f'(x^*) = 0$ is necessary but **NOT** sufficient — the point could be a maximum, an inflection point, or a saddle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fermat's Theorem — The Necessary Condition

> **Statement (Fermat's Theorem, 1630s):**
> If $f(x)$ is differentiable at $x^*$ and $x^*$ is a local minimum or maximum of $f$, then:
> $$f'(x^*) = 0$$

**Why this is true (intuition):** At any local extremum, the graph is *locally flat* — the slope of the tangent line must be horizontal. If the slope were non-zero, the function would strictly increase (or decrease) on one side, contradicting the "local best" nature of the optimum.

**Points where $f'(x) = 0$ are called:**
- **Stationary points** (UK convention)
- **Critical points** (US convention)
- KTU textbooks use both terms interchangeably.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The converse of Fermat's Theorem is FALSE. $f'(x) = 0$ does NOT imply that $x$ is a local extremum — counter-example: $f(x) = x^3$ at $x = 0$ has $f'(0) = 0$ but no local extremum (it's an inflection point).

---

## 2.2 The Second-Derivative Sufficient Condition

> **Theorem (Second-Order Sufficient Condition):**
> Let $f$ be twice continuously differentiable in a neighborhood of $x^*$. If:
> 1. $f'(x^*) = 0$ (stationary)
> 2. $f''(x^*) > 0$ (positive curvature)
>
> then $x^*$ is a **strict local minimum**.
>
> Similarly, $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) < 0$ implies $x^*$ is a **strict local maximum**.

**Intuition:** The sign of $f''(x^*)$ tells you the **direction of curvature**:
- $f''(x^*) > 0$ → the graph **bends upward** (cup-shaped) → local valley → minimum.
- $f''(x^*) < 0$ → the graph **bends downward** (cap-shaped) → local peak → maximum.
- $f''(x^*) = 0$ → the test is **inconclusive** — go to higher derivatives.

---

## 2.3 The Higher-Order Derivative Test

When $f''(x^*) = 0$, the second test fails. We proceed to the **$n^{\text{th}}$-order test**:

> **Higher-Order Derivative Test:**
> Suppose $f^{(k)}(x^*) = 0$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n-1$ and $f^{(n)}(x^*) \neq 0$.
> - If $n$ is **even** and $f^{(n)}(x^*) > 0$, then $x^*$ is a **local minimum**.
> - If $n$ is **even** and $f^{(n)}(x^*) < 0$, then $x^*$ is a **local maximum**.
> - If $n$ is **odd**, then $x^*$ is **neither** (it is an inflection point).

**Mnemonic for KTU exams:** *"Even order = extremum; Odd order = saddle/inflection. Sign of derivative tells you max or min."*

---

## 2.4 The First-Derivative Test (Sign-Change Test)

This test does not require second derivatives. Inspect the **sign of $f'(x)$** on either side of $x^*$:

| Sign Pattern of $f'(x)$ around $x^*$ | Conclusion |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| $- \to +$ (changes from negative to positive) | **Local Minimum** |
| $+ \to -$ (changes from positive to negative) | **Local Maximum** |
| $+$ to $+$ (no sign change) | Not an extremum |
| $-$ to $-$ (no sign change) | Not an extremum |

> [!NOTE]
> The first-derivative test works even when $f$ is **not twice-differentiable**, making it useful for piecewise and absolute-value functions. KTU problems on $|x|$ type functions are very common.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Property / Test | Mathematical Condition | Type | Result |
|---|-----------------|------------------------|------|--------|
| 1 | **Fermat's Necessary Condition** | $f'(x^*) = 0$ | Necessary | $x^*$ is a candidate extremum |
| 2 | **Second-Order Sufficient Test (min)** | $f'(x^*)=0, \, f''(x^*) > 0$ | Sufficient | $x^*$ is strict local **minimum** |
| 3 | **Second-Order Sufficient Test (max)** | $f'(x^*)=0, \, f''(x^*) < 0$ | Sufficient | $x^*$ is strict local **maximum** |
| 4 | **Second-Order Test Inconclusive** | $f'(x^*)=0, \, f''(x^*) = 0$ | Inconclusive | Use higher-order or first-derivative test |
| 5 | **n-th Order Test (min)** | $f^{(1..n-1)}(x^*)=0$, $n$ even, $f^{(n)}(x^*)>0$ | Sufficient | Local **minimum** |
| 6 | **n-th Order Test (max)** | $f^{(1..n-1)}(x^*)=0$, $n$ even, $f^{(n)}(x^*)<0$ | Sufficient | Local **maximum** |
| 7 | **n-th Order Test (saddle)** | $f^{(1..n-1)}(x^*)=0$, $n$ odd | — | Inflection / saddle point |
| 8 | **First-Derivative Test (min)** | $f'$ goes from $-$ to $+$ at $x^*$ | Sufficient | Local **minimum** |
| 9 | **First-Derivative Test (max)** | $f'$ goes from $+$ to $-$ at $x^*$ | Sufficient | Local **maximum** |
| 10 | **Convexity Criterion** | $f''(x) \ge 0, \, \forall x \in I$ | Sufficient | Every local min is global min on $I$ |
| 11 | **Strict Convexity** | $f''(x) > 0, \, \forall x \in I$ | Sufficient | Unique global minimum on $I$ |
| 12 | **Boundary Point Test** (closed interval $[a,b]$) | Compare $f(a), f(b), f(x^*)$ | Sufficient | Global min = $\min\{f(a), f(b), f(x^*)\}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** Conditions 2, 3, 5, 6, 8, 9 are **sufficient** but **not necessary** (they guarantee optimality, but optimality can occur without them). Condition 1 is **necessary** but **not sufficient**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

Optimality of single-variable functions is the mathematical heart of:

- **Mechanical Engineering:** Minimum-weight design of a uniform strength beam; finding the optimal cross-section that minimizes material cost.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Minimizing power loss in a transmission line by optimal choice of conductor radius (Kelvin's Law).
- **Civil Engineering:** Optimal depth of a foundation for minimum cost; optimal column height for maximum buckling load.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Finding the optimal residence time in a reactor to maximize conversion.
- **Economics & Management:** Profit maximization, cost minimization, break-even analysis — all reduce to single-variable optimization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Connection:** Many Module 1 KTU problems are framed as *word problems* from these engineering domains. The student is expected to **formulate** the objective function, **differentiate**, set $f'(x) = 0$, and verify with $f''(x)$ to determine the optimal engineering parameter.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Fermat's Necessary Condition

**Setup:** Let $x^*$ be an interior local minimum of $f$ and assume $f$ is differentiable at $x^*$.

**Step 1 — Define the difference quotient from the right:**

By definition of the right-hand derivative:

$$
f'(x^*) = \lim_{h \to 0^+} \frac{f(x^* + h) - f(x^*)}{h}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the local minimum property:**

Since $x^*$ is a local minimum, for all sufficiently small $h$:

$$
f(x^* + h) \ge f(x^*)
$$

which means:

$$
f(x^* + h) - f(x^*) \ge 0
$$

**Step 3 — Bound the quotient from the right:**

For $h > 0$, dividing the non-negative numerator by the positive denominator:

$$
\frac{f(x^* + h) - f(x^*)}{h} \ge 0
$$

Taking the limit $h \to 0^+$:

$$
\lim_{h \to 0^+} \frac{f(x^* + h) - f(x^*)}{h} \ge 0
$$

Therefore, the right-hand derivative satisfies:

$$
f'(x^*) \ge 0
$$

**Step 4 — Repeat the argument from the left:**

Similarly, for $h < 0$ (so $h$ is negative and the numerator is non-negative):

$$
\frac{f(x^* + h) - f(x^*)}{h} \le 0
$$

Taking $h \to 0^-$:

$$
f'(x^*) \le 0
$$

**Step 5 — Combine the two inequalities:**

$$
f'(x^*) \ge 0 \quad \text{and} \quad f'(x^*) \le 0
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{f'(x^*) = 0}
$$

This is the **first-order necessary condition** for an interior local extremum of a differentiable function.

> [!NOTE]
> The same derivation works with $\le$ replaced by $\ge$ for a local maximum, yielding the identical conclusion $f'(x^*) = 0$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Second-Order Sufficient Condition

**Setup:** Suppose $f$ is twice continuously differentiable near $x^*$, with $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$. We want to prove that $x^*$ is a strict local minimum.

**Step 1 — Use Taylor's Theorem with remainder:**

By the second-order Taylor expansion around $x^*$:

$$
f(x^* + h) = f(x^*) + f'(x^*) h + \frac{1}{2} f''(\xi) h^2
$$

for some $\xi$ between $x^*$ and $x^* + h$.

**Step 2 — Substitute $f'(x^*) = 0$:**

$$
f(x^* + h) = f(x^*) + \frac{1}{2} f''(\xi) h^2
$$

**Step 3 — Use continuity of $f''$:**

Since $f''(x^*) > 0$ and $f''$ is continuous, there exists a neighborhood of $x^*$ (say, $|h| < \delta$) where:

$$
f''(\xi) > \frac{f''(x^*)}{2} > 0
$$

**Step 4 — Bound the difference:**

For all $|h| < \delta$:

$$
f(x^* + h) - f(x^*) = \frac{1}{2} f''(\xi) h^2 \ge \frac{f''(x^*)}{4} h^2 > 0
$$

(Strict inequality holds because $h^2 > 0$ when $h \neq 0$.)

**Step 5 — Conclude:**

$$
f(x^* + h) > f(x^*) \quad \forall \, 0 < \vert h \vert < \delta
$$

By definition, $x^*$ is a **strict local minimum**. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The crucial insight is that $f'(x^*) = 0$ *eliminates the linear term* in Taylor's expansion, leaving only the **second-order** term $\frac{1}{2} f''(\xi) h^2$ which is always **non-negative** (and is sign-definite when $f''$ does not change sign near $x^*$).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Pattern Problem

**Problem:** Find all local extrema of $f(x) = x^4 - 4x^3 + 4x^2 + 2$ and classify them.

**Step 1 — First derivative:**

$$
f'(x) = 4x^3 - 12x^2 + 8x = 4x(x^2 - 3x + 2) = 4x(x-1)(x-2)
$$

**Step 2 — Set $f'(x) = 0$ to find stationary points:**

$$
4x(x-1)(x-2) = 0 \implies x_1 = 0, \quad x_2 = 1, \quad x_3 = 2
$$

**Step 3 — Second derivative:**

$$
f''(x) = 12x^2 - 24x + 8
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $f''$ at each stationary point:**

$$
f''(0) = 8 > 0 \implies x = 0 \text{ is a local minimum}
$$

$$
f''(1) = 12 - 24 + 8 = -4 < 0 \implies x = 1 \text{ is a local maximum}
$$

$$
f''(2) = 48 - 48 + 8 = 8 > 0 \implies x = 2 \text{ is a local minimum}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the function values:**

$$
f(0) = 0 - 0 + 0 + 2 = 2
$$

$$
f(1) = 1 - 4 + 4 + 2 = 3
$$

$$
f(2) = 16 - 32 + 16 + 2 = 2
$$

**Step 6 — Summary table:**

| Stationary Point $x^*$ | $f'(x^*)$ | $f''(x^*)$ | $f(x^*)$ | Classification |
|------------------------|------------|------------|----------|----------------|
| $0$ | $0$ | $+8$ | $2$ | Local **Minimum** |
| $1$ | $0$ | $-4$ | $3$ | Local **Maximum** |
| $2$ | $0$ | $+8$ | $2$ | Local **Minimum** |

**Step 7 — Global consideration:**

The function $f(x) = x^4 - 4x^3 + 4x^2 + 2$ is a **quartic with positive leading coefficient**, so $f(x) \to +\infty$ as $x \to \pm\infty$. Hence the two local minima are **equal global minima** with $f^* = 2$ attained at $x^* = 0$ and $x^* = 2$.

> [!NOTE]
> This problem is a **classic KTU 14-mark question** because it tests the entire chain: derivative → stationary points → second-derivative test → classification → global comparison. Each sub-step carries roughly 2-3 marks.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — The Inconclusive Second-Derivative Test

**Problem:** Find and classify the stationary points of $f(x) = x^4$.

**Step 1 — Derivatives:**

$$
f'(x) = 4x^3, \qquad f''(x) = 12x^2
$$

**Step 2 — Stationary point:**

$$
f'(x) = 0 \implies x = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Second derivative at the stationary point:**

$$
f''(0) = 0 \implies \text{test is INCONCLUSIVE}
$$

**Step 4 — Third derivative (still zero):**

$$
f'''(x) = 24x \implies f'''(0) = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Fourth derivative (non-zero):**

$$
f^{(4)}(x) = 24 \implies f^{(4)}(0) = 24 > 0
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the higher-order test:**

The first non-zero derivative is at order $n = 4$, which is **even**, and the value is **positive**. Therefore:

$$
x = 0 \text{ is a strict LOCAL MINIMUM (and the global minimum of } x^4\text{)}
$$

This is consistent with the obvious fact that $x^4 \ge 0$ with equality only at $x = 0$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Engineering Application (Beam Design)

**Problem:** A cantilever beam of length $L$ fixed at one end carries a uniformly distributed load $w$ per unit length. The deflection at the free end is given by:

$$
\delta(x) = \frac{w x^2 (6L^2 - 4Lx + x^2)}{24 EI}
$$

Find the value of $x \in [0, L]$ at which the deflection is **maximum**, where $x$ is the distance from the fixed end.

**Step 1 — Simplify (drop the positive constants $\frac{w}{24EI}$ for analysis):**

$$
g(x) = x^2 (6L^2 - 4Lx + x^2) = 6L^2 x^2 - 4L x^3 + x^4
$$

**Step 2 — First derivative:**

$$
g'(x) = 12L^2 x - 12L x^2 + 4x^3 = 4x(3L^2 - 3Lx + x^2)
$$

**Step 3 — Stationary points in $[0, L]$:**

$$
4x(3L^2 - 3Lx + x^2) = 0 \implies x = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad x^2 - 3Lx + 3L^2 = 0
$$

The quadratic $x^2 - 3Lx + 3L^2 = 0$ has discriminant $9L^2 - 12L^2 = -3L^2 < 0$, so it has **no real roots**. Thus, $x = 0$ is the only interior stationary point.

**Step 4 — Check the boundary $x = L$:**

Since $g(0) = 0$ and $g(L) = L^2(6L^2 - 4L^2 + L^2) = 3L^4 > 0$, and no other critical points exist in $(0, L)$, the function is **monotonically increasing** on $[0, L]$.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

The maximum deflection occurs at the **free end** $x = L$:

$$
\delta_{\max} = \frac{wL^4}{8EI}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** KTU students often forget to check the **endpoints** of the domain. A function can have its maximum at $x = L$ even if $f'(L) \neq 0$. Always compare $f$ at all critical points **and** at the boundary.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Procedure

The following step-by-step decision matrix tells you **exactly** which test to apply:

$$
\text{STEP 1: } f'(x^*) = 0? \xrightarrow{\text{NO}} \text{Not an interior extremum; check boundary.}
$$

$$
\text{STEP 2: } f''(x^*) \neq 0? \xrightarrow{\text{YES}} \begin{cases} f''(x^*) > 0 \Rightarrow \text{strict local MIN} \\ f''(x^*) < 0 \Rightarrow \text{strict local MAX} \end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{STEP 3: } f''(x^*) = 0? \xrightarrow{\text{YES}} \text{Use first-derivative test OR higher-order test.}
$$

$$
\text{STEP 4: Higher-order: } f^{(1..n-1)}(x^*) = 0, \, f^{(n)}(x^*) \neq 0 \Rightarrow \begin{cases} n \text{ even, } f^{(n)} > 0 \Rightarrow \text{MIN} \\ n \text{ even, } f^{(n)} < 0 \Rightarrow \text{MAX} \\ n \text{ odd} \Rightarrow \text{Inflection} \end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{STEP 5: Convexity: } f''(x) \ge 0 \, \forall x \in I \Rightarrow \text{Any local min is GLOBAL on } I
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: Complete Procedure for Classifying Extrema

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given f of x] --> B[Compute first derivative f prime of x]
    B --> C[Set f prime of x equal to 0]
    C --> D{Real solutions x star exist?}
    D -- NO --> E[NO interior critical points]
    E --> F[Check boundary endpoints only]
    F --> G[END]
    D -- YES --> H[List all candidate stationary points x star]
    H --> I[Compute second derivative f double prime of x]
    I --> J{Evaluate f double prime at x star}
    J -- POSITIVE --> K[x star is STRICT LOCAL MINIMUM]
    J -- NEGATIVE --> L[x star is STRICT LOCAL MAXIMUM]
    J -- ZERO --> M[Second test INCONCLUSIVE]
    M --> N{Is f differentiable with f prime change of sign?}
    N -- YES --> O[Use FIRST DERIVATIVE TEST]
    N -- NO --> P[Use HIGHER ORDER derivative test]
    P --> Q{What is the order n of first nonzero derivative}
    Q -- n is EVEN --> R{nth derivative positive gives MIN, negative gives MAX}
    Q -- n is ODD --> S[x star is INFLECTION point, not an extremum]
    O --> T[Determine classification from sign change]
    K --> U[Compare all local extrema values]
    L --> U
    R --> U
    S --> V[x star is NOT an extremum]
    T --> U
    U --> W{Is f strictly convex on interval}
    W -- YES --> X[UNIQUE global extremum at local extremum]
    W -- NO --> Y[Multiple global candidates possible]
    X --> Z[FINAL ANSWER]
    Y --> Z
    V --> Z
    G --> Z
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Mapping Function Properties to Optimum Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1[Function f of x]
        A2[Differentiable on open interval I]
    end

    subgraph FIRST["FIRST ORDER ANALYSIS"]
        B1[Compute f prime of x]
        B2[Set f prime equal to 0]
        B3[Stationary points: x star]
    end

    subgraph SECOND["SECOND ORDER ANALYSIS"]
        C1[Compute f double prime of x]
        C2[Evaluate at x star]
        C3{Test result}
    end

    subgraph THIRD["HIGHER ORDER ANALYSIS"]
        D1[Compute successive derivatives]
        D2[Find first nonzero derivative]
        D3{Order parity check}
    end

    subgraph CONVEX["CONVEXITY ANALYSIS"]
        E1[Check sign of f double prime on I]
        E2{f double prime strictly greater than 0 on I}
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OPTIMUM CLASSIFICATION"]
        F1[Local Minimum]
        F2[Local Maximum]
        F3[Inflection Point]
        F4[Global Minimum]
        F5[Global Maximum]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 -- POS --> F1
    C3 -- NEG --> F2
    C3 -- ZERO --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 -- EVEN POS --> F1
    D3 -- EVEN NEG --> F2
    D3 -- ODD --> F3
    E1 --> E2
    E2 -- YES --> F4
    E2 -- NO --> F5
    F1 --> F4
    F2 --> F5
```

## 4.3 Concept Map: Relationship Between Critical Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Fermat Theorem: f prime of x star equals 0] --> P2[Stationary or Critical Point]
    P2 --> P3{Second derivative test}
    P3 -- f double prime greater than 0 --> P4[Local Minimum]
    P3 -- f double prime less than 0 --> P5[Local Maximum]
    P3 -- f double prime equals 0 --> P6[Higher order or first derivative test]

    P6 -- f prime changes sign --> P7[Classification via first derivative test]
    P6 -- First nonzero derivative at order n --> P8{n is even and positive: MIN}
    P8 --> P4
    P6 -- n is odd --> P9[Inflection or saddle]

    P4 --> P10{Strict convexity on I}
    P10 -- YES --> P11[Local MIN equals GLOBAL MIN]
    P10 -- NO --> P12[Only LOCAL, not global]

    Q1[Boundary points] --> Q2[Compare f at all critical AND boundary points]
    Q2 --> Q3[GLOBAL min or max on closed interval]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first flowchart is the **operational algorithm** KTU examiners expect you to follow in your solution scripts. The second diagram isolates the **three independent layers of analysis** (first-order, second-order, convexity). The third diagram shows **how local results can be promoted to global results** through convexity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 3 marks]**

**State Fermat's Theorem on necessary condition for a local extremum of a differentiable single variable function. Is the converse true? Justify with a counter-example.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Fermat's Theorem:** If $f(x)$ is differentiable at an interior point $x^*$ and $x^*$ is a local minimum or maximum of $f$, then $f'(x^*) = 0$.
>
> **Converse is FALSE.** The condition $f'(x^*) = 0$ is necessary but not sufficient.
>
> **Counter-example:** Consider $f(x) = x^3$ at $x^* = 0$.
> - $f'(x) = 3x^2 \implies f'(0) = 0$ (stationary).
> - $f''(0) = 0$ — second test inconclusive.
> - For $x < 0$, $f(x) < 0$; for $x > 0$, $f(x) > 0$. The function is **strictly increasing** through the origin, hence $x = 0$ is **NOT** a local extremum.
>
> Therefore, $f'(x^*) = 0$ does not imply that $x^*$ is an extremum. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 3 marks]**

**Distinguish between a local minimum and a global minimum of a single variable function. Under what additional condition on $f(x)$ does every local minimum automatically become a global minimum?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Local Minimum:** $x^*$ is a local minimum if $\exists \, \delta > 0$ such that $f(x^*) \le f(x)$ for all $x \in (x^* - \delta, x^* + \delta)$.
>
> **Global Minimum:** $x^*$ is a global minimum if $f(x^*) \le f(x)$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$ (or for all $x$ in the domain of interest).
>
> **Key Distinction:** A global minimum is also a local minimum, but a local minimum need not be global — there may be a deeper valley elsewhere.
>
> **Additional Condition:** If $f(x)$ is **strictly convex** on the entire interval of interest (i.e., $f''(x) > 0$ for all $x$ in the interval), then any local minimum is the **unique** global minimum. This is the **global optimality theorem for strictly convex functions**. $\blacksquare$

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Pattern, Module 1, 14 marks]**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
Find all local maxima and minima of $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1$. Also determine the global maximum and global minimum on the closed interval $[0, 5]$.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
Apply the higher-order derivative test to classify the stationary point of $f(x) = x^5$ at $x = 0$. Justify each step.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1 — First derivative:**

$$
f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 3(x^2 - 4x + 3) = 3(x-1)(x-3)
$$

**Step 2 — Stationary points:**

$$
f'(x) = 0 \implies x = 1 \text{ and } x = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Second derivative:**

$$
f''(x) = 6x - 12
$$

**Step 4 — Second-derivative test:**

$$
f''(1) = 6 - 12 = -6 < 0 \implies x = 1 \text{ is a LOCAL MAXIMUM}
$$

$$
f''(3) = 18 - 12 = 6 > 0 \implies x = 3 \text{ is a LOCAL MINIMUM}
$$

**Step 5 — Function values:**

$$
f(0) = 0 - 0 + 0 + 1 = 1
$$

$$
f(1) = 1 - 6 + 9 + 1 = 5
$$

$$
f(3) = 27 - 54 + 27 + 1 = 1
$$

$$
f(5) = 125 - 150 + 45 + 1 = 21
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison on $[0, 5]$:**

| Point $x$ | $f(x)$ | Type |
|-----------|--------|------|
| $0$ (boundary) | $1$ | Candidate |
| $1$ (stationary) | $5$ | Local Max |
| $3$ (stationary) | $1$ | Local Min |
| $5$ (boundary) | $21$ | Candidate |

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**

- **Global Maximum on $[0,5]$:** $f(5) = 21$ at $x^* = 5$ (boundary point).
- **Global Minimum on $[0,5]$:** $f(3) = f(0) = 1$ at $x^* = 0$ (boundary) and $x^* = 3$ (local min).

> [!NOTE]
> The **global maximum here is a boundary point**, not an interior stationary point. KTU examiners reward students who do not forget to check endpoints of closed intervals.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1 — First derivative of $f(x) = x^5$:**

$$
f'(x) = 5x^4
$$

**Step 2 — Stationary point:**

$$
f'(x) = 0 \implies x = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Second derivative:**

$$
f''(x) = 20x^3 \implies f''(0) = 0 \quad \text{(second test inconclusive)}
$$

**Step 4 — Third derivative:**

$$
f'''(x) = 60x^2 \implies f'''(0) = 0 \quad \text{(still zero)}
$$

**Step 5 — Fourth derivative:**

$$
f^{(4)}(x) = 120x \implies f^{(4)}(0) = 0 \quad \text{(still zero)}
$$

**Step 6 — Fifth derivative:**

$$
f^{(5)}(x) = 120 \implies f^{(5)}(0) = 120 \neq 0
$$

**Step 7 — Apply the higher-order test:**

The first non-zero derivative at $x = 0$ is at order $n = 5$ (which is **odd**). According to the higher-order test, an **odd** order first non-zero derivative corresponds to a **point of inflection**, not an extremum.

**Step 8 — Verification by sign analysis:**

For $x < 0$: $f(x) = x^5 < 0$.
For $x > 0$: $f(x) = x^5 > 0$.

The function strictly increases through the origin with no local max or min. Hence, $x = 0$ is a **stationary inflection point**. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Pattern, Module 1, 14 marks]**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
A rectangular box with a square base and an open top is to be constructed using $S$ square meters of material. Find the dimensions that give the **maximum volume**.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
Use the **first-derivative (sign-change) test** to find and classify all extrema of:

$$
f(x) = \vert x - 1 \vert + \vert x - 3 \vert
$$

on the real line. Explain why the second-derivative test cannot be applied here.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1 — Variables and constraints:**

Let the side of the square base be $x$ meters and the height be $h$ meters. Volume:

$$
V = x^2 h
$$

The surface area constraint (open top, square base):

$$
S = \underbrace{x^2}_{\text{base}} + \underbrace{4 x h}_{\text{four sides}}
$$

**Step 2 — Eliminate $h$ using the constraint:**

$$
4xh = S - x^2 \implies h = \frac{S - x^2}{4x}
$$

**Step 3 — Express volume as a single-variable function:**

$$
V(x) = x^2 \cdot \frac{S - x^2}{4x} = \frac{x(S - x^2)}{4} = \frac{Sx - x^3}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — First derivative:**

$$
V'(x) = \frac{S - 3x^2}{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Set $V'(x) = 0$:**

$$
S - 3x^2 = 0 \implies x^2 = \frac{S}{3} \implies x = \sqrt{\frac{S}{3}}
$$

(Only the positive root is geometrically meaningful.)

**Step 6 — Second derivative test:**

$$
V''(x) = \frac{-6x}{4} = -\frac{3x}{2}
$$

At $x = \sqrt{S/3}$:

$$
V''\left(\sqrt{S/3}\right) = -\frac{3}{2}\sqrt{\frac{S}{3}} < 0
$$

Hence $x = \sqrt{S/3}$ gives a **local maximum** of $V$.

**Step 7 — Compute $h$:**

$$
h = \frac{S - S/3}{4 \sqrt{S/3}} = \frac{2S/3}{4\sqrt{S/3}} = \frac{S}{6\sqrt{S/3}} = \frac{1}{6}\sqrt{\frac{S^3}{S}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot 3 = \frac{\sqrt{S}}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{S}{3}}
$$

So $h = \frac{x}{2}$.

**Step 8 — Conclusion:**

- Base side: $x^* = \sqrt{S/3}$ meters
- Height: $h^* = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{S/3}$ meters
- Maximum volume: $V_{\max} = \frac{S}{6}\sqrt{\frac{S}{3}}$ cubic meters

> [!NOTE]
> Note the elegant result: the height of the optimal box is **half the base side**, regardless of $S$. This kind of dimension-free design insight is a hallmark of optimization problems in KTU examinations.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Understand the function:**

$$
f(x) = \vert x - 1 \vert + \vert x - 3 \vert
$$

This is the **sum of distances** from $x$ to the points $1$ and $3$ on the real line.

**Step 2 — Why the second-derivative test fails:**

The function involves absolute values, so it is **not differentiable at $x = 1$ and $x = 3$** (the corners). Hence $f''(x)$ does not exist at these candidate points, and the second-derivative test cannot be applied.

**Step 3 — Piecewise definition (removing the absolute values):**

$$
f(x) = \begin{cases} (1 - x) + (3 - x) = 4 - 2x, & x \le 1 \\ (x - 1) + (3 - x) = 2, & 1 \le x \le 3 \\ (x - 1) + (x - 3) = 2x - 4, & x \ge 3 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $f'(x)$ on each region:**

$$
f'(x) = \begin{cases} -2, & x < 1 \\ \text{undefined at } x = 1 \\ 0, & 1 < x < 3 \\ \text{undefined at } x = 3 \\ +2, & x > 3 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — First-derivative (sign-change) test:**

- **At $x = 1$:** $f'$ goes from $-2$ to $0$ (changes from negative to positive). $\Rightarrow$ **Local Minimum**.
- **At $x = 3$:** $f'$ goes from $0$ to $+2$ (no sign change at the boundary of the analysis). The function is **constant** on $[1, 3]$ at value $2$.

**Step 6 — Verify using the formula $f(1) = 2$ and $f(3) = 2$:**

The function achieves the **minimum value $f^* = 2$** at **every** $x \in [1, 3]$ — an entire **interval** of minima, not a single point.

**Step 7 — Global behaviour:**

- As $x \to -\infty$, $f(x) \to +\infty$ (linear slope $-2$).
- As $x \to +\infty$, $f(x) \to +\infty$ (linear slope $+2$).
- The minimum on $\mathbb{R}$ is $f^* = 2$ attained on the closed interval $[1, 3]$.

**Step 8 — Conclusion:**

The function has a **global minimum of $2$** on the entire interval $[1, 3]$. There are **no local or global maxima** (the function is unbounded above). This is geometrically intuitive: $x$ is closest to the "midpoint" of the cluster $\{1, 3\}$ when $x$ is anywhere between them. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Module 1 Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify the second-derivative sign** at the stationary point. Many students correctly set $f'(x) = 0$ but stop there without using the second-derivative test for classification. This typically costs **2-3 marks** out of 7.
>
> 2. **Not checking boundary points** of a closed interval $[a, b]$. The global extremum on a closed interval is *always* the maximum of $f$ at the critical points and the endpoints. Missing this costs **2-4 marks** depending on the question.
>
> 3. **Treating $f'(x^*) = 0$ as sufficient** to declare $x^*$ an extremum. KTU examiners explicitly test this misconception with functions like $x^3$ or $x^5$ where the second derivative vanishes. Always state: "Since $f''(x^*) = 0$, the second test is inconclusive; using the higher-order test..."
>
> 4. **Computing $f'(x)$ but not simplifying** before solving $f'(x) = 0$. This leads to algebraic errors in finding stationary points. Always factor completely.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to specify units** in engineering applications. KTU valuation keys often deduct 0.5–1 mark for "dimensionless" answers in design problems.
>
> 6. **Confusing necessary and sufficient conditions** in definition questions. Memorize: Fermat = necessary; second-derivative test = sufficient. Mixing these up in Part A (3-mark) questions is a frequent cause of zero marks.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Properties of Single Variable Functions and Optimality Criteria**

- **Definition of optimum:** A point $x^*$ where $f(x^*) \le f(x)$ (min) or $f(x^*) \ge f(x)$ (max) for all $x$ in a specified domain.
- **Local vs. Global:** Local = best in a neighborhood; Global = best on the entire domain. Global ⇒ Local, but Local ⇏ Global.
- **Fermat's Necessary Condition:** $f'(x^*) = 0$ at any interior local extremum of a differentiable function. Converse is FALSE.
- **Second-Order Sufficient Test:** $f'(x^*) = 0$ AND $f''(x^*) > 0$ ⇒ local **minimum**; $f''(x^*) < 0$ ⇒ local **maximum**.
- **Inconclusive Case:** If $f''(x^*) = 0$, use the **first-derivative (sign-change) test** or the **higher-order derivative test**.
- **Higher-Order Test:** First non-zero derivative at order $n$. If $n$ is **even**, $x^*$ is a min (positive) or max (negative). If $n$ is **odd**, $x^*$ is an **inflection point**.
- **First-Derivative Test:** $f'$ changing from $-$ to $+$ ⇒ min; $+$ to $-$ ⇒ max. Works for non-differentiable functions like $|x|$.
- **Convexity Theorem:** If $f''(x) > 0$ for all $x \in I$, then $f$ is **strictly convex** and has a **unique** local minimum, which is the **global** minimum.
- **Closed Interval $[a, b]$ Procedure:** Find all critical points in $(a, b)$, evaluate $f$ at each critical point AND at the endpoints $a, b$. The global min is the smallest; the global max is the largest.
- **Boundary Points:** Can be global extrema even if not stationary (e.g., monotonic functions on a closed interval).
- **Inflection Points:** Have $f'(x) = 0$ and $f''(x) = 0$ (or undefined) but are **not** extrema — the function changes from increasing to increasing (or decreasing to decreasing) but with curvature change.
- **Engineering Connection:** Optimality criteria underlie minimum-weight design, minimum-cost sizing, maximum-efficiency selection, and profit/loss analysis in KTU-designated engineering problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Always present your solution in the standard 4-step format — (1) Compute $f'(x)$, (2) Find stationary points, (3) Apply second-derivative OR first-derivative test, (4) State classification clearly. Examiners award step-marks; missing any one step costs at least 2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Region Elimination Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET638 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Region Elimination Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Region Elimination Methods** (also called *Sequential Search Methods* or *Elimination Methods*) are a class of single-variable, derivative-free optimization algorithms used to locate the optimum of a **unimodal function** $f(x)$ within a known bounded interval $[a, b]$. These methods systematically reduce (eliminate) a portion of the search interval at every iteration by strategically placing two interior test points and comparing their function values, until the interval of uncertainty is reduced to a pre-specified tolerance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Region Elimination Methods are classified under *Single-Variable Non-Linear Programming* and form the foundational approach for problems where derivatives are either unavailable, unreliable, or computationally expensive to evaluate.

### 1.2 The Unimodal Function Assumption

A function $f(x)$ is said to be **unimodal** on the interval $[a, b]$ if there exists a unique point $x^*$ such that:
- $f(x)$ is strictly monotonically decreasing on $[a, x^*]$
- $f(x)$ is strictly monotonically increasing on $[x^*, b]$

The point $x^*$ is the global minimum (or maximum) of $f(x)$ on $[a, b]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Unimodality Matters:** Region elimination methods do not search blindly — they exploit the unimodal property to mathematically *prove* that the optimum cannot lie in the eliminated sub-interval. This is the source of their guaranteed convergence.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hot or Cold" Game

Imagine you are blindfolded and told a hidden coin is somewhere along a 100-meter road. You are allowed to touch only two points on the road, and after each touch, you are told "hotter" or "colder" relative to your previous touch. This is exactly how region elimination works:

| Game Element | Optimization Counterpart |
|---|---|
| The road segment | Search interval $[a, b]$ |
| Two touch-points | Two interior test points $x_1, x_2$ |
| "Hotter / Colder" feedback | Function values $f(x_1)$ vs $f(x_2)$ |
| Eliminating a road portion | Discarding sub-interval that cannot contain the optimum |
| Coin location | Optimum $x^*$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Unlike calculus-based methods (Newton, steepest descent) that use slope/curvature information, region elimination methods rely purely on **value comparison** — making them robust for noisy, discontinuous, or experimental data.

### 1.4 Classification of Region Elimination Methods

Under the KTU 2024 scheme, the principal methods studied are:

1. **Fibonacci Search Method** — the most efficient in terms of the *minimum number of function evaluations*.
2. **Golden Section Search Method** — the practical, fixed-ratio version of Fibonacci search.
3. **Quadratic Interpolation Method** — uses three points to fit a parabola and obtain a direct estimate of the optimum.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fundamental Elimination Principle

Let $f(x)$ be unimodal on $[a, b]$. Suppose we choose two interior points $x_1$ and $x_2$ such that $a < x_1 < x_2 < b$. Then the **elimination rule** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } f(x_1) < f(x_2) &: \quad \text{Eliminate } [x_2, b], \text{ keep } [a, x_2] \text{ (min must lie left of } x_2) \\
\text{If } f(x_1) > f(x_2) &: \quad \text{Eliminate } [a, x_1], \text{ keep } [x_1, b] \text{ (min must lie right of } x_1) \\
\text{If } f(x_1) = f(x_2) &: \quad \text{Eliminate } [a, x_1] \cup [x_2, b], \text{ keep } [x_1, x_2]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This rule is the *engine* of all three methods. The methods differ only in **how** they choose the points $x_1$ and $x_2$.

### 2.2 Why Symmetry is Critical

To make each iteration *fair* and to maximize information, the two test points should be **symmetrically placed** within the current interval. Let the current interval be $[a, b]$ of length $L = b - a$. Then:

$$
x_1 = a + \alpha \cdot L \qquad x_2 = a + (1 - \alpha) \cdot L
$$

where $\alpha$ is a fixed ratio (different for each method). The key insight is that after eliminating one side, **one of the previously computed points must lie inside the new interval**, so only **one new function evaluation** is needed per iteration. This property is called the **memory property** of the search.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Method | Reduction Ratio per Iteration | Test Point Formula | Final Reduction Factor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fibonacci Search** | $1/F_n$ (variable) | $x_1 = a + (F_{n-2}/F_n)(b-a)$ | $1/F_n$ |
| 2 | **Golden Section Search** | $0.618$ (constant) | $x_1 = a + 0.382(b-a)$ | $(0.618)^{N-1}$ |
| 3 | **Quadratic Interpolation** | Variable (model-based) | $x_3 = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{(b^2-c^2)f(a) + (c^2-a^2)f(b) + (a^2-b^2)f(c)}{(b-c)f(a) + (c-a)f(b) + (a-b)f(c)}$ | Direct estimate |

**Auxiliary Constants and Identities:**

| Symbol | Definition | Value / Identity |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau$ (Golden Ratio complement) | $\tau = \frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}$ | $\tau \approx 0.6180339...$ |
| $1 - \tau$ | Reciprocal golden ratio | $1 - \tau = 0.3819660...$ |
| Self-similarity equation | $\tau^2 + \tau - 1 = 0$ | $\tau^2 = 1 - \tau$ |
| Fibonacci recursion | $F_n = F_{n-1} + F_{n-2}$ | $F_0 = 1, F_1 = 1$ (engineering convention) |
| $F_{n+1}/F_n$ limit | $\lim_{n \to \infty} F_{n+1}/F_n$ | $\tau$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In KTU answer sheets, many students write $F_0 = 0$. The **engineering convention** is $F_0 = 1, F_1 = 1$ (so $F_2 = 2, F_3 = 3, F_4 = 5, F_5 = 8, \dots$). Using this convention is critical for Fibonacci search — examiners will deduct marks if you are inconsistent.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Region elimination methods are extensively used in:

- **Mechanical Engineering:** Tuning the parameters of vibration absorbers, where the response function is obtained experimentally.
- **Process Control:** Finding the optimal setpoint for a chemical reactor whose yield vs. temperature curve is determined empirically.
- **Civil Engineering:** Calibrating soil parameters from laboratory stress-strain data.
- **Electronics:** Tuning antenna lengths, filter cut-off frequencies via bench measurement.
- **Machine Learning Hyperparameter Tuning:** One-dimensional line search in stochastic gradient methods.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Golden Section Ratio ($\tau$)

We seek a ratio $\tau \in (0, 1)$ such that after eliminating the larger sub-interval, the **remaining interval is geometrically similar** to the original (same proportional layout of test points). This is the principle of *self-similarity*.

**Step 1:** Set up the geometry. Let original interval length be $L = 1$ (normalized). Place two points symmetrically such that the shorter segment (to be eliminated) has length $\tau$.

**Step 2:** Write the self-similarity condition. After elimination, the remaining interval has length $1 - \tau$. The test point preserved inside it must still divide it in the same ratio $\tau$. Hence:

$$
\frac{1 - \tau}{1} = \frac{\tau}{1}
$$

**Step 3:** Cross-multiply and form the quadratic:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - \tau &= \tau \\
\tau^2 + \tau - 1 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the quadratic formula, retaining the positive root in $(0, 1)$:

$$
\tau = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4}}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{5} - 1}{2} \approx 0.6180339
$$

This is the **golden section ratio**, and $1 - \tau = \tau^2 \approx 0.3819660$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** This derivation is a frequently asked 7-mark question. You must show the geometry → equation → solution clearly.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Test-Point Formulas (Golden Section)

Given the current interval $[a, b]$ with length $L = b - a$, we place the two points as:

$$
x_1 = a + (1 - \tau)(b - a) = a + 0.382 \cdot (b - a)
$$
$$
x_2 = a + \tau(b - a) = a + 0.618 \cdot (b - a)
$$

**Verification of symmetry:** The distance from $a$ to $x_1$ equals the distance from $x_2$ to $b$, both being $(1 - \tau)(b - a) = 0.382(b - a)$.

**Why only one new evaluation per iteration:** After eliminating, suppose $f(x_1) < f(x_2)$, so the new interval is $[a, x_2]$. The point $x_1$ now sits inside this new interval at the correct position because the new length is $\tau(b - a)$, and $x_1 = a + (1 - \tau)(b - a) = a + (1 - \tau) \cdot \tau \cdot [(b - a)/\tau] = a + (1 - \tau) \cdot (\text{new length})/1$. So we reuse $f(x_1)$ and only compute $f(x_2^{\text{new}})$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Quadratic Interpolation Optimum

Given three points $a < b < c$ with function values $f(a), f(b), f(c)$, fit a parabola:

$$
\phi(x) = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 x + \alpha_2 x^2
$$

**Step 1:** Set up the three equations:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_0 + \alpha_1 a + \alpha_2 a^2 &= f(a) \\
\alpha_0 + \alpha_1 b + \alpha_2 b^2 &= f(b) \\
\alpha_0 + \alpha_1 c + \alpha_2 c^2 &= f(c)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Take the derivative of the parabola and set to zero to find its vertex:

$$
\phi'(x) = \alpha_1 + 2\alpha_2 x = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x_3 = -\frac{\alpha_1}{2\alpha_2}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve the system (subtract first equation from the other two) and substitute. The final result is the closed-form expression:

$$
x_3 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(b^2 - c^2) f(a) + (c^2 - a^2) f(b) + (a^2 - b^2) f(c)}{(b - c) f(a) + (c - a) f(b) + (a - b) f(c)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *cubic-free, closed-form* estimate of the optimum. It assumes $f$ is well-approximated by a parabola near the minimum, which is true for most smooth functions.

---

### 3.4 Number of Function Evaluations — Comparison Table

| Method | Function Evaluations for Reduction Factor $\epsilon$ | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Equal Interval Search** | $N = (b - a)/\epsilon$ | Most inefficient — direct subdivision |
| **Fibonacci Search** | $F_n \geq (b - a)/\epsilon$ | Optimal among all region elimination |
| **Golden Section** | $N \geq \log_{1/\tau}[(b - a)/\epsilon] + 1$ | Asymptotically equivalent to Fibonacci |
| **Quadratic Interpolation** | 2 per iteration (after initial 3) | Fast if function is near-quadratic |

**Example (KTU typical):** To reduce an interval of length $L_0 = 10$ to $\epsilon = 0.1$ (reduction factor = 100):
- Equal interval: needs ~100 evaluations.
- Golden section: needs $N \approx 19$ evaluations.
- Fibonacci: needs $F_{11} = 89$ total interval units, so $N = 11$ evaluations (with the pre-planning trick).
- Quadratic: often converges in 4–5 evaluations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Fibonacci requires the user to *pre-specify* the total number of iterations $N$ before starting, which is its only practical drawback. Golden section removes this requirement at the cost of a small (asymptotic) loss of efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of Region Elimination Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start: Define f x and interval a b]) --> initNode[Initialize interval a0 a0, b0 b0]
    initNode --> chooseNode{Select Method}
    chooseNode --> fiboNode[Fibonacci Search]
    chooseNode --> goldNode[Golden Section Search]
    chooseNode --> quadNode[Quadratic Interpolation]

    fiboNode --> fiboLogic["Pre-compute F_n series\nChoose N such that F_n greater than L0/epsilon\nSet k = 1"]
    goldNode --> goldLogic["Set tau = 0.6180339\nCompute x1 = a + 0.382 L\nCompute x2 = a + 0.618 L"]
    quadNode --> quadLogic["Choose 3 points a, b, c\nEvaluate f at each point\nFit parabola and find x3"]

    fiboLogic --> evalLoop1[Evaluate and Compare]
    goldLogic --> evalLoop1
    quadLogic --> evalLoop1

    evalLoop1 --> elimNode{Elimination Rule}
    elimNode -->|f x1 less than f x2| leftElim[Keep left sub-interval]
    elimNode -->|f x1 greater than f x2| rightElim[Keep right sub-interval]
    elimNode -->|f x1 equals f x2| midElim[Keep middle sub-interval]

    leftElim --> memNode[Memory Property: Reuse one old point]
    rightElim --> memNode
    midElim --> memNode

    memNode --> convNode{Convergence Check}
    convNode -->|b - a greater than epsilon| evalLoop1
    convNode -->|b - a less than epsilon| stopNode([Stop: Report x optimal])
```

### 4.2 Golden Section — Single Iteration Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode["Input: a, b, tau = 0.618"] --> x1Node["x1 = a + 0.382 times b minus a"]
    inputNode --> x2Node["x2 = a + 0.618 times b minus a"]
    x1Node --> f1Node["f1 = f x1"]
    x2Node --> f2Node["f2 = f x2"]
    f1Node --> decisionNode{f1 less than f2}
    f2Node --> decisionNode
    decisionNode -->|Yes| caseA["b = x2\nx2 = x1\nf2 = f1\nx1 = a + 0.382 times b minus a"]
    decisionNode -->|No| caseB["a = x1\nx1 = x2\nf1 = f2\nx2 = a + 0.618 times b minus a"]
    caseA --> loopReturn[Return to Convergence Check]
    caseB --> loopReturn
```

### 4.3 Quadratic Interpolation — Fitting and Convergence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1["Step A: Choose 3 distinct points\na, b, c within search interval"] --> p2["Step B: Evaluate f at a, b, c"]
    p2 --> p3["Step C: Check ordering condition\nEither a less than b less than c with f decreasing then increasing"]
    p3 --> p4["Step D: Compute x3 using closed-form parabola vertex formula"]
```
