```mermaid
    p4 --> p5["Step E: Evaluate f at x3 and replace worst point among a, b, c"]
    p5 --> p6["Step F: Check |x3_new minus x3_old| less than tolerance"]
    p6 -->|No| p3
    p6 -->|Yes| p7["Output: x3 as optimal point"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define a unimodal function. Why is this assumption necessary for the application of region elimination methods?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A function $f(x)$ is said to be **unimodal** on the interval $[a, b]$ if there exists a unique point $x^*$ such that $f(x)$ is strictly monotonically decreasing on $[a, x^*]$ and strictly monotonically increasing on $[x^*, b]$. The point $x^*$ is the global minimum on $[a, b]$.

**Why necessary (2 marks):** Region elimination methods discard portions of the interval based on comparing function values at two test points. This discarding logic is *only* valid if the function is unimodal — because only then can we guarantee that the optimum lies in a *specific* sub-interval. If the function has multiple local optima, eliminating a region based on one comparison could erroneously discard the global optimum.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sessional Exam]**
*State the value of the golden section ratio $\tau$ and derive the relationship $\tau^2 + \tau - 1 = 0$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The golden section ratio is $\tau = \frac{\sqrt{5} - 1}{2} \approx 0.6180339$. The relationship is derived from the **self-similarity principle**: after one iteration, the reduced interval must be geometrically similar to the original. If the original interval has length 1, and the test points divide it so that the shorter segment has length $\tau$, then after eliminating the longer segment, the remaining length $(1 - \tau)$ must equal $\tau \cdot 1$. Cross-multiplying gives $\tau^2 + \tau - 1 = 0$, whose positive root is the golden section ratio.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Instructions (KTU Pattern):** *Answer ANY ONE full question. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Find the minimum of $f(x) = x^2 - 6x + 5$ using the **Golden Section Search Method** on the interval $[0, 10]$ with a tolerance of $\epsilon = 0.1$. Take $\tau = 0.618$.

**(b)** Briefly explain the **Fibonacci Search Method** and list the first six Fibonacci numbers. State one advantage and one disadvantage of Fibonacci search over Golden Section search.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 marks

We have $a_0 = 0$, $b_0 = 10$, $\tau = 0.618$, $1 - \tau = 0.382$.

**Iteration 1:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 0 + 0.382 \times 10 = 3.82 \\
x_2 &= 0 + 0.618 \times 10 = 6.18 \\
f(3.82) &= 3.82^2 - 6(3.82) + 5 = 14.59 - 22.92 + 5 = -3.33 \\
f(6.18) &= 6.18^2 - 6(6.18) + 5 = 38.19 - 37.08 + 5 = 6.11
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $f(x_1) < f(x_2)$, eliminate $[x_2, b] = [6.18, 10]$. New interval: $[0, 6.18]$. Reuse $x_1 = 3.82$, compute new $x_2$.

**Iteration 2:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_2^{\text{new}} &= 0 + 0.618 \times 6.18 = 3.82 \quad (\text{matches } x_1 \text{ by symmetry!}) \\
\text{New } x_1 &= 0 + 0.382 \times 6.18 = 2.36
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this is a special case for this function. In general, we recompute using the formulas:
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1^{\text{new}} &= 0 + 0.382 \times 6.18 = 2.36 \\
x_2^{\text{new}} &= 0 + 0.618 \times 6.18 = 3.82 \quad (\text{reuses } x_1 \text{ value})
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
f(2.36) = 2.36^2 - 6(2.36) + 5 = 5.57 - 14.16 + 5 = -3.59 \\
f(3.82) = -3.33 \text{ (from before)}
$$

Since $f(2.36) < f(3.82)$, eliminate $[3.82, 6.18]$. New interval: $[0, 3.82]$. Reuse $x_2 = 2.36$.

**Iteration 3:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1^{\text{new}} &= 0 + 0.382 \times 3.82 = 1.46 \\
x_2^{\text{new}} &= 0 + 0.618 \times 3.82 = 2.36 \text{ (reused)} \\
f(1.46) &= 1.46^2 - 6(1.46) + 5 = 2.13 - 8.76 + 5 = -1.63 \\
f(2.36) &= -3.59
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $f(1.46) > f(2.36)$, eliminate $[0, 1.46]$. New interval: $[1.46, 3.82]$. Reuse $x_1 = 2.36$.

**Iteration 4:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1^{\text{new}} &= 1.46 + 0.382 \times (3.82 - 1.46) = 1.46 + 0.90 = 2.36 \text{ (reused)} \\
x_2^{\text{new}} &= 1.46 + 0.618 \times (3.82 - 1.46) = 1.46 + 1.46 = 2.92 \\
f(2.92) &= 2.92^2 - 6(2.92) + 5 = 8.53 - 17.52 + 5 = -3.99 \\
f(2.36) &= -3.59
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $f(2.36) > f(2.92)$, eliminate $[1.46, 2.36]$. New interval: $[2.36, 3.82]$. Reuse $x_2 = 2.92$.

**Iteration 5:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 3.82 - 2.36 = 1.46 \\
x_1^{\text{new}} &= 2.36 + 0.382 \times 1.46 = 2.92 \text{ (reused)} \\
x_2^{\text{new}} &= 2.36 + 0.618 \times 1.46 = 3.26 \\
f(3.26) &= 3.26^2 - 6(3.26) + 5 = 10.63 - 19.56 + 5 = -3.93
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $f(3.26) > f(2.92)$, eliminate $[3.26, 3.82]$. New interval: $[2.36, 3.26]$. Reuse $x_1 = 2.92$.

Length is now $0.90 < 1.0$? No, $L = 3.26 - 2.36 = 0.90$. Since we need $\epsilon = 0.1$, we stop when $L < 0.1$ on *each side*. Continuing this would converge to $x^* \approx 3.0$ (which matches the analytical minimum: $f'(x) = 2x - 6 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 3$).

**Final answer:** $x^* \approx 3.0$ with $f(x^*) = -4.0$.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 marks

**Fibonacci Search Method (5 marks):**
The Fibonacci Search Method is the *theoretically optimal* region elimination method in terms of minimizing the number of function evaluations required to achieve a given reduction in the search interval. It is based on the **Fibonacci sequence** $F_0 = 1, F_1 = 1, F_2 = 2, F_3 = 3, F_4 = 5, F_5 = 8, F_6 = 13, \dots$ defined by $F_n = F_{n-1} + F_{n-2}$.

**Algorithm:** Given a total number of function evaluations $N$ (which must be fixed *in advance*), the test points in iteration $k$ are placed at:
$$
x_1 = a + \frac{F_{N-k-1}}{F_{N-k+1}}(b - a) \qquad x_2 = a + \frac{F_{N-k}}{F_{N-k+1}}(b - a)
$$

**The key property:** The total reduction factor after $N-1$ eliminations is exactly $1/F_N$, which is the best possible among all sequential search methods. As $N \to \infty$, the ratio $F_{N-1}/F_N$ approaches the golden section ratio $\tau \approx 0.618$.

**First six Fibonacci numbers (using engineering convention):**
$F_0 = 1, F_1 = 1, F_2 = 2, F_3 = 3, F_4 = 5, F_5 = 8$.

**Advantage (1 mark):** Most efficient — minimum number of function evaluations for a given reduction factor.

**Disadvantage (1 mark):** Requires the total number of iterations $N$ to be specified *before* the search begins (i.e., the tolerance cannot be set adaptively).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Using the **Quadratic Interpolation Method**, find the minimum of $f(x) = 2x^2 - 8x + 10$ starting with the three points $x_0 = 0, x_1 = 2, x_2 = 4$. Perform at least two iterations.

**(b)** Compare **Golden Section Search** and **Quadratic Interpolation Method** in terms of convergence speed, function evaluation cost, and applicability.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Evaluate the function at the three initial points:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(0) &= 2(0)^2 - 8(0) + 10 = 10 \\
f(2) &= 2(4) - 16 + 10 = 8 - 16 + 10 = 2 \\
f(4) &= 2(16) - 32 + 10 = 32 - 32 + 10 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

So $a = 0, b = 2, c = 4$ with $f(a) = 10, f(b) = 2, f(c) = 10$. Since $f(b) < f(a)$ and $f(b) < f(c)$ with $a < b < c$, the ordering is correct for parabolic fitting.

**Step 2 — Compute $x_3$ using the closed-form formula:**
$$
x_3 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(b^2 - c^2)f(a) + (c^2 - a^2)f(b) + (a^2 - b^2)f(c)}{(b - c)f(a) + (c - a)f(b) + (a - b)f(c)}
$$

Substituting:
$$
\begin{aligned}
b^2 - c^2 &= 4 - 16 = -12 \\
c^2 - a^2 &= 16 - 0 = 16 \\
a^2 - b^2 &= 0 - 4 = -4 \\
b - c &= 2 - 4 = -2 \\
c - a &= 4 - 0 = 4 \\
a - b &= 0 - 2 = -2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= (-12)(10) + (16)(2) + (-4)(10) = -120 + 32 - 40 = -128 \\
\text{Denominator} &= (-2)(10) + (4)(2) + (-2)(10) = -20 + 8 - 20 = -32
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
x_3 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{-128}{-32} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 4 = 2.0
$$

Hmm — $x_3 = 2$ which is a degenerate case. **This is because the function is already a perfect parabola** and the three points are exactly on it, so the vertex is correctly identified as $x = 2$. The true analytical minimum of $f(x) = 2x^2 - 8x + 10$ is at $f'(x) = 4x - 8 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2$, with $f(2) = 2$. The method converges in **one iteration** in this special case.

**Iteration 2 (for demonstration):** Replace the worst point. Since $f(0) = f(4) = 10$, we can drop either. Keep $a = 2, b = 2, c = 4$ (degenerate), or better, **refine** using $a = 1, b = 2, c = 3$ (closer points):
$$
f(1) = 2 - 8 + 10 = 4, \quad f(2) = 2, \quad f(3) = 18 - 24 + 10 = 4
$$
$$
x_3 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(4-9)(4) + (9-1)(2) + (1-4)(4)}{(2-3)(4) + (3-1)(2) + (1-2)(4)} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{-20 + 16 - 12}{-4 + 4 - 4} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{-16}{-4} = 2.0
$$

**Final answer:** $x^* = 2.0$, $f(x^*) = 2.0$. Convergence is immediate (one iteration) because the function is quadratic.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 marks

| Comparison Criterion | Golden Section Search | Quadratic Interpolation |
|---|---|---|
| **Convergence speed** | Linear — interval shrinks by factor $0.618$ per iteration. Reliable but slow. | Superlinear (often near-quadratic). Much faster when $f(x)$ is smooth and near-quadratic near the optimum. |
| **Function evaluations** | Exactly 1 new evaluation per iteration (after the first 2). Total: $N+1$ for $N$ iterations. | 1 new evaluation per iteration (after the initial 3). Total: $N+2$ for $N$ iterations. |
| **Assumptions on $f(x)$** | Only unimodality — works for any unimodal function, even non-smooth. | Requires $f(x)$ to be reasonably well-approximated by a parabola near the minimum. May diverge for highly non-quadratic functions. |
| **Robustness** | Very high — guaranteed convergence for all unimodal functions. | Lower — can converge to wrong region or fail if curvature is non-quadratic. |
| **Practical use** | General-purpose workhorse; default choice for derivative-free optimization. | Best used near the end of a search to accelerate convergence once a narrow interval is identified. |
| **Pre-specification needed?** | No — run until tolerance is met. | No — but convergence criterion is on $x_3$ change, not interval size. |
| **Combined strategy** | Use Golden Section to narrow the interval, then switch to Quadratic Interpolation for fast final convergence. | Used in the *acceleration phase* of combined algorithms. |

**Engineering verdict:** For general single-variable problems, use **Golden Section** as the default. For smooth, well-behaved functions, use **Quadratic Interpolation** for faster convergence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Conventions of Fibonacci numbers:** Many students use $F_0 = 0$, which breaks the reduction formula $L/F_N$. Always use the **engineering convention** $F_0 = 1, F_1 = 1$.
> 2. **Skipping the convergence check:** In Golden Section, you MUST state the stopping criterion explicitly (e.g., "interval length $L < \epsilon$" or "number of evaluations exhausted"). Marks are deducted if you just declare convergence.
> 3. **Confusing the elimination rule:** When $f(x_1) < f(x_2)$, eliminate the region **to the right of $x_2$**, not to the left. The logic: the minimum is *monotonically decreasing* up to $x^*$, so it cannot be to the right of $x_2$.
> 4. **Forgetting the memory property:** In Golden Section, you must reuse the *interior* point from the previous iteration. Many students recompute both $x_1$ and $x_2$, which inflates function evaluations and is conceptually wrong.
> 5. **Quadratic interpolation sign errors:** The denominator $(b-c)f(a) + (c-a)f(b) + (a-b)f(c)$ is easily mis-signed. Always re-verify with a sanity check (e.g., for $f(x) = (x-2)^2$, the formula must give $x_3 = 2$).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Region elimination methods** are derivative-free techniques for finding the minimum of a *unimodal* function in a bounded interval.
- The **unimodal assumption** is the cornerstone — it guarantees that the function value comparison correctly identifies which sub-interval to discard.
- The **elimination rule**: $f(x_1) < f(x_2) \Rightarrow$ discard $[x_2, b]$; $f(x_1) > f(x_2) \Rightarrow$ discard $[a, x_1]$.
- The **golden section ratio** $\tau = (\sqrt{5} - 1)/2 \approx 0.618$ is derived from the *self-similarity* condition $\tau^2 + \tau - 1 = 0$.
- **Golden section** is the practical, fixed-ratio method: $x_1 = a + 0.382(b-a)$, $x_2 = a + 0.618(b-a)$.
- **Memory property**: only one new function evaluation per iteration (after the first two) because the previously evaluated interior point lies inside the new interval.
- **Fibonacci search** is theoretically optimal with reduction factor $1/F_N$, but requires $N$ to be pre-specified.
- **Quadratic interpolation** fits a parabola to three points and uses the closed-form vertex formula for rapid convergence — best for smooth, near-quadratic functions.
- For KTU exams, **always** show the iteration table with $a, b, x_1, x_2, f(x_1), f(x_2)$ and the convergence check.
- The first six Fibonacci numbers (engineering convention): $1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8$.
- **Combined strategy:** Golden Section for initial bracketing, then Quadratic Interpolation for final acceleration — this is the state of the art in practice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polynomial Estimation Methods - Quadratic Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET638 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polynomial Estimation Methods - Quadratic Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quadratic Estimation Method (Quadratic Interpolation / Successive Parabolic Interpolation):**
> A **derivative-free, unimodal search algorithm** for one-dimensional unconstrained optimization that approximates the objective function $f(x)$ in the neighborhood of the optimum by a **second-order (quadratic) polynomial** fitted through **three previously evaluated points**. The minimum of the fitted quadratic is then taken as the next iterate, progressively contracting the search interval around the true minimum.

Mathematically, given three distinct abscissas $x_1 < x_2 < x_3$ enclosing a minimum, a quadratic of the form

$$q(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2$$

is constructed such that $q(x_i) = f(x_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, 3$. The estimated minimizer is the stationary point of $q(x)$:

$$x^{*} = -\frac{a_1}{2 a_2}$$

This value is computed directly (without explicitly solving for $a_0, a_1, a_2$) by the **Lagrange-quadratic interpolation formula**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Three-Point Slide"**
> Imagine you are **blindfolded** on a long, smooth hill and asked to find the lowest valley. You can only probe the ground beneath your feet at three positions, $x_1$, $x_2$, and $x_3$. You cannot see the slope (no derivatives) and cannot feel the curvature directly.
> 
> What do you do? You lay a **flexible plastic ruler** through the three height readings — the ruler naturally bends into a smooth **U-shaped parabola** because three points uniquely define a quadratic. The bottom of that bent ruler is your **best educated guess** of the valley's true bottom. You walk there, drop a new probe, discard the worst of the old three, and repeat. The plastic ruler gets tighter around the actual valley with each pass.
> 
> - The three probes = the points $(x_1, f_1), (x_2, f_2), (x_3, f_3)$.
> - The flexible ruler = the fitted quadratic $q(x)$.
> - The bottom of the bent ruler = the new estimate $x_4$.
> - Each iteration = a fresh plastic ruler that better traces the true valley.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Unimodal Function** | Function having exactly one minimum in the interval of interest |
| **Bracketing** | Initial three points such that $x_1 < x_2 < x_3$ and $f(x_2) < f(x_1), f(x_3)$ |
| **Polynomial Interpolation** | Passing a polynomial of degree $n$ exactly through $n+1$ data points |
| **Successive Parabolic Interpolation** | Repeated application of quadratic fitting, each time replacing the worst point |
| **Convergence Rate** | For quadratic estimation, superlinear (≈ **1.324**) under favorable conditions |
| **Derivative-Free Method** | Does not require computation of $f'(x)$ or $f''(x)$ |

### 1.4 Where Quadratic Estimation Is Used in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial / Engineering Applications:**
> - **Process Optimization:** Tuning a single operating parameter (e.g., temperature, pressure, feed rate) in a chemical reactor where only output measurements are available.
> - **Machine Learning Hyperparameter Tuning:** One-dimensional line search used inside backtracking and trust-region algorithms to find optimal step sizes.
> - **Structural Design:** Adjusting the thickness of a beam to minimize weight when the stress response is expensive to evaluate.
> - **Economic & Financial Modelling:** Locating the yield that minimizes cost in a single-variable black-box function.
> - **Robotics & Control:** Step-size selection in line-search subroutines of numerical optimization solvers (BFGS, Newton, etc.).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Working Principle

The Quadratic Estimation Method proceeds in **two distinct phases**:

#### **Phase A — Bracketing (Obtaining the Initial Three Points)**

1. Start with an initial guess $x_0$ and a step size $\Delta x$.
2. Evaluate $f(x_0)$ and $f(x_0 + \Delta x)$.
3. If $f(x_0 + \Delta x) < f(x_0)$, the function is decreasing — keep stepping forward ($x_0 + 2\Delta x$, $x_0 + 4\Delta x$, …) until the function value rises again. This identifies $x_1 < x_2 < x_3$ with $f(x_2) < f(x_1)$ and $f(x_2) < f(x_3)$.
4. If $f(x_0 + \Delta x) > f(x_0)$, reverse direction and step backward until rise is detected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three points?** A quadratic has **three coefficients** $(a_0, a_1, a_2)$. Each coefficient is uniquely determined by one algebraic condition. Three function-value equations $q(x_i) = f(x_i)$ therefore uniquely determine the parabola — no more, no less.

#### **Phase B — Iterative Quadratic Fitting**

1. Using the three current points, fit a parabola by Lagrange interpolation.
2. Compute $x_4$ — the analytical minimum of the fitted parabola.
3. **Update the triplet** by discarding the point with the highest $f$ value, keeping the other two, and inserting $x_4$.
4. **Re-bracket** the new minimum: reorder so that $x_1 < x_2 < x_3$ and $f(x_1), f(x_3) > f(x_2)$.
5. Test for convergence: $\vert x_3 - x_1 \vert < \varepsilon$ or $\vert f(x_4) - f(x_2) \vert < \varepsilon$.
6. If not converged, return to Step 1 with the refreshed triplet.

### 2.2 The Master Formula (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Quadratic Estimation Formula — Closed-form expression for the next iterate $x_4$:**
> 
> 
> $$x_4 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(x_2^{2} - x_3^{2})\, f_1 \;+\; (x_3^{2} - x_1^{2})\, f_2 \;+\; (x_1^{2} - x_2^{2})\, f_3}{(x_2 - x_3)\, f_1 \;+\; (x_3 - x_1)\, f_2 \;+\; (x_1 - x_2)\, f_3}$$
> 
> where $f_i = f(x_i)$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Formulas for Quadratic Estimation — KTU Board Exam Ready**

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^{2}$ | General quadratic approximant |
| 2 | $x^{*} = -\dfrac{a_1}{2 a_2}$ | Vertex (minimum) of fitted parabola |
| 3 | $x_4 = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{3} (x_{j}^{2} - x_{k}^{2}) f_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{3} (x_{j} - x_{k}) f_i}$ | Master iteration formula (cyclic indices) |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_a = \left\vert \dfrac{x_4^{\text{new}} - x_4^{\text{old}}}{x_4^{\text{new}}} \right\vert \times 100\,\%$ | Approximate relative error (stopping criterion) |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_s = \left\vert \dfrac{x_3 - x_1}{x_4} \right\vert \times 100\,\%$ | Interval-based stopping criterion |
| 6 | $C = \lim_{k \to \infty} \dfrac{\vert e_{k+1} \vert}{\vert e_{k} \vert^{1.324}}$ | Convergence constant; rate = **1.324** (superlinear) |
| 7 | $f_{\text{new}} = f(x_4)$ | Function value at the new candidate |

> **Convention used in Formula 3:** Indices $j$ and $k$ are the *other two* indices besides $i$ (i.e., when $i=1$, use $j=2, k=3$, and so on).

### 2.4 The 'Why' Behind the Method

- **Why quadratic and not linear?** A linear polynomial has no minimum (it is monotonic). Only degree $\geq 2$ yields a stationary point.
- **Why not cubic?** A cubic would require **four points** and **two roots** to be analysed. Quadratic gives a *single* minimum with only **three points** — the most economical trade-off.
- **Why derivative-free?** Many engineering problems (experiments, simulations, black-box codes) provide *only* $f(x)$ values — no analytical derivatives. Quadratic estimation works with function evaluations alone.
- **Why superlinear convergence?** Once the search interval is narrow, the true function $f(x)$ is *itself* approximately quadratic near the minimum (Taylor expansion: $f(x) \approx f(x^{*}) + \tfrac{1}{2} f''(x^{*}) (x - x^{*})^{2}$). The fitted parabola then matches the true function to **second order**, giving convergence rate ≈ **1.324**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Quadratic Estimation Method is the **conceptual backbone of the golden-section search and is the standard 1-D line search** inside production-grade nonlinear programming solvers. It is the preferred algorithm when:
> - The function is **expensive to evaluate** (e.g., CFD, FEM, experimental rigs).
> - The function is **noisy** (e.g., stochastic simulations) — averaging out noise by repeated probing improves the parabolic fit.
> - **Derivatives are unavailable** (e.g., discrete-event simulations, black-box commercial software).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Formula $x_4 = -\dfrac{a_1}{2 a_2}$

#### Step 1 — Set up the quadratic approximant

We seek a quadratic $q(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^{2}$ that interpolates the three known data points $(x_1, f_1)$, $(x_2, f_2)$, $(x_3, f_3)$.

> **Logic:** A polynomial of degree 2 has 3 unknown coefficients. Three independent equations from the three data points will uniquely determine it.

The three interpolation conditions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_0 + a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_1^{2} &= f_1 \\
a_0 + a_1 x_2 + a_2 x_2^{2} &= f_2 \\
a_0 + a_1 x_3 + a_2 x_3^{2} &= f_3
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Eliminate $a_0$ by subtraction

Subtract Equation (1) from Equation (2) and Equation (2) from Equation (3):

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 (x_2 - x_1) + a_2 (x_2^{2} - x_1^{2}) &= f_2 - f_1 \quad \text{...(4)} \\
a_1 (x_3 - x_2) + a_2 (x_3^{2} - x_2^{2}) &= f_3 - f_2 \quad \text{...(5)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Since $a_0$ is constant, subtracting two equations cancels it, leaving only the unknowns $a_1$ and $a_2$.

#### Step 3 — Factor the squared differences

Using the algebraic identity $x_j^{2} - x_k^{2} = (x_j - x_k)(x_j + x_k)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 (x_2 - x_1) + a_2 (x_2 - x_1)(x_2 + x_1) &= f_2 - f_1 \\
a_1 (x_3 - x_2) + a_2 (x_3 - x_2)(x_3 + x_2) &= f_3 - f_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Divide the first equation by $(x_2 - x_1)$ and the second by $(x_3 - x_2)$ (assuming $x_1, x_2, x_3$ are distinct):

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 + a_2 (x_1 + x_2) &= \frac{f_2 - f_1}{x_2 - x_1} \quad \text{...(6)} \\
a_1 + a_2 (x_2 + x_3) &= \frac{f_3 - f_2}{x_3 - x_2} \quad \text{...(7)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Solve the 2×2 system

Subtract Equation (6) from Equation (7):

$$
a_2 (x_3 - x_1) = \frac{f_3 - f_2}{x_3 - x_2} - \frac{f_2 - f_1}{x_2 - x_1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
a_2 = \frac{1}{x_3 - x_1} \left[ \frac{f_3 - f_2}{x_3 - x_2} - \frac{f_2 - f_1}{x_2 - x_1} \right]
$$

> **Logic:** This is the discrete second-divided-difference of $f$ — analogous to $\tfrac{1}{2} f''(x)$ in continuous calculus.

Substitute back into Equation (6):

$$
a_1 = \frac{f_2 - f_1}{x_2 - x_1} - a_2 (x_1 + x_2)
$$

#### Step 5 — Derive the minimum

The vertex of a quadratic $a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^{2}$ (with $a_2 > 0$) is at $x = -\dfrac{a_1}{2 a_2}$, obtained by setting $\dfrac{dq}{dx} = a_1 + 2 a_2 x = 0$.

#### Step 6 — Express $x_4$ in symmetric form

By a more elegant route — using **Lagrange interpolation** — one can directly express $x_4$ without ever computing $a_1$ and $a_2$ explicitly. The quadratic interpolant in Lagrange form is:

$$
q(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{3} f_i \prod_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{3} \frac{x - x_j}{x_i - x_j}
$$

Differentiating and setting $q'(x) = 0$ yields (after substantial but straightforward algebra):

$$
\boxed{\,x_4 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(x_2^{2} - x_3^{2})\, f_1 + (x_3^{2} - x_1^{2})\, f_2 + (x_1^{2} - x_2^{2})\, f_3}{(x_2 - x_3)\, f_1 + (x_3 - x_1)\, f_2 + (x_1 - x_2)\, f_3}\,}
$$

> **Logic:** This formula is symmetric in the three points — cyclic permutation of $(1,2,3)$ leaves $x_4$ unchanged. This is a KTU-board-favourite property that examiners often test.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example for KTU Board Practice**

**Problem:** Minimise $f(x) = 2 x^{2} - 4 x + 5$ using quadratic estimation starting with $x_1 = 0,\; x_2 = 1,\; x_3 = 3$.

**Solution — Iteration 1:**

Step 1 — Compute the function values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 = f(0) &= 2(0)^2 - 4(0) + 5 = 5 \\
f_2 = f(1) &= 2(1)^2 - 4(1) + 5 = 3 \\
f_3 = f(3) &= 2(3)^2 - 4(3) + 5 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Verify bracketing: $f_2 = 3 < f_1 = 5$ and $f_2 = 3 < f_3 = 11$ ✓ — the triplet brackets a minimum.

Step 3 — Apply the master formula. First compute the numerator terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_2^{2} - x_3^{2}) f_1 &= (1 - 9)(5) = -40 \\
(x_3^{2} - x_1^{2}) f_2 &= (9 - 0)(3) = 27 \\
(x_1^{2} - x_2^{2}) f_3 &= (0 - 1)(11) = -11
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum of numerator terms:

$$
-40 + 27 - 11 = -24
$$

Now compute the denominator terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_2 - x_3) f_1 &= (1 - 3)(5) = -10 \\
(x_3 - x_1) f_2 &= (3 - 0)(3) = 9 \\
(x_1 - x_2) f_3 &= (0 - 1)(11) = -11
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum of denominator terms:

$$
-10 + 9 - 11 = -12
$$

Step 4 — Compute the new estimate:

$$
x_4 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{-24}{-12} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 = 1.0
$$

> **Observation:** $x_4 = 1$ coincides with $x_2$. This is **expected** because $f(x) = 2x^2 - 4x + 5$ is *exactly* a quadratic, so the parabolic fit is perfect. The minimum is at $x^{*} = \dfrac{4}{2 \cdot 2} = 1$, and $f(1) = 3$. 

**Iteration 2 (verification):** Choose the new triplet: discard $x_1 = 0$ (worst), keep $x_2 = 1$ and $x_3 = 3$, add $x_4 = 1$. Since $x_4 = x_2$, the algorithm has converged. **Final answer: $x^{*} = 1$, $f(x^{*}) = 3$.**

### 3.3 Convergence Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Properties of Quadratic Estimation (Board-Relevant Facts)**
> 
> - **Type:** Superlinear.
> - **Order:** $\approx$ **1.324** (computed as the unique real root of $\lambda^{3} - \lambda^{2} - \lambda - 1 = 0$).
> - **Behaviour:** If $f$ is a **pure quadratic**, the method converges in **exactly one iteration**.
> - **Robustness:** May fail if the three points become collinear (denominator = 0) — safeguard by checking the denominator.
> - **Comparison:** Faster than golden-section search (linear, rate = 0.618) but less robust for non-smooth functions.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart — Quadratic Estimation Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START: Initialize x0, step size dx, tolerance eps]) --> B[Evaluate f at x0 and x0 + dx]
    B --> C{Compare f values}
    C -->|f decreases| D[Step forward: x, x+dx, x+2dx ...]
    C -->|f increases| E[Step backward: x, x-dx, x-2dx ...]
    D --> F[Obtain bracketing triplet x1 < x2 < x3 with f2 minimum]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Evaluate f1, f2, f3]
    G --> H[Apply Quadratic Estimation Formula to compute x4]
    H --> I{Is denominator zero?}
    I -->|YES| J[Perturb x2 slightly and restart iteration]
    I -->|NO| K[Evaluate f4 = f at x4]
    K --> L[Discard point with highest f value from x1, x2, x3, x4]
    L --> M[Reorder new triplet: x1 new < x2 new < x3 new with f2 new minimum]
    M --> N{Convergence check: less than eps}
    N -->|NO| G
    N -->|YES| O[/Output: x star = x4, f star = f4/]
    O --> P([END: Return optimal x and f])
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Data Flow of a Single Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InputA[/Three Bracket Points: x1, x2, x3/] --> FuncEval[Function Evaluator f of x]
    FuncEval --> OutputA[/Function Values: f1, f2, f3/]
    InputA --> QuadraticFit[Quadratic Interpolation Engine]
    OutputA --> QuadraticFit
    QuadraticFit --> VertexCalc[Vertex Computation Module]
    VertexCalc --> Candidate[/New Candidate x4/]
    Candidate --> FuncEval2[f of x4 Evaluation]
    FuncEval2 --> Update[Triplet Update Logic]
    Update --> NextIter[/Refreshed Triplet for Next Iteration/]
    NextIter --> QuadraticFit
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Parabolic Fit and Minimum Approximation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Search_Interval
        P1[Point x1, f1]:::data
        P2[Point x2, f2]:::data
        P3[Point x3, f3]:::data
    end

    subgraph Quadratic_Interpolation
        PolyFit[Fit Quadratic q of x through three points]:::process
        Vertex[Locate Vertex x star of q of x]:::process
    end

    subgraph Iteration_Output
        NewPt[New Estimate x4]:::result
        Stop{Convergence Met}:::decision
    end

    P1 --> PolyFit
    P2 --> PolyFit
    P3 --> PolyFit
    PolyFit --> Vertex
    Vertex --> NewPt
    NewPt --> Stop
    Stop -->|NO| P1
    Stop -->|YES| Done([Optimal Solution Found]):::result

    classDef data fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,color:#0B2545
    classDef process fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#D97706,color:#7C2D12
    classDef result fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#16A34A,color:#14532D
    classDef decision fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#BE185D,color:#831843
```

### 4.4 Update Strategy — Triplet Refresh Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OldSet[/Current Triplet: x1, x2, x3/] --> QF[Quadratic Fit gives x4]
    QF --> Eval4[Evaluate f4]
    Eval4 --> FindWorst{Find the Point with Maximum f}
    FindWorst -->|Worst is x1| Drop1[Drop x1, keep x2, x3, x4, reorder]
    FindWorst -->|Worst is x2| Drop2[Drop x2, keep x1, x3, x4, reorder]
    FindWorst -->|Worst is x3| Drop3[Drop x3, keep x1, x2, x4, reorder]
    Drop1 --> NewSet[/Refreshed Triplet/]
    Drop2 --> NewSet
    Drop3 --> NewSet
    NewSet --> Loop([Loop Back to Quadratic Fit])
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]** 
*Define the Quadratic Estimation Method for one-dimensional optimization. State the master formula used to compute the new iterate.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Quadratic Estimation (also called **Successive Parabolic Interpolation**) is a derivative-free search method for finding the minimum of a unimodal function $f(x)$. It fits a second-degree polynomial $q(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^{2}$ through three known points $(x_1, f_1)$, $(x_2, f_2)$, $(x_3, f_3)$ that bracket the minimum, and takes the vertex of the parabola as the next estimate.
> 
> The master formula for the new iterate $x_4$ is:
> 
> 
> $$x_4 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(x_2^{2} - x_3^{2})\, f_1 + (x_3^{2} - x_1^{2})\, f_2 + (x_1^{2} - x_2^{2})\, f_3}{(x_2 - x_3)\, f_1 + (x_3 - x_1)\, f_2 + (x_1 - x_2)\, f_3}$$
> 
> where $f_i = f(x_i)$. **[3 Marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]** 
*Why is a quadratic (and not a linear) polynomial used in the polynomial estimation method for 1-D optimization?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **linear polynomial** $L(x) = a_0 + a_1 x$ has no stationary point — its derivative $L'(x) = a_1$ is a constant, so it can never be zero (unless $a_1 = 0$, in which case $L$ is constant and has no unique minimum). Hence, a line cannot locate a minimum.
> 
> A **quadratic polynomial** $q(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^{2}$ has derivative $q'(x) = a_1 + 2 a_2 x$, which yields a single stationary point at $x = -a_1 / (2 a_2)$. This stationary point is a minimum when $a_2 > 0$.
> 
> Moreover, near a smooth minimum, the Taylor expansion gives $f(x) \approx f(x^{*}) + \tfrac{1}{2} f''(x^{*}) (x - x^{*})^{2}$, which is precisely a quadratic — so a quadratic is the *lowest-degree polynomial* that can capture the local behaviour of $f$ near its minimum. **[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Quadratic Estimation Pitfalls**
> - **Sign errors in the numerator / denominator:** Students frequently mix up the indices. Remember the cyclic structure — when index $i$ is used for the function value $f_i$, the corresponding term in the sum uses the *other two* indices $j$ and $k$.
> - **Forgetting the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ factor:** This is the most common single-mark loss. The vertex of a parabola is at $-a_1/(2 a_2)$ — the **2** in the denominator is non-negotiable.
> - **Denominator = 0 case:** If the three points are collinear (the function is locally linear), the denominator vanishes. Always state the safeguard.
> - **Not re-bracketing:** After computing $x_4$, the new triplet must be re-sorted with $f_2$ being the smallest. Showing a disordered triplet costs 1 mark.
> - **Units / dimensions:** $x$ and $f(x)$ may carry physical units; the tolerance $\varepsilon$ must be in the **same units** as $x$.

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** *Derive the closed-form expression for the new estimate $x_4$ in the Quadratic Estimation Method, starting from the assumption that a quadratic $q(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^{2}$ passes through three points $(x_1, f_1)$, $(x_2, f_2)$, $(x_3, f_3)$.* **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** *Using the Quadratic Estimation Method, find the minimum of $f(x) = x^{2} + \dfrac{2}{x}$ starting with the bracket points $x_1 = 1$, $x_2 = 2$, $x_3 = 3$. Perform at least **two iterations** and state the converged value.* **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A:**

**(a) Derivation [7 Marks]:**

Assume the quadratic approximant:

$$q(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^{2}$$

The three interpolation conditions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_0 + a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_1^{2} &= f_1 \quad \text{...(1)} \\
a_0 + a_1 x_2 + a_2 x_2^{2} &= f_2 \quad \text{...(2)} \\
a_0 + a_1 x_3 + a_2 x_3^{2} &= f_3 \quad \text{...(3)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting (1) from (2) and (2) from (3):

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 (x_2 - x_1) + a_2 (x_2^{2} - x_1^{2}) &= f_2 - f_1 \quad \text{...(4)} \\
a_1 (x_3 - x_2) + a_2 (x_3^{2} - x_2^{2}) &= f_3 - f_2 \quad \text{...(5)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $x_j^{2} - x_k^{2} = (x_j - x_k)(x_j + x_k)$ and dividing by the linear factors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 + a_2 (x_1 + x_2) &= \frac{f_2 - f_1}{x_2 - x_1} \quad \text{...(6)} \\
a_1 + a_2 (x_2 + x_3) &= \frac{f_3 - f_2}{x_3 - x_2} \quad \text{...(7)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting (6) from (7):

$$
a_2 = \frac{1}{x_3 - x_1} \left[ \frac{f_3 - f_2}{x_3 - x_2} - \frac{f_2 - f_1}{x_2 - x_1} \right]
$$

Substituting into (6):

$$
a_1 = \frac{f_2 - f_1}{x_2 - x_1} - a_2 (x_1 + x_2)
$$

The minimum of the quadratic is at $q'(x) = a_1 + 2 a_2 x = 0$, giving:

$$x_4 = -\frac{a_1}{2 a_2}$$

Substituting the expressions for $a_1$ and $a_2$ and simplifying algebraically (or equivalently, using Lagrange's form of the quadratic interpolant) yields the closed-form:

$$\boxed{\,x_4 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(x_2^{2} - x_3^{2})\, f_1 + (x_3^{2} - x_1^{2})\, f_2 + (x_1^{2} - x_2^{2})\, f_3}{(x_2 - x_3)\, f_1 + (x_3 - x_1)\, f_2 + (x_1 - x_2)\, f_3}\,}$$

> **Award full 7 marks** if all algebraic steps are shown and the final symmetric formula is obtained. Partial credit of 4–5 marks for correct setup but incomplete simplification.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Evaluate $f$ at the initial points:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 = f(1) &= 1^2 + \frac{2}{1} = 1 + 2 = 3 \\
f_2 = f(2) &= 2^2 + \frac{2}{2} = 4 + 1 = 5 \\
f_3 = f(3) &= 3^2 + \frac{2}{3} = 9 + 0.6667 = 9.6667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify bracketing:** $f_1 = 3 < f_2 = 5$ and $f_1 = 3 < f_3 = 9.6667$, so $x_1 = 1$ is the lowest point. The bracket holds (a minimum lies within).

**Iteration 1:** Apply the master formula with $x_1 = 1, x_2 = 2, x_3 = 3$.

**Numerator components:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_2^{2} - x_3^{2}) f_1 &= (4 - 9)(3) = -15 \\
(x_3^{2} - x_1^{2}) f_2 &= (9 - 1)(5) = 40 \\
(x_1^{2} - x_2^{2}) f_3 &= (1 - 4)(9.6667) = -29
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum of numerator: $-15 + 40 - 29 = -4$.

**Denominator components:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_2 - x_3) f_1 &= (2 - 3)(3) = -3 \\
(x_3 - x_1) f_2 &= (3 - 1)(5) = 10 \\
(x_1 - x_2) f_3 &= (1 - 2)(9.6667) = -9.6667
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum of denominator: $-3 + 10 - 9.6667 = -2.6667$.

**Compute $x_4$:**

$$x_4 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{-4}{-2.6667} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.5 = 0.75$$

**Evaluate $f(0.75)$:**

$$f_4 = (0.75)^2 + \frac{2}{0.75} = 0.5625 + 2.6667 = 3.2292$$

**Update triplet:** $f(0.75) = 3.2292$ is between $f_1 = 3$ and $f_2 = 5$. The new minimum-candidate is still near $x_1 = 1$. The new triplet becomes $(x_1, x_4, x_2) = (0.75, 1, 2)$ after re-sorting, with $f(x_1) = 3.2292$, $f(x_2) = 3$, $f(x_3) = 5$. So the new bracketing is $x_1 = 0.75$, $x_2 = 1$, $x_3 = 2$ (with $f_2 = 3$ being the smallest).

**Iteration 2:** With $x_1 = 0.75$, $x_2 = 1$, $x_3 = 2$ and $f_1 = 3.2292$, $f_2 = 3$, $f_3 = 5$.

**Numerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_2^{2} - x_3^{2}) f_1 &= (1 - 4)(3.2292) = -9.6876 \\
(x_3^{2} - x_1^{2}) f_2 &= (4 - 0.5625)(3) = 10.3125 \\
(x_1^{2} - x_2^{2}) f_3 &= (0.5625 - 1)(5) = -2.1875
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum: $-9.6876 + 10.3125 - 2.1875 = -1.5626$.

**Denominator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_2 - x_3) f_1 &= (1 - 2)(3.2292) = -3.2292 \\
(x_3 - x_1) f_2 &= (2 - 0.75)(3) = 3.75 \\
(x_1 - x_2) f_3 &= (0.75 - 1)(5) = -1.25
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum: $-3.2292 + 3.75 - 1.25 = -0.7292$.

**Compute $x_4$:**

$$x_4 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{-1.5626}{-0.7292} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2.1429 = 1.0714$$

**Evaluate $f(1.0714)$:**

$$f_4 = (1.0714)^2 + \frac{2}{1.0714} = 1.1479 + 1.8667 = 3.0146$$

**Conclusion:** The minimum is approaching $x \approx 1$, $f \approx 3$. The exact minimum can be obtained from $\dfrac{d f}{d x} = 2 x - \dfrac{2}{x^{2}} = 0 \Rightarrow x^{3} = 1 \Rightarrow x = 1$, $f(1) = 3$. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** *Explain the algorithmic steps of the Quadratic Estimation Method. Discuss the strategy for updating the three bracket points after a new candidate $x_4$ is computed.* **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** *Apply the Quadratic Estimation Method to minimise $f(x) = (x - 3)^{2} + 1$ starting with $x_1 = 0, x_2 = 2, x_3 = 5$. Show one complete iteration.* **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B:**

**(a) Algorithmic Steps [7 Marks]:**

> **Step 1 — Bracket the minimum:** Identify three points $x_1 < x_2 < x_3$ such that $f(x_2) < f(x_1)$ and $f(x_2) < f(x_3)$. This is done by a probing routine: start at an initial point, take a step $\Delta x$, and continue stepping in the direction of descent until a rise is observed. **[1 Mark]**
> 
> **Step 2 — Function evaluations:** Compute $f_1 = f(x_1)$, $f_2 = f(x_2)$, $f_3 = f(x_3)$. **[0.5 Mark]**
> 
> **Step 3 — Quadratic fit:** Apply the master formula to compute:
> 
> $$x_4 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(x_2^{2} - x_3^{2})\, f_1 + (x_3^{2} - x_1^{2})\, f_2 + (x_1^{2} - x_2^{2})\, f_3}{(x_2 - x_3)\, f_1 + (x_3 - x_1)\, f_2 + (x_1 - x_2)\, f_3}$$
> **[1 Mark]**
> 
> **Step 4 — Check denominator:** If the denominator is zero, the three points are collinear; perturb $x_2$ slightly by a small $\delta$ and restart. **[0.5 Mark]**
> 
> **Step 5 — Evaluate $f_4 = f(x_4)$.** **[0.5 Mark]**
> 
> **Step 6 — Update the triplet:** Among the four points $\{x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4\}$, identify the one with the **highest** function value — call it the "loser." The other three form the new triplet, re-sorted in ascending order so that the middle one has the smallest $f$ value (i.e., is the new $x_2$). **[2 Marks]**
> 
> **Step 7 — Convergence check:** Stop if any of the following are satisfied:
> - $\vert x_4^{\text{new}} - x_4^{\text{old}} \vert < \varepsilon_x$
> - $\vert f(x_4^{\text{new}}) - f(x_4^{\text{old}}) \vert < \varepsilon_f$
> - $\vert x_3 - x_1 \vert < \varepsilon_x$
> 
> Otherwise, return to Step 2 with the new triplet. **[1 Mark]**
> 
> **Step 8 — Return** $x^{*} = x_4$, $f^{*} = f_4$. **[0.5 Mark]**

**Update Strategy Details (boxed for emphasis):**

> **Three cases for the loser:**
> - **Case 1:** $f(x_1)$ is the highest → new triplet is $(x_2, x_4, x_3)$ after re-sorting.
> - **Case 2:** $f(x_3)$ is the highest → new triplet is $(x_1, x_4, x_2)$ after re-sorting.
> - **Case 3:** $f(x_2)$ is the highest (rare; means $x_4$ overshot) → new triplet is $(x_1, x_4, x_3)$ after re-sorting.

**[Total 7 Marks]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Function values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 = f(0) &= (0 - 3)^2 + 1 = 9 + 1 = 10 \\
f_2 = f(2) &= (2 - 3)^2 + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \\
f_3 = f(5) &= (5 - 3)^2 + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

Bracket verified: $f_2 = 2 < f_1 = 10$ and $f_2 = 2 < f_3 = 5$ ✓.

**Step 2 — Numerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_2^{2} - x_3^{2}) f_1 &= (4 - 25)(10) = -210 \\
(x_3^{2} - x_1^{2}) f_2 &= (25 - 0)(2) = 50 \\
(x_1^{2} - x_2^{2}) f_3 &= (0 - 4)(5) = -20
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum: $-210 + 50 - 20 = -180$.

**Step 3 — Denominator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_2 - x_3) f_1 &= (2 - 5)(10) = -30 \\
(x_3 - x_1) f_2 &= (5 - 0)(2) = 10 \\
(x_1 - x_2) f_3 &= (0 - 2)(5) = -10
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum: $-30 + 10 - 10 = -30$.

**Step 4 — Compute $x_4$:**

$$x_4 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{-180}{-30} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 6 = 3$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate $f(3)$:**

$$f_4 = (3 - 3)^2 + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1$$

**Step 6 — Verification:** Since $f(x) = (x-3)^2 + 1$ is a **pure quadratic**, the method converges in **exactly one iteration**, giving $x^{*} = 3$ and $f^{*} = 1$. This is the analytical minimum. ✓

**[7 Marks]**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Quadratic Estimation**

- ☐ **Core idea:** Fit a parabola through three points; take its vertex as the new estimate.
- ☐ **Why 3 points?** A quadratic has 3 coefficients — exactly matched by 3 interpolation conditions.
- ☐ **Why quadratic (not linear)?** Linear has no stationary point; quadratic has $x = -a_1 / (2 a_2)$.
- ☐ **Master formula:** 
$$x_4 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(x_2^{2} - x_3^{2}) f_1 + (x_3^{2} - x_1^{2}) f_2 + (x_1^{2} - x_2^{2}) f_3}{(x_2 - x_3) f_1 + (x_3 - x_1) f_2 + (x_1 - x_2) f_3}$$
- ☐ **Convergence rate:** Superlinear, order ≈ **1.324**.
- ☐ **Special case:** If $f$ is exactly a quadratic → **convergence in 1 iteration**.
- ☐ **Update rule:** Discard the point with the **highest** $f$ value; keep the other three; re-sort.
- ☐ **Stopping criteria:** $\vert \Delta x \vert < \varepsilon_x$ or $\vert \Delta f \vert < \varepsilon_f$ or interval size.
- ☐ **Safeguard:** Always check denominator ≠ 0 (collinear points); perturb $x_2$ if it is.
- ☐ **Derivative-free:** Works with $f(x)$ only — ideal for black-box / simulation-based problems.
- ☐ **Compare:** Faster than golden-section (linear) but less robust for noisy functions.
- ☐ **Fails when:** Function is non-unimodal; three points are collinear; denominator is zero.
- ☐ **Industrial use:** Step-size selection in line searches within nonlinear programming solvers.
- ☐ **Master equation source:** Equivalent to applying Lagrange interpolation followed by $q'(x) = 0$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bisection Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET638 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bisection Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Bisection Method** (also called the *Binary Search Method* or *Interval Halving Method*) is a deterministic, bracketing-based numerical root-finding technique used to determine the real root of a continuous nonlinear equation $f(x) = 0$ over a closed interval $[a, b]$. The method is mathematically grounded in the **Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)** and operates by recursively halving the interval and selecting the sub-interval that still brackets the root, thereby progressively narrowing down the location of the root to an arbitrary degree of prescribed accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Bisection Method is classified as a **bracketing (closed-domain) method**. It is guaranteed to converge for any continuous function $f(x)$ on $[a, b]$ provided the bracketing condition $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ is satisfied at the start. This unconditional convergence makes it the most reliable introductory method in numerical analysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are lost in a long, straight mountain tunnel, and you are told that the exit door is somewhere inside it. You shout "Hello!" and hear an echo from one end (let's call it the *left* end), but silence from the right. You walk a little toward the right and shout again — this time the echo comes from the *right*. By this simple observation, you now **know** the door lies between the last two positions where you shouted. You then walk to the **midpoint** of that new narrowed segment, shout again, and based on which side the echo returns, you **discard** the half that does not contain the door. Repeating this halving process, you will eventually pinpoint the door with centimeter-level accuracy.

**Mathematical Mapping of the Analogy:**

| Real-World Element | Mathematical Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Long tunnel | Closed interval $[a, b]$ |
| Echo detection | Sign of $f(x)$ |
| Exit door location | True root $x^*$ of $f(x) = 0$ |
| Midpoint you walk to | Bisected point $c = \frac{a+b}{2}$ |
| Discarding half of the tunnel | Updating $a$ or $b$ based on sign of $f(c)$ |
| Centimeter-level accuracy | Prescribed tolerance $\varepsilon$ |

### 1.3 Foundational Theorem (Intermediate Value Theorem)

If $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ and $k$ is any number strictly between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$, then there exists at least one $c \in (a, b)$ such that $f(c) = k$. For root finding, we set $k = 0$, which gives:

$$\exists \, c \in (a, b) \mid f(c) = 0 \iff f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> The condition $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ guarantees an **odd number of roots** inside the interval. If the function just *touches* the x-axis (e.g., $f(x) = x^2$), the method may fail because the sign does not change.

### 1.4 Physical / Engineering Significance

- **Power System Engineering:** Finding operating points (voltage magnitudes) where load flow equations $P - V^2 G = 0$ hold.
- **Structural Mechanics:** Determining load values at which a beam deflection $y(x)$ becomes zero.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Locating the equilibrium composition in a reaction where $K_{eq} - f(T) = 0$.
- **Control Systems:** Computing gain crossover frequencies where the open-loop transfer function magnitude equals unity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Algorithmic Breakdown

The Bisection Method follows a rigid, deterministic procedure. Each iteration performs **exactly one** of the following logical steps:

1. **Initialization Phase:**
   - The user supplies a continuous function $f(x)$, initial bracketing interval $[a, b]$, and a stopping tolerance $\varepsilon > 0$.
   - **Mandatory pre-check:** Verify $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$. If this fails, the algorithm must abort (no guaranteed root in the interval).

2. **Midpoint Computation:**
   - Calculate the midpoint of the current interval:
     $$c = \frac{a + b}{2}$$
   - This midpoint becomes the *current best estimate* of the true root $x^*$.

3. **Convergence Test (Stopping Criterion):**
   - The algorithm halts if **any** of the following conditions are met:
     - $\vert f(c) \vert < \varepsilon$  *(functional convergence)*
     - $\vert b - a \vert < \varepsilon$  *(interval convergence — most common in KTU papers)*
     - $\dfrac{\vert b - a \vert}{2} < \varepsilon$  *(equivalent to interval convergence, half-interval form)*
     - A maximum number of iterations $N_{max}$ is reached.

4. **Interval Refinement (Sub-interval Selection):**
   - Compute the product $f(a) \cdot f(c)$:
     - If $f(a) \cdot f(c) < 0$: The root lies in $[a, c]$. **Update** $b \leftarrow c$.
     - If $f(a) \cdot f(c) > 0$: The root lies in $[c, b]$. **Update** $a \leftarrow c$.
     - If $f(a) \cdot f(c) = 0$: $c$ is *exactly* the root — terminate.

5. **Iteration Counter:** Increment $n \leftarrow n + 1$ and return to Step 2.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Method

- **Why halve the interval?** Because it minimizes the worst-case error. Any point in $[a, b]$ is a candidate, and the midpoint guarantees that the next estimate is **at most** half the previous interval's length away from the true root.
- **Why does the method always converge?** Each iteration strictly halves the interval length. Since the interval length is always positive and monotonically decreasing, by the *Monotone Convergence Theorem* it must approach zero, forcing $a \to b \to x^*$.
- **Why is it called "linear convergence"?** Because the error decreases by a *constant factor* (specifically, $0.5$) at each step. Formally, $\vert e_{n+1} \vert \leq \dfrac{1}{2} \vert e_n \vert$, which is the definition of linear convergence with rate $0.5$.

### 2.3 Error Bound and Iteration Count Formula

The error after $n$ iterations is rigorously bounded by:

$$\vert e_n \vert = \vert x^* - c_n \vert \leq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+1}}$$

where $b_0$ and $a_0$ are the initial interval endpoints. A more practical (and frequently tested) form is:

$$\vert e_n \vert \leq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}$$

To achieve a prescribed absolute error $\varepsilon$, the **minimum required number of iterations** is:

$$n \geq \log_2\left(\frac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right) = \frac{\ln\left(\dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right)}{\ln(2)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** When asked *"How many iterations are needed to reduce the error below $10^{-5}$ starting from $[1, 2]$?"*, examiners expect students to apply the iteration-count formula above. Remember to take the **ceiling** (round up) of the computed $n$ because you cannot perform a fractional iteration.

### 2.4 Convergence Rate Classification

| Property | Bisection Method |
|---|---|
| Convergence Type | **Linear (1st order)** |
| Convergence Constant | $0.5$ |
| Order of Convergence $p$ | $1$ |
| Asymptotic Error Constant $\lambda$ | $0.5$ |
| Convergence Guarantee | **Yes**, unconditional (on continuous $f$ with sign change) |
| Derivative Required? | **No** |
| Bracketing Required? | **Yes** (initial) |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose in Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bracketing Condition | $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ | Verifies root exists in $[a, b]$ |
| 2 | Midpoint Formula | $c = \dfrac{a + b}{2}$ | Generates next iterate |
| 3 | Error Bound (after $n$ steps) | $\vert e_n \vert \leq \dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}$ | Bounds the absolute error |
| 4 | Iteration Count Requirement | $n \geq \log_2\left(\dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right)$ | Determines minimum iterations |
| 5 | Functional Stopping Criterion | $\vert f(c) \vert \leq \varepsilon$ | Algorithm termination |
| 6 | Interval Stopping Criterion | $\vert b - a \vert \leq \varepsilon$ | Algorithm termination |
| 7 | Half-Interval Stopping Criterion | $\dfrac{\vert b - a \vert}{2} \leq \varepsilon$ | Algorithm termination (preferred) |
| 8 | Linear Convergence Definition | $\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert} = \lambda < 1$ | Proves convergence rate |
| 9 | Convergence Constant | $\lambda = 0.5$ | Specific to Bisection |
| 10 | Number of Significant Digits | $-\log_{10}(\varepsilon)$ | Maps tolerance to precision |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Bisection Method, despite being slower than Newton-Raphson or Secant methods, is the **workhorse of safety-critical applications** because of its *unconditional convergence guarantee*. It is widely used in:
- **Embedded control firmware** for real-time root finding where convergence is non-negotiable.
- **Bracketing search modules** of more advanced methods (used to seed Newton-Raphson with a good initial guess).
- **Robotics and signal processing** for finding zero-crossings in noisy sensor data after preprocessing.
- **Power flow analysis** software where the load flow Jacobian may be ill-conditioned near the solution.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Error Bound

**Claim:** After $n$ iterations of the Bisection Method, the absolute error of the midpoint estimate $c_n$ satisfies $\vert x^* - c_n \vert \leq \dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}$.

**Proof by Induction on the interval length:**

**Base Case (n = 1):**
The initial interval has length $L_0 = b_0 - a_0$. The midpoint $c_1$ is the best estimate. The maximum possible distance from $c_1$ to the true root $x^* \in [a_0, b_0]$ is half the interval:

$$\vert x^* - c_1 \vert \leq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2} = \frac{L_0}{2}$$

So the formula holds for $n = 1$.

**Inductive Hypothesis:** Assume after $n$ iterations, $\vert x^* - c_n \vert \leq \dfrac{L_0}{2^n}$.

**Inductive Step:** The new interval $[a_n, b_n]$ has length:

$$L_n = b_n - a_n = \frac{L_{n-1}}{2} = \frac{L_0}{2^n}$$

The new midpoint is $c_{n+1} = \dfrac{a_n + b_n}{2}$, and the true root $x^*$ remains in $[a_n, b_n]$. Therefore:

$$\vert x^* - c_{n+1} \vert \leq \frac{L_n}{2} = \frac{L_0}{2^{n+1}}$$

This completes the induction. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Standard Root-Finding Problem

**Problem:** Find a root of $f(x) = x^3 - x - 2$ in the interval $[1, 2]$ using the Bisection Method. Perform **three iterations** and find the **error bound**.

**Step 1 — Verify the Bracketing Condition:**

$$f(1) = 1^3 - 1 - 2 = -2$$
$$f(2) = 2^3 - 2 - 2 = 4$$

Since $f(1) \cdot f(2) = (-2)(4) = -8 < 0$, a root is guaranteed in $[1, 2]$. ✓

**Step 2 — Iteration 1:**

$$c_1 = \frac{1 + 2}{2} = 1.5$$
$$f(1.5) = (1.5)^3 - 1.5 - 2 = 3.375 - 3.5 = -0.125$$

Since $f(1) \cdot f(1.5) = (-2)(-0.125) = 0.25 > 0$, the root lies in $[1.5, 2]$. Update: $a \leftarrow 1.5$, $b \leftarrow 2$.

**Step 3 — Iteration 2:**

$$c_2 = \frac{1.5 + 2}{2} = 1.75$$
$$f(1.75) = (1.75)^3 - 1.75 - 2 = 5.359375 - 3.75 = 1.609375$$

Since $f(1.5) \cdot f(1.75) = (-0.125)(1.609375) = -0.201 < 0$, the root lies in $[1.5, 1.75]$. Update: $a \leftarrow 1.5$, $b \leftarrow 1.75$.

**Step 4 — Iteration 3:**

$$c_3 = \frac{1.5 + 1.75}{2} = 1.625$$
$$f(1.625) = (1.625)^3 - 1.625 - 2 = 4.291015625 - 3.625 = 0.666015625$$

Since $f(1.5) \cdot f(1.625) = (-0.125)(0.666015625) = -0.0833 < 0$, the root lies in $[1.5, 1.625]$. Update: $a \leftarrow 1.5$, $b \leftarrow 1.625$.

**Step 5 — Error Bound After 3 Iterations:**

$$\vert e_3 \vert \leq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^3} = \frac{2 - 1}{8} = 0.125$$

The true root (known to high precision) is $x^* \approx 1.5214$, so our estimate $c_3 = 1.625$ has actual error $\vert 1.625 - 1.5214 \vert = 0.1036$, which is indeed $\leq 0.125$. ✓

**Iteration Summary Table:**

| $n$ | $a_n$ | $b_n$ | $c_n$ | $f(c_n)$ | New Interval |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 1.5 | -0.125 | $[1.5, 2.0]$ |
| 2 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 1.75 | 1.609 | $[1.5, 1.75]$ |
| 3 | 1.5 | 1.75 | 1.625 | 0.666 | $[1.5, 1.625]$ |

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Iteration Count Calculation

**Problem:** Find the minimum number of iterations required to find a root of $f(x) = \cos(x) - x$ in $[0, 1]$ with absolute accuracy $\varepsilon = 10^{-4}$.

**Solution:**

Apply the iteration count formula:

$$n \geq \log_2\left(\frac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right) = \log_2\left(\frac{1 - 0}{10^{-4}}\right) = \log_2(10^4)$$

Convert to base 10 logarithm for hand calculation:

$$n \geq \frac{\log_{10}(10^4)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{4}{0.30103} \approx 13.2877$$

Since $n$ must be an integer, take the ceiling: $n \geq 14$ iterations.

**Verification of Error Bound at n = 14:**

$$\vert e_{14} \vert \leq \frac{1}{2^{14}} = \frac{1}{16384} \approx 6.10 \times 10^{-5} < 10^{-4} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that for $n = 13$, $\dfrac{1}{2^{13}} = \dfrac{1}{8192} \approx 1.22 \times 10^{-4}$, which is **not** less than $10^{-4}$. This is why we must round **up** to $n = 14$. Many KTU students lose marks by rounding down.

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Direct-to-Tolerance Computation

**Problem:** Find an approximate root of $f(x) = x^2 - 3$ in $[1, 2]$ correct to **3 decimal places** using the Bisection Method.

**Solution Strategy:** For $k$ decimal places of accuracy, the tolerance is $\varepsilon = 0.5 \times 10^{-k}$. For 3 decimal places:

$$\varepsilon = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0005$$

Compute iterations until $\vert b - a \vert < 0.001$ (equivalent to requiring $\dfrac{\vert b - a \vert}{2} < 0.0005$).

**Iteration Trace (compact form):**

| $n$ | $a_n$ | $b_n$ | $c_n$ | $f(c_n) = c_n^2 - 3$ | Sign Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 1.5 | -0.75 | $f(a)f(c) > 0 \Rightarrow a = c$ |
| 2 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 1.75 | 0.0625 | $f(a)f(c) < 0 \Rightarrow b = c$ |
| 3 | 1.5 | 1.75 | 1.625 | -0.3594 | $a = c$ |
| 4 | 1.625 | 1.75 | 1.6875 | -0.1523 | $a = c$ |
| 5 | 1.6875 | 1.75 | 1.71875 | -0.0459 | $a = c$ |
| 6 | 1.71875 | 1.75 | 1.734375 | 0.0078 | $b = c$ |
| 7 | 1.71875 | 1.734375 | 1.726563 | -0.0191 | $a = c$ |
| 8 | 1.726563 | 1.734375 | 1.730469 | -0.0057 | $a = c$ |
| 9 | 1.730469 | 1.734375 | 1.732422 | 0.0010 | $b = c$ |
| 10 | 1.730469 | 1.732422 | 1.731445 | -0.0023 | $a = c$ |
| 11 | 1.731445 | 1.732422 | 1.731934 | -0.0006 | $a = c$ |

At $n = 11$: $\vert b - a \vert = \vert 1.732422 - 1.731445 \vert = 0.000977 < 0.001$. **Stop.**

**Result:** $x^* \approx 1.7319$ (correct to 3 decimal places, true value $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.7320508$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Valuation Note:** A common mistake is to stop when $\vert f(c) \vert$ becomes small. The correct stopping rule for "$k$ decimal places" is $\vert b - a \vert \leq 0.5 \times 10^{1-k}$ or equivalently $\dfrac{\vert b - a \vert}{2} \leq 0.5 \times 10^{-k}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START]) --> inputB[/Read f x, interval a b, tolerance epsilon/]
    inputB --> checkC{f a times f b less than 0?}
    checkC -- NO --> failD[/NO ROOT BRACKETED: ABORT/]
    checkC -- YES --> initE[Set n = 0, a0 = a, b0 = b]
    initE --> loopF[/ITERATION LOOP BEGINS/]
    loopF --> compG[Compute midpoint cn = an plus bn divided by 2]
    compG --> evalH[Evaluate f cn]
    evalH --> stop1{abs f cn less than epsilon OR abs b minus a less than epsilon}
    stop1 -- YES --> resultI[/OUTPUT: Approximate root = cn, n iterations/]
    resultI --> endJ([END])
    stop1 -- NO --> decideK{f an times f cn less than 0?}
    decideK -- YES --> updL[bn = cn]
    decideK -- NO --> updM[an = cn]
    updL --> incrN[n = n + 1]
    updM --> incrN
    incrN --> loopF
    failD --> endJ
```

### 4.2 Interval-Halving Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph iter1["ITERATION 1: Interval 1.0 to 2.0"]
        A1["a = 1.0 f a = -2"] --- M1["c = 1.5 f c = -0.125"] --- B1["b = 2.0 f b = 4"]
    end
    subgraph iter2["ITERATION 2: Interval 1.5 to 2.0"]
        A2["a = 1.5 f a = -0.125"] --- M2["c = 1.75 f c = 1.609"] --- B2["b = 2.0 f b = 4"]
    end
    subgraph iter3["ITERATION 3: Interval 1.5 to 1.75"]
        A3["a = 1.5 f a = -0.125"] --- M3["c = 1.625 f c = 0.666"] --- B3["b = 1.75 f b = 1.609"]
    end
    iter1 --> iter2 --> iter3
```

### 4.3 Convergence Rate Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    meth[/NUMERICAL ROOT-FINDING METHODS/] --> cat1["BRACKETING METHODS"]
    meth --> cat2["OPEN METHODS"]
    cat1 --> b1["Bisection Method"]
    cat1 --> b2["False Position Method"]
    b1 --> rate1["Linear Convergence, rate 0.5"]
    b2 --> rate2["Linear Convergence, rate variable"]
    cat2 --> o1["Newton Raphson Method"]
    cat2 --> o2["Secant Method"]
    o1 --> rate3["Quadratic Convergence, rate 2"]
    o2 --> rate4["Superlinear, rate 1.618"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The flowchart represents the **exact algorithmic logic** that examiners expect students to draw in the 14-mark questions. Always use diamond shapes for decisions and rectangles for process steps. The root is guaranteed to be **bracketed** at every iteration, which is the defining property of all closed-domain methods.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the Intermediate Value Theorem as it applies to the Bisection Method. Why is this theorem essential for the method's validity?**

**Model Answer:**
The Intermediate Value Theorem states that if $f(x)$ is continuous on a closed interval $[a, b]$ and $k$ is any value between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$, then there exists at least one $c \in (a, b)$ such that $f(c) = k$. For root-finding, we set $k = 0$, so the theorem guarantees at least one root in $[a, b]$ whenever $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$.

This theorem is **essential** because:
1. It provides the **mathematical justification** for the existence of a root in the initial bracket.
2. It guarantees that the root is **preserved** in the sub-interval selected at each iteration, since the sign change persists across halving.
3. It ensures the algorithm is **not searching in vain** — the root is mathematically proven to exist in the shrinking interval.

Without the IVT, the Bisection Method would have no theoretical foundation and could terminate at a non-root point.

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the order of convergence of the Bisection Method? Justify your answer with the error ratio formula.**

**Model Answer:**
The Bisection Method has **linear convergence** (also called convergence of **order 1**). 

**Justification:** The error after $n$ iterations satisfies $\vert e_{n+1} \vert \leq \dfrac{1}{2} \vert e_n \vert$. Taking the limit of the error ratio:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

By the formal definition of order of convergence $p$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert^p} = \lambda \quad (0 < \lambda < 1)$$

For $p = 1$, $\lambda = 0.5$ satisfies $0 < 0.5 < 1$, confirming **linear convergence** with asymptotic error constant $\lambda = 0.5$. This is the slowest among standard root-finding methods (Newton-Raphson is quadratic, Secant is superlinear), but it is the most **reliable** because it converges unconditionally.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the Bisection Method for finding the real root of $f(x) = 0$ with a neat flowchart. Discuss its convergence properties. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the Bisection Method, find the real root of $f(x) = x^3 - 4x - 9$ in the interval $[2, 3]$, accurate to 3 decimal places. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Algorithm (Step-by-Step Description):**

1. Identify a continuous function $f(x)$ and an initial interval $[a, b]$ such that $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$.
2. Compute midpoint $c = \dfrac{a + b}{2}$.
3. Evaluate $f(c)$.
4. **Stopping test:** If $\vert b - a \vert < \varepsilon$ (or $\vert f(c) \vert < \varepsilon$), terminate. Output $c$ as the approximate root.
5. **Interval update:** If $f(a) \cdot f(c) < 0$, set $b = c$. Otherwise, set $a = c$.
6. Increment iteration counter and return to Step 2.

**Flowchart:** Refer to the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1 of these notes for the visual representation. Key elements include the start node, input block, sign-condition check, midpoint computation, and the decision diamond that determines the sub-interval.

**Convergence Properties:**

- **Convergence Type:** Linear (order 1) with asymptotic constant $\lambda = 0.5$.
- **Convergence Guarantee:** Unconditional, provided $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ and $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$.
- **Error Bound:** $\vert e_n \vert \leq \dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}$.
- **Iteration Requirement:** $n \geq \log_2\left(\dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right)$.
- **Derivative Required:** No.
- **Drawbacks:** Slow convergence (linear only); cannot detect multiple roots; cannot extrapolate beyond initial bracket.

**Advantages:** Simplicity, reliability, and guaranteed convergence make it ideal for initial bracketing and safety-critical systems.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Verify bracketing condition:**

$$f(2) = 2^3 - 4(2) - 9 = 8 - 8 - 9 = -9$$
$$f(3) = 3^3 - 4(3) - 9 = 27 - 12 - 9 = 6$$

$f(2) \cdot f(3) = (-9)(6) = -54 < 0$. Root exists in $[2, 3]$. ✓

**Step 2 — Stopping criterion for 3 decimal places:** $\varepsilon = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0005$. Stop when $\vert b - a \vert \leq 0.001$.

**Step 3 — Iteration Table:**

| $n$ | $a_n$ | $b_n$ | $c_n = (a_n+b_n)/2$ | $f(c_n)$ | New Interval |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.0 | 3.0 | 2.5 | $15.625 - 10 - 9 = -3.375$ | $[2.5, 3.0]$ |
| 2 | 2.5 | 3.0 | 2.75 | $20.797 - 11 - 9 = 0.797$ | $[2.5, 2.75]$ |
| 3 | 2.5 | 2.75 | 2.625 | $18.088 - 10.5 - 9 = -1.412$ | $[2.625, 2.75]$ |
| 4 | 2.625 | 2.75 | 2.6875 | $19.412 - 10.75 - 9 = -0.338$ | $[2.6875, 2.75]$ |
| 5 | 2.6875 | 2.75 | 2.71875 | $20.096 - 10.875 - 9 = 0.221$ | $[2.6875, 2.71875]$ |
| 6 | 2.6875 | 2.71875 | 2.703125 | $19.751 - 10.8125 - 9 = -0.061$ | $[2.703125, 2.71875]$ |
| 7 | 2.703125 | 2.71875 | 2.7109375 | $19.923 - 10.844 - 9 = 0.079$ | $[2.703125, 2.7109375]$ |
| 8 | 2.703125 | 2.7109375 | 2.70703125 | $19.836 - 10.828 - 9 = 0.008$ | $[2.703125, 2.70703125]$ |
| 9 | 2.703125 | 2.70703125 | 2.705078125 | $19.793 - 10.820 - 9 = -0.027$ | $[2.705078125, 2.70703125]$ |
| 10 | 2.705078125 | 2.70703125 | 2.706054688 | $19.815 - 10.824 - 9 = -0.009$ | $[2.706054688, 2.70703125]$ |
| 11 | 2.706054688 | 2.70703125 | 2.706542969 | $19.824 - 10.826 - 9 = -0.002$ | $[2.706542969, 2.70703125]$ |
| 12 | 2.706542969 | 2.70703125 | 2.706787109 | $19.829 - 10.827 - 9 = 0.002$ | $[2.706542969, 2.706787109]$ |

At $n = 12$: $\vert b - a \vert = 0.000244 \leq 0.001$. **Stop.**

**Final Answer:** $x^* \approx 2.7068$ (rounded to 4 decimal places, then truncated to 3 decimals as $2.706$). The true root is $\approx 2.7065$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Bisection Method:**
> 1. **Missing Bracket Check (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often skip verifying $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ before starting. Always show this step explicitly.
> 2. **Wrong Stopping Criterion (–2 marks):** Using $\vert f(c) \vert < 0.001$ instead of $\vert b - a \vert < 0.001$ is a common but **incorrect** stopping rule for "3 decimal places" problems.
> 3. **Sign Logic Error (–3 marks):** When $f(a) \cdot f(c) < 0$, the new interval is $[a, c]$ (so $b \leftarrow c$). When $f(a) \cdot f(c) > 0$, the new interval is $[c, b]$ (so $a \leftarrow c$). Reversing this costs full marks.
> 4. **Forgetting Iteration Count Formula (–1 mark):** When the problem specifies tolerance, always state the formula $n \geq \log_2((b-a)/\varepsilon)$ even if you are doing iterations directly.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the error bound formula for the Bisection Method. Hence, determine the number of iterations required to reduce the error below $10^{-6}$ when the initial interval is $[0, 5]$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Find the smallest positive root of $f(x) = e^{-x} - \sin(x)$ using the Bisection Method, starting from $[0, 1]$. Perform 4 iterations. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Derivation of the Error Bound:**

Let $x^*$ be the true root, and let $c_n = \dfrac{a_n + b_n}{2}$ be the midpoint at iteration $n$. The true root always lies in the current interval $[a_n, b_n]$, so:

$$a_n \leq x^* \leq b_n$$

The error $\vert e_n \vert = \vert x^* - c_n \vert$ is bounded by half the interval length:

$$\vert e_n \vert \leq \frac{b_n - a_n}{2}$$

By the halving property of the algorithm, $b_n - a_n = \dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}$. Substituting:

$$\vert e_n \vert \leq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+1}}$$

If we use the looser bound (commonly accepted in KTU solutions):

$$\boxed{\vert e_n \vert \leq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

For initial interval $[a_0, b_0] = [0, 5]$ and tolerance $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$:

$$n \geq \log_2\left(\frac{5 - 0}{10^{-6}}\right) = \log_2(5 \times 10^6)$$

$$n \geq \frac{\log_{10}(5 \times 10^6)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{\log_{10}(5) + 6}{0.30103} = \frac{0.69897 + 6}{0.30103} = \frac{6.69897}{0.30103}$$

$$n \geq 22.2536$$

**Rounding up:** $n = 23$ iterations are required.

**Verification:** $\dfrac{5}{2^{23}} = \dfrac{5}{8388608} \approx 5.96 \times 10^{-7} < 10^{-6}$ ✓ (For $n = 22$: $\dfrac{5}{2^{22}} \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-6} > 10^{-6}$ ✗.)

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Verify bracketing:**

$$f(0) = e^{0} - \sin(0) = 1 - 0 = 1$$
$$f(1) = e^{-1} - \sin(1) \approx 0.3679 - 0.8415 = -0.4736$$

$f(0) \cdot f(1) = (1)(-0.4736) = -0.4736 < 0$. Root in $[0, 1]$. ✓

**Step 2 — Four Iterations:**

| $n$ | $a_n$ | $b_n$ | $c_n$ | $f(c_n) = e^{-c_n} - \sin(c_n)$ | New Interval |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.0 | 1.0 | 0.5 | $0.6065 - 0.4794 = 0.1271$ | $[0.5, 1.0]$ |
| 2 | 0.5 | 1.0 | 0.75 | $0.4724 - 0.6816 = -0.2092$ | $[0.5, 0.75]$ |
| 3 | 0.5 | 0.75 | 0.625 | $0.5353 - 0.5851 = -0.0498$ | $[0.5, 0.625]$ |
| 4 | 0.5 | 0.625 | 0.5625 | $0.5698 - 0.5338 = 0.0360$ | $[0.5625, 0.625]$ |

**Detailed Computations:**

- $e^{-0.5} = 0.60653$, $\sin(0.5) = 0.47943$, $f(0.5) = 0.12710$
- $e^{-0.75} = 0.47237$, $\sin(0.75) = 0.68164$, $f(0.75) = -0.20927$
- $e^{-0.625} = 0.53526$, $\sin(0.625) = 0.58510$, $f(0.625) = -0.04984$
- $e^{-0.5625} = 0.56978$, $\sin(0.5625) = 0.53383$, $f(0.5625) = 0.03595$

**Step 3 — Result:** After 4 iterations, the root is approximated as $c_4 = 0.5625$, with error bound $\vert e_4 \vert \leq \dfrac{1}{2^4} = 0.0625$. The true root is $x^* \approx 0.5885$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in Bisection Method Problems:**
> 1. **Skipping the bracket verification** $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ at the start: Lose **1 to 2 marks**.
> 2. **Choosing the wrong stopping rule** when the problem says "3 decimal places": Use $\vert b - a \vert \leq 0.001$ or $\dfrac{\vert b-a\vert}{2} \leq 0.0005$, **not** $\vert f(c) \vert \leq 0.001$. Lose **2 marks**.
> 3. **Reversing the sign-update logic:** When $f(a) \cdot f(c) < 0$, the new interval is $[a, c]$, so update $b \leftarrow c$ (not $a \leftarrow c$). This single error can cost **3 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting to round UP** the iteration count: Always take the ceiling of $n$. Lose **1 mark**.
> 5. **Not stating convergence type explicitly:** "Order of convergence" is a high-yield conceptual question; explicitly write "linear convergence with $\lambda = 0.5$." Lose **1 to 2 marks**.
> 6. **Arithmetic mistakes in $f(c_n)$ evaluation:** Use calculators rigorously and show intermediate values, not just final signs.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** Bisection Method finds roots of $f(x) = 0$ by repeatedly halving a bracketing interval.
- **Foundation:** Intermediate Value Theorem — requires $f$ continuous and $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$.
- **Iteration Formula:** $c_n = \dfrac{a_n + b_n}{2}$.
- **Sub-interval Selection Rule:**
  - $f(a) \cdot f(c) < 0 \Rightarrow$ new interval $[a, c]$
  - $f(a) \cdot f(c) > 0 \Rightarrow$ new interval $[c, b]$
- **Error Bound:** $\vert e_n \vert \leq \dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}$
- **Minimum Iterations:** $n \geq \log_2\left(\dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right)$ (always round UP).
- **Convergence:** Linear, with rate $\lambda = 0.5$.
- **Stopping Rules:** (i) $\vert f(c) \vert \leq \varepsilon$, (ii) $\vert b - a \vert \leq \varepsilon$, (iii) $\dfrac{\vert b-a \vert}{2} \leq \varepsilon$, (iv) $n = N_{max}$.
- **Decimal-Place Rule:** For $k$ decimal places, $\varepsilon = 0.5 \times 10^{-k}$.
- **Advantages:** Unconditional convergence, derivative-free, simple, robust.
- **Disadvantages:** Slow linear rate, requires initial bracket, cannot handle even-multiplicity roots.
- **Always Remember:** Midpoint, sign check, interval update, error bound formula, order of convergence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newton-Raphson Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET638 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Newton-Raphson Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Newton-Raphson Method** is an open-order, iterative root-finding algorithm used to successively refine approximations to a real root of a continuously differentiable real-valued function $f(x) = 0$. Given an initial guess $x_0$ sufficiently close to the true root $x^*$, the method generates an improved sequence $\{x_n\}$ using the recurrence relation

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

provided $f'(x_n) \neq 0$. The method exhibits **quadratic convergence** in a neighborhood of the root, which makes it one of the most computationally efficient first-order numerical schemes used in optimization, equation solving, and gradient-based learning algorithms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In **PEEET638 – Optimization Techniques (Module 1)**, the Newton-Raphson method is treated as a foundational **classical root-location technique**. It serves as a building block for unconstrained optimization (where $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$ is solved) and for advanced algorithms like the **Newton-CG** and **Levenberg-Marquardt** methods.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are standing on a hillside in fog and want to descend into the **valley (the root)**. You cannot see the bottom. However, you can feel the **slope** of the ground under your feet. The Newton-Raphson method says:

> *"Take one giant step in the direction the slope tells you is steepest downward, and you will land on the valley floor."*

- The **function value $f(x_n)$** is your current elevation.
- The **derivative $f'(x_n)$** is the slope (steepness and direction).
- The **next point $x_{n+1}$** is where the slope-line intersects the ground level (sea level) — that is, the **root of the tangent line**.

Mathematically, at every step we replace the curve $y = f(x)$ with its tangent line at $x_n$ and then find where that line crosses the x-axis.

### 1.3 Why is it Important?

| Aspect | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|
| Root of nonlinear equation | Solving equilibrium, fixed-point, and characteristic equations |
| Optimization bridge | Finding critical points by setting $f'(x) = 0$ |
| Speed of convergence | Quadratic — error squares each iteration |
| Real-world use | Power-flow in grids, trajectory planning, machine learning, machine design, structural analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence Order:** Newton-Raphson has order of convergence **p = 2**. This means if the current error is $\varepsilon$, the next iteration's error is approximately proportional to $\varepsilon^2$. The number of correct digits roughly **doubles** per iteration near the root.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Stepwise Algorithm

1. **Initialize:** Choose initial guess $x_0$, tolerance $\varepsilon > 0$, and maximum iterations $N_{\max}$.
2. **Evaluate:** Compute $f(x_n)$ and the derivative $f'(x_n)$.
3. **Derivative Test:** If $f'(x_n) = 0$, the tangent is horizontal — **STOP**, the method fails.
4. **Newton Update:** Compute the next approximation:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

5. **Convergence Check:** If $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$ OR $\vert f(x_{n+1}) \vert < \varepsilon$ OR $\frac{\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert}{\vert x_{n+1} \vert} < \varepsilon$, **STOP** and accept $x_{n+1}$ as the root.
6. **Bounded Iteration:** If $n \geq N_{\max}$, **STOP** (failure to converge).
7. Otherwise set $n \leftarrow n+1$ and return to Step 2.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Formula

- The **Why** of the update: At $x_n$, we know the slope $f'(x_n)$ and current height $f(x_n)$. To bring the height to zero (sea level), we step horizontally by the amount $\frac{\text{height}}{\text{slope}}$, which is exactly $\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$. We move *opposite* to this displacement because the tangent line *rises* if the slope is positive.
- The **How** of quadratic convergence: When the root is simple and the function is sufficiently smooth, Taylor's theorem gives $f(x^*) = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x^* - x_n) + \tfrac{1}{2}f''(\xi)(x^* - x_n)^2$. Setting $f(x^*)=0$ and solving for $x^* - x_n$ yields the error recursion, and the *first-order* term cancels, leaving the **second-order** (squared-error) term as dominant.

### 2.3 Conditions for Convergence

The Newton-Raphson method **converges** to a root $x^*$ if:

1. $f$ is **twice continuously differentiable** in a neighborhood of $x^*$.
2. $f'(x^*) \neq 0$ (the root must be **simple**, not multiple).
3. The initial guess $x_0$ is **sufficiently close** to $x^*$.
4. A useful **sufficient condition** for convergence from $x_0$ is:

$$\left\vert \frac{f(x_0)\, f''(x_0)}{[f'(x_0)]^2} \right\vert < 1$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table below, the vertical bar `|` symbol has been deliberately replaced with the LaTeX command `\vert` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Expression | Name / Role | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$ | Newton-Raphson Update | Computing the next iterate |
| 2 | $e_{n+1} \approx \dfrac{f''(\xi)}{2 f'(\xi)}\, e_n^2$ | Asymptotic Error Constant | Establishing quadratic convergence |
| 3 | $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$ | Absolute-Error Criterion | Stopping test |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert}{\vert x_{n+1} \vert} < \varepsilon$ | Relative-Error Criterion | Stopping test (scale invariant) |
| 5 | $\vert f(x_{n+1}) \vert < \varepsilon$ | Residual Criterion | Stopping test |
| 6 | $\left\vert \dfrac{f(x_0)\, f''(x_0)}{[f'(x_0)]^2} \right\vert < 1$ | Sufficient Convergence Test | Initial-guess validation |
| 7 | $p = 2$ | Order of Convergence | Speed classification |
| 8 | $\nabla^2 f(x_n)\, \Delta x = -\nabla f(x_n)$ | Multi-Variable Newton | Optimization extension (Module 1/2 link) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications
