# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 KKT Algorithm Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define NLP Problem] --> B[Standardize Constraints as gj x less than or equal to 0]
    B --> C[Construct Lagrangian Function L]
    C --> D[Apply Stationarity: Partial derivatives of L equals 0]
    D --> E{Hypothesize Active Constraint Set}
    E -->|Try All Combinations| F[Apply Complementary Slackness]
    F --> G[Solve Stationarity Equations]
    G --> H{All lambdas greater than or equal to 0?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Check Primal Feasibility gj less than or equal to 0]
    H -->|No| E
    I --> J{Feasible and Satisfies All KKT?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Optimal Solution Found]
    J -->|No| E
    K --> L[Verify Sufficiency via Convexity Check]
    L --> M[Report Global Optimum]
```

## 4.2 Constraint Activity Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Constraint gj x] --> node2{gj x star equals 0?}
    node2 -->|Yes Active| node3[lambda j can be greater than 0]
    node2 -->|No Inactive| node4[lambda j must equal 0]
    node3 --> node5{lambda j greater than 0?}
    node5 -->|Yes Strictly Active| node6[Constraint is Binding]
    node5 -->|No Degenerate| node7[Constraint active but lambda equals 0]
    node4 --> node8[Constraint is Slack]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of KKT Solver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Input NLP Data: f, g, h, x bounds] --> Parser[Constraint Standardizer]
    Parser --> Lagrangian[Symbolic Lagrangian Builder]
    Lagrangian --> Grad[Automatic Gradient Engine]
    Grad --> HypGen[Active Set Hypothesis Generator]
    HypGen --> SysSolver[Linear/Nonlinear System Solver]
    SysSolver --> Validator[KKT Condition Validator]
    Validator --> ConvCheck[Convexity Checker Hessian PSD]
    Validator -->|Any Condition Fails| HypGen
    ConvCheck --> Output[Optimal x star, lambda star, f star]
```

## 4.4 Relationship Between Classical Methods and KKT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Classic[Lagrange Multipliers Equality Only] --> KuhnTucker
    GJM[Generalized Lagrange Method] --> KuhnTucker
    Farkas[Farkas Lemma 1902] --> KuhnTucker
    Karush[Karush 1939 MS Thesis] --> KuhnTucker1951
    Kuhn1951[Kuhn Tucker 1951 Paper] --> KuhnTucker
    KuhnTucker[KKT Theorems 1951 General NLP] --> ConvexProg[Convex Programming]
    KuhnTucker --> DualTheory[Duality Theory Wolfe 1961]
    KuhnTucker --> Interior[Interior Point Methods 1984]
    KuhnTucker --> SQP[SQP Algorithms Modern Solvers]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note (for KTU General Awareness):**
> - **William Karush (1939)**: First derived the conditions in an unpublished Master's thesis at the University of Chicago.
> - **Kuhn & Tucker (1951)**: Published the conditions in a landmark paper, "Nonlinear Programming," at the 2nd Berkeley Symposium.
> - The conditions are called **KKT** (Karush-Kuhn-Tucker) since Karush's earlier contribution was recognized decades later.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Short Answer — Definition)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

> **Q:** State the **Kuhn-Tucker necessary conditions** for the nonlinear programming problem:
> Minimize $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$, $j = 1, \ldots, m$ and $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, $k = 1, \ldots, p$. Explain the **complementary slackness condition** with its physical interpretation.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Kuhn-Tucker necessary conditions require that at the optimal point $\mathbf{x}^*$, there exist multipliers $\lambda_j^* \geq 0$ and $\mu_k^*$ such that:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j^* \nabla g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \mu_k^* \nabla h_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Complementary Slackness Condition:**
$$
\lambda_j^* g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad \forall j = 1, 2, \ldots, m
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** If a constraint is **inactive** ($g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$), it does not affect the optimum, so $\lambda_j^* = 0$. If a constraint is **active** ($g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$), it is binding the optimum, and its multiplier $\lambda_j^*$ represents the marginal rate of change of the objective with respect to relaxing that constraint. The condition $\lambda_j^* g_j = 0$ ensures that exactly one of these two states holds for each constraint. **[3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2 (Short Answer — Conceptual)
**[KTU University Exam — December 2022]**

> **Q:** Distinguish between the **Kuhn-Tucker necessary conditions** and the **Kuhn-Tucker sufficient conditions**. Under what additional assumptions do the necessary conditions become sufficient?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Necessary Conditions | Sufficient Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Optimal $\Rightarrow$ KKT holds | KKT holds $\Rightarrow$ Optimal |
| **Requirements** | Constraint qualification (e.g., LICQ) and $\mathcal{C}^1$ differentiability | $f$ convex, $g_j$ convex (for active constraints with $\lambda_j > 0$), $h_k$ affine |
| **Strength** | Local optimum | Global optimum |
| **Use** | Identify candidate optimal points | Confirm global optimality |

**Additional Assumptions for Sufficiency:**
The necessary conditions become sufficient when the problem is a **convex program**, i.e., when $f$ is convex, each inequality constraint $g_j$ is a convex function, and each equality constraint $h_k$ is affine (linear). Under these conditions, KKT is both necessary and sufficient for global optimality. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Full Solution)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> **Q:** Use the **Kuhn-Tucker conditions** to solve the following nonlinear programming problem:
> $$ \text{Minimize } f(x_1, x_2) = 2x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 - 2x_1 x_2 - 4x_1 - 6x_2 $$
> Subject to:
> $$ x_1 + x_2 \leq 2, \quad 2x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 6, \quad x_1 \geq 0, \quad x_2 \geq 0 $$

### Part (a): Formulate the KKT Conditions and Solve the System (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Standardize constraints as $g_j \leq 0$:**
$$
g_1: x_1 + x_2 - 2 \leq 0
$$
$$
g_2: 2x_1 + 3x_2 - 6 \leq 0
$$
$$
g_3: -x_1 \leq 0 \quad (\text{i.e., } x_1 \geq 0)
$$
$$
g_4: -x_2 \leq 0 \quad (\text{i.e., } x_2 \geq 0)
$$

**Step 2: Construct the Lagrangian function:**

$$
\mathcal{L} = 2x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 - 2x_1 x_2 - 4x_1 - 6x_2 + \lambda_1(x_1 + x_2 - 2) + \lambda_2(2x_1 + 3x_2 - 6) - \lambda_3 x_1 - \lambda_4 x_2
$$

**Step 3: Stationarity conditions:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_1} = 4x_1 - 2x_2 - 4 + \lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2 - \lambda_3 = 0 \quad \cdots (i)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_2} = 4x_2 - 2x_1 - 6 + \lambda_1 + 3\lambda_2 - \lambda_4 = 0 \quad \cdots (ii)
$$

**Step 4: Complementary slackness conditions:**

$$
\lambda_1(x_1 + x_2 - 2) = 0, \quad \lambda_2(2x_1 + 3x_2 - 6) = 0, \quad \lambda_3 x_1 = 0, \quad \lambda_4 x_2 = 0
$$

**Step 5: Test Hypotheses — Try active constraint set $\{g_1, g_3, g_4\}$:**

Assume $g_1$ active ($x_1 + x_2 = 2$), $g_3$ active ($x_1 = 0$), $g_4$ active ($x_2 = 0$). 
But $x_1 = x_2 = 0$ gives $0 + 0 = 0 \neq 2$. Contradiction.

**Try $\{g_1, g_3\}$ active:** $x_1 = 0$, $x_2 = 2$.
- $g_2(0, 2) = 6 - 6 = 0 \Rightarrow g_2$ also active!
- $g_4(0, 2) = -2 < 0 \Rightarrow g_4$ inactive, so $\lambda_4 = 0$.

**Try $\{g_1, g_2, g_3\}$ active:** $x_1 = 0$, $x_1 + x_2 = 2 \Rightarrow x_2 = 2$, $2x_1 + 3x_2 = 6$ ✓.

From (i) with $x_1 = 0$, $x_2 = 2$, $\lambda_3 = 0$ (inactive):
$$
4(0) - 2(2) - 4 + \lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2 - 0 = 0
$$
$$
-8 + \lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2 = 0 \quad \cdots (i')
$$

From (ii) with $x_1 = 0$, $x_2 = 2$, $\lambda_4 = 0$ (inactive):
$$
4(2) - 2(0) - 6 + \lambda_1 + 3\lambda_2 - 0 = 0
$$
$$
2 + \lambda_1 + 3\lambda_2 = 0 \quad \cdots (ii')
$$

**Solving (i') and (ii'):**

Subtracting (ii') from (i'):
$$
-10 - \lambda_2 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_2 = -10
$$

But $\lambda_2 \geq 0$ required! Contradiction. Hypothesis **rejected**.

**Step 6: Try $\{g_2, g_3\}$ active:** $x_1 = 0$, $2x_1 + 3x_2 = 6 \Rightarrow x_2 = 2$, $g_4$ inactive.

This leads to same numerical contradiction.

**Step 7: Try $\{g_2\}$ only active with interior $x_1, x_2 > 0$:**
- $g_1$ inactive ($\lambda_1 = 0$), $g_3$ inactive ($\lambda_3 = 0$), $g_4$ inactive ($\lambda_4 = 0$).
- $g_2$ active: $2x_1 + 3x_2 = 6$.

From (i): $4x_1 - 2x_2 - 4 + 2\lambda_2 = 0$
From (ii): $4x_2 - 2x_1 - 6 + 3\lambda_2 = 0$

Combined with $2x_1 + 3x_2 = 6 \Rightarrow x_1 = (6 - 3x_2)/2$:

Substituting and solving the $3 \times 3$ linear system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4x_1 - 2x_2 + 2\lambda_2 &= 4 \\
-2x_1 + 4x_2 + 3\lambda_2 &= 6 \\
2x_1 + 3x_2 &= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

From third equation: $x_1 = 3 - 1.5 x_2$. Substituting into first:
$$
4(3 - 1.5x_2) - 2x_2 + 2\lambda_2 = 4 \Rightarrow 12 - 8x_2 + 2\lambda_2 = 4 \Rightarrow 2\lambda_2 = -8 + 8x_2 \quad \cdots (*)
$$

Substituting into second:
$$
-2(3 - 1.5x_2) + 4x_2 + 3\lambda_2 = 6 \Rightarrow -6 + 7x_2 + 3\lambda_2 = 6 \Rightarrow 3\lambda_2 = 12 - 7x_2 \quad \cdots (**)
$$

From (*): $\lambda_2 = -4 + 4x_2$. Substituting into (**):
$$
3(-4 + 4x_2) = 12 - 7x_2 \Rightarrow -12 + 12x_2 = 12 - 7x_2 \Rightarrow 19x_2 = 24 \Rightarrow x_2 = 24/19 \approx 1.263
$$

Then $x_1 = 3 - 1.5(24/19) = (57 - 36)/19 = 21/19 \approx 1.105$.

$\lambda_2 = -4 + 4(24/19) = (-76 + 96)/19 = 20/19 \approx 1.053 \geq 0$ ✓

**Verify:** $x_1 + x_2 = 45/19 \approx 2.37 > 2$ — Wait, this violates $g_1 \leq 0$!

So $g_1$ should be active too. Let's go back to step 7 carefully.

**Revised Step 7: Try $\{g_1, g_2\}$ active with $x_1, x_2 > 0$:**
- $g_3$ inactive ($\lambda_3 = 0$), $g_4$ inactive ($\lambda_4 = 0$).
- $x_1 + x_2 = 2$, $2x_1 + 3x_2 = 6 \Rightarrow x_2 = 2$, $x_1 = 0$.

But then $x_1 = 0$ means $g_3$ is active too! We return to the same case.

**Try $\{g_2\}$ only active with $x_1, x_2 > 0$:** We had $(21/19, 24/19)$. Check feasibility:
- $g_1 = 21/19 + 24/19 - 2 = 45/19 - 38/19 = 7/19 > 0$ ✗ Infeasible!

This means the optimal point is at a vertex. Try vertex $(0, 0)$: $f = 0$.
Try vertex $(0, 2)$: $f = 0 + 8 - 0 - 0 - 12 = -4$.
Try vertex on $x_2 = 0$ axis: From $g_2$, $x_1 = 3$. Check $g_1$: $3 > 2$ ✗.
Intersect $g_1$ with $x_1$-axis: $x_1 = 2, x_2 = 0$. $f = 8 + 0 - 0 - 8 - 0 = 0$.

So **minimum is at $(0, 2)$** with $f^* = -4$. We already showed the KKT system gives no valid interior solution; the active constraint set $\{g_1, g_2, g_3\}$ at $(0, 2)$ led to contradiction, so let's verify directly.

At $(0, 2)$: $\nabla f = (-4 - 2x_2, -6 - 2x_1 + \text{cross terms})$... Let me recompute. Actually $f = 2x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 - 2x_1 x_2 - 4x_1 - 6x_2$, so $\nabla f = (4x_1 - 2x_2 - 4, 4x_2 - 2x_1 - 6)$. At $(0,2)$: $\nabla f = (-8, 2)$.

Active constraints: $g_1, g_2, g_3$ (with $g_4$ inactive, $\lambda_4 = 0$).

KKT: $(-8, 2) + \lambda_1(1,1) + \lambda_2(2,3) + \lambda_3(-1, 0) = (0, 0)$

From x-component: $-8 + \lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2 - \lambda_3 = 0$
From y-component: $2 + \lambda_1 + 3\lambda_2 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 + 3\lambda_2 = -2$

Since we need $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3 \geq 0$, this is impossible (right-hand side is negative, but all multipliers non-negative, sum cannot be negative). 

**Issue:** This indicates our earlier KKT derivation was correct but the hypothesis leads to infeasible multipliers. The point $(0,2)$ satisfies feasibility but **NOT KKT**, meaning either it's not optimal or constraint qualification fails.

But wait — at $(0,2)$, the gradient $\nabla f = (-8, 2)$ has negative $x_1$-component, suggesting moving in $+x_1$ direction decreases $f$. But constraint $g_3: -x_1 \leq 0$ blocks this — so the point IS a local optimum even though KKT system has no solution. This indicates **LICQ fails** at $(0,2)$ because gradients $\nabla g_1, \nabla g_2, \nabla g_3$ are:

$(1,1), (2,3), (-1,0)$. Determinant of $[\nabla g_1; \nabla g_3]$ = $0 \cdot 1 - 1 \cdot (-1) = 1 \neq 0$. Actually these are linearly independent! Let me recompute KKT properly.

Actually the issue is more subtle. The point $(0,2)$ has $g_1 = 0$, $g_2 = 0$, $g_3 = 0$ all active. But the multipliers must satisfy:

$-8 + \lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2 - \lambda_3 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2 - \lambda_3 = 8$
$2 + \lambda_1 + 3\lambda_2 + 0 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 + 3\lambda_2 = -2$

From second: $\lambda_1 = -2 - 3\lambda_2$. For $\lambda_1 \geq 0$, need $\lambda_2 \leq -2/3$. But $\lambda_2 \geq 0$. Contradiction. **KKT fails at $(0,2)$.**

This means the **true optimum** is not at $(0,2)$. Let me reconsider. At $(0,2)$, $f = -4$. Let's check neighboring points:
- $(0.1, 1.9)$: $f = 0.02 + 7.22 - 0.38 - 0.4 - 11.4 = -4.94$. **Lower!**
- This is feasible since $x_1 + x_2 = 2$, $2x_1 + 3x_2 = 5.9 \leq 6$.

So $(0,2)$ is NOT optimal. My earlier analysis was correct: minimum is interior to a constraint.

**The Correct Optimal Solution via KKT:**
From earlier, when we tried $\{g_2\}$ only active (treating $x_1, x_2$ as interior in $x_1, x_2 > 0$), we got $(21/19, 24/19)$, but $g_1$ was violated. So $g_1$ must be active too.

**Try $\{g_1, g_2\}$ active** with $x_1, x_2 > 0$:
- $x_1 + x_2 = 2$, $2x_1 + 3x_2 = 6 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0, x_2 = 2$ (forced).
- But this gives $x_1 = 0$, contradiction with $x_1 > 0$.

**Try $\{g_1\}$ only active** with $x_1, x_2 > 0$, $g_2$ inactive ($\lambda_2 = 0$):
- $g_3, g_4$ inactive: $\lambda_3 = \lambda_4 = 0$.
- $x_1 + x_2 = 2 \Rightarrow x_2 = 2 - x_1$.

KKT equations:
$4x_1 - 2x_2 - 4 + \lambda_1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 4 + 2x_2 - 4x_1$
$4x_2 - 2x_1 - 6 + \lambda_1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 6 + 2x_1 - 4x_2$

Setting equal:
$4 + 2x_2 - 4x_1 = 6 + 2x_1 - 4x_2$
$6x_2 - 6x_1 = 2 \Rightarrow x_2 - x_1 = 1/3$

Combined with $x_1 + x_2 = 2$: $2x_2 = 7/3 \Rightarrow x_2 = 7/6$, $x_1 = 5/6$.

$\lambda_1 = 4 + 2(7/6) - 4(5/6) = 4 + 14/6 - 20/6 = 4 - 1 = 3 \geq 0$ ✓

**Verify:** $g_2 = 2(5/6) + 3(7/6) - 6 = 10/6 + 21/6 - 36/6 = -5/6 < 0$ ✓ Inactive confirmed.

**Optimal Solution:** $x_1^* = 5/6, x_2^* = 7/6$, $\lambda_1^* = 3$, others zero.

$f^* = 2(25/36) + 2(49/36) - 2(5/6)(7/6) - 4(5/6) - 6(7/6)$
$= 50/36 + 98/36 - 70/36 - 20/6 - 42/6$
$= 78/36 - 62/6$
$= 78/36 - 372/36 = -294/36 = -49/6 \approx -8.167$

This is indeed lower than $f(0,2) = -4$. ✓

### Part (b): Verify Sufficiency Using Convexity (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute the Hessian of $f$:**

The objective is $f = 2x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 - 2x_1 x_2 - 4x_1 - 6x_2$.

$$
H = \begin{pmatrix} \partial^2 f / \partial x_1^2 & \partial^2 f / \partial x_1 \partial x_2 \\ \partial^2 f / \partial x_2 \partial x_1 & \partial^2 f / \partial x_2^2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Check Positive Semi-Definiteness:**

Leading principal minors:
- $\Delta_1 = 4 > 0$
- $\Delta_2 = (4)(4) - (-2)(-2) = 16 - 4 = 12 > 0$

Since both leading principal minors are positive, $H$ is **positive definite**. Therefore $f$ is **strictly convex**. ✓

**Step 3: Check constraint convexity:**

All constraints $g_1, g_2, g_3, g_4$ are **linear**, hence both convex and concave. ✓

**Step 4: Conclude sufficiency:**

Since $f$ is convex and all constraints are linear (convex), this is a **convex programming problem**. For convex programs, the KKT conditions are **both necessary and sufficient** for global optimality.

Therefore, $\mathbf{x}^* = (5/6, 7/6)^T$ is the **global minimum** of the given NLP with $f^* = -49/6$.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Alternative Set]**

> **Q:** A manufacturing firm produces two products. The cost function is $C(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 6x_1 - 8x_2 + 25$. The production is constrained by:
> $$ x_1 + x_2 \leq 5, \quad 2x_1 + x_2 \leq 8, \quad x_1, x_2 \geq 0 $$
> Use the **Kuhn-Tucker method** to find the optimal production levels and minimum cost.

### Part (a): Formulate and Solve Using KKT (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Standardize constraints:**
$$
g_1: x_1 + x_2 - 5 \leq 0
$$
$$
g_2: 2x_1 + x_2 - 8 \leq 0
$$
$$
g_3: -x_1 \leq 0
$$
$$
g_4: -x_2 \leq 0
$$

**Step 2: Lagrangian:**

$$
\mathcal{L} = x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 6x_1 - 8x_2 + 25 + \lambda_1(x_1 + x_2 - 5) + \lambda_2(2x_1 + x_2 - 8) - \lambda_3 x_1 - \lambda_4 x_2
$$

**Step 3: Stationarity:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 - 6 + \lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2 - \lambda_3 = 0 \quad (I)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2 - 8 + \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 - \lambda_4 = 0 \quad (II)
$$

**Step 4: Test active set $\{g_1\}$ only:** $x_1 + x_2 = 5$, $x_1, x_2 > 0$, $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = \lambda_4 = 0$.

From (I): $2x_1 - 6 + \lambda_1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 6 - 2x_1$
From (II): $2x_2 - 8 + \lambda_1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 8 - 2x_2$

Setting equal: $6 - 2x_1 = 8 - 2x_2 \Rightarrow x_2 - x_1 = 1$

Combined with $x_1 + x_2 = 5$: $x_2 = 3, x_1 = 2$.

$\lambda_1 = 6 - 4 = 2 \geq 0$ ✓

**Verify feasibility of $g_2$:** $g_2(2,3) = 4 + 3 - 8 = -1 \leq 0$ ✓ Inactive confirmed.

**Optimal:** $x_1^* = 2, x_2^* = 3, \lambda_1^* = 2$.

### Part (b): Compute Minimum Cost and Verify Global Optimality (7 Marks)

**Step 5: Minimum Cost:**

$$
C^* = (2)^2 + (3)^2 - 6(2) - 8(3) + 25 = 4 + 9 - 12 - 24 + 25 = 2
$$

**Step 6: Convexity Verification:**

Hessian of $C$:
$$
H = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Both eigenvalues are $2 > 0$, so $H$ is positive definite $\Rightarrow C$ is strictly convex.

All constraints are linear (convex), so this is a **convex program** and KKT is sufficient for **global optimum**.

**Interpretation:** The firm should produce **2 units of product 1** and **3 units of product 2** for a **minimum cost of ₹2 (or cost units)**. The shadow price $\lambda_1^* = 2$ indicates that relaxing the resource constraint $x_1 + x_2 \leq 5$ by one unit would decrease the cost by 2 units.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks (and How to Avoid Them)**
>
> **Pitfall 1: Wrong Sign Convention (–2 to –3 marks)**
> - Always write constraints as $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$.
> - If given $x_1 + x_2 \geq 5$, rewrite as $5 - x_1 - x_2 \leq 0$ before forming Lagrangian.
>
> **Pitfall 2: Forgetting Complementary Slackness (–2 marks)**
> - Many students solve stationarity equations but forget to verify that **inactive** constraints have $\lambda_j = 0$ and **active** ones have $\lambda_j \geq 0$.
>
> **Pitfall 3: Not Checking All Hypotheses (–3 to –4 marks)**
> - When multiple constraints exist, you must test multiple active sets. A point is optimal only if **at least one** hypothesis yields valid KKT multipliers.
>
> **Pitfall 4: Missing Sufficiency Check (–2 marks)**
> - KTU expects you to verify that the problem is convex (Hessian PSD) before claiming global optimality.
>
> **Pitfall 5: Confusing $\nabla g_j$ in Stationarity (–1 mark)**
> - The stationarity condition is $\nabla f + \sum \lambda_j \nabla g_j = \mathbf{0}$, **NOT** $\nabla f = \sum \lambda_j \nabla g_j$. The sign is the same for $g_j \leq 0$ convention with $\lambda_j \geq 0$.
>
> **Pitfall 6: Skipping Feasibility Verification (–1 mark)**
> - Always confirm $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq 0$ and $h_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ at the end. A KKT point is meaningful only if feasible.

---

## 5.5 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Kuhn-Tucker Theorems**

### Core Concepts (Must Know)
- [ ] **KKT Generalizes Lagrange Multipliers** to inequality constraints.
- [ ] **Four Conditions**: Stationarity, Primal Feasibility, Dual Feasibility ($\lambda_j \geq 0$), Complementary Slackness ($\lambda_j g_j = 0$).
- [ ] **Lagrangian Form**: $\mathcal{L} = f + \sum \lambda_j g_j + \sum \mu_k h_k$ with $g_j \leq 0$ and $\lambda_j \geq 0$.

### Computational Steps (Must Practice)
- [ ] **Step 1**: Standardize all constraints as $g_j \leq 0$.
- [ ] **Step 2**: Form Lagrangian function.
- [ ] **Step 3**: Write stationarity equations $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial x_i = 0$.
- [ ] **Step 4**: Hypothesize active constraints, apply complementary slackness.
- [ ] **Step 5**: Solve the system for $x_i^*$ and $\lambda_j^*$.
- [ ] **Step 6**: Verify dual feasibility ($\lambda_j \geq 0$), primal feasibility ($g_j \leq 0$).
- [ ] **Step 7**: Verify sufficiency via convexity (Hessian PSD + linear constraints).

### Critical Theorems to Remember
- [ ] **KT Necessary Theorem**: Optimum + LICQ $\Rightarrow$ KKT holds.
- [ ] **KT Sufficient Theorem**: Convex $f$ + Convex $g_j$ + Linear $h_k$ + KKT holds $\Rightarrow$ Global optimum.
- [ ] **Constraint Qualification**: LICQ ensures KKT necessity holds.
- [ ] **Slater's Condition**: For convex programs, strict interior feasibility $\Rightarrow$ strong duality.

### Key Equations (Quick Reference)

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = \nabla f + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j \nabla g_j + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \mu_k \nabla h_k = \mathbf{0}
$$

$$
\lambda_j \geq 0, \quad g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad \lambda_j g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0
$$

### Common Problem Patterns in KTU Exams
- 2-variable, 2–4 constraints, convex objective $\Rightarrow$ **Always solvable by direct KKT**.
- Quadratic objective + linear constraints $\Rightarrow$ **KKT yields linear system**.
- Non-convex objective $\Rightarrow$ **KKT gives candidate, must verify local vs global**.
- Equality constraints only $\Rightarrow$ **Reduces to classical Lagrange Multipliers**.

### Quick Mnemonic: **"S-P-D-C"**
- **S**tationarity
- **P**rimal feasibility
- **D**ual feasibility
- **C**omplementary slackness

> **Remember the order:** S-P-D-C = "**S**ee **P**roblems, **D**eal with **C**onstraints" — a useful memory aid during the exam.

---

### Suggested Further Reading (KTU Library References)

- S.S. Rao, *Engineering Optimization: Theory and Practice*, 5th Edition, Wiley (2019).
- Kalyanmoy Deb, *Optimization for Engineering Design: Algorithms and Examples*, PHI Learning.
- H.W. Kuhn and A.W. Tucker, "Nonlinear Programming," *Proceedings of the 2nd Berkeley Symposium on Mathematical Statistics and Probability*, University of California Press, 1951, pp. 481–492.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Saddle Point Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET638 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Saddle Point Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous framework of **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)** and **Mathematical Optimization**, a **Saddle Point** is a critical point of a multivariate function $f(\mathbf{x})$ which is simultaneously a **local maximum** along one principal direction (or subspace) and a **local minimum** along another principal direction (or subspace) of the parameter space.

Mathematically, a point $\mathbf{x}^* \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is a **saddle point** of the function $f(\mathbf{x})$ if it satisfies the directional inequality:

$$f(\mathbf{x}^* + \alpha \mathbf{d}_1) \leq f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x}^* + \beta \mathbf{d}_2)$$

for all sufficiently small $\alpha > 0$ and $\beta > 0$ and for some non-zero direction vectors $\mathbf{d}_1, \mathbf{d}_2 \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** A saddle point is a stationary point where the **Hessian Matrix** is **indefinite** (i.e., it possesses both positive and negative eigenvalues). It is **NEITHER** a strict local maximum **NOR** a strict local minimum in the full-dimensional space.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Pass

Imagine a hiker traversing a mountain ridge. The hiker stands at a specific point on the ridge. If the hiker walks **along** the ridge (one direction), the elevation **decreases** on both sides — making it a local **maximum** in the direction perpendicular to the ridge. However, if the hiker walks **across** the ridge (perpendicular direction), the elevation **increases** as they go down into the valley — making it a local **minimum** along the ridge.

This point is **neither the top of the mountain nor the bottom of the valley**; it is a strategic "pass" or "col" — hence the name **saddle point**.

| Geometric Feature | Mathematical Counterpart |
| :--- | :--- |
| Mountain Top | Local Maximum |
| Valley Bottom | Local Minimum |
| Mountain Pass (Ridge) | **Saddle Point** |
| Flat Plains | Inflection / Stationary Point |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** In **structural mechanics**, saddle points represent **bifurcation equilibria** — configurations where the structure is stable in some load directions and unstable in others. In **game theory**, the saddle point represents the **optimal mixed strategy** where neither player can unilaterally improve their payoff.

## 1.3 Standardized Lexicon (KTU Notation)

> [!NOTE]
> - **$f(\mathbf{x})$** — The objective (scalar) function being analyzed.
> - **$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)$** — The gradient vector at the candidate point.
> - **$H(\mathbf{x})$** — The **Hessian Matrix** of second partial derivatives.
> - **$\mathbf{x}^*$** — The candidate saddle point.
> - **$\lambda$** — The **Lagrange Multiplier** (introduced via Lagrangian reformulation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Saddle Point

A point $\mathbf{x}^*$ can be classified as a saddle point only if the following **three-tier hierarchy** of conditions is satisfied:

### Tier 1: First-Order Necessary Condition (Stationarity)

The gradient must vanish — this is a *prerequisite* for *all* unconstrained critical points (maxima, minima, and saddle points alike):

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$

This means every first partial derivative must equal **zero**:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}\bigg|_{\mathbf{x}^*} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2}\bigg|_{\mathbf{x}^*} = \cdots = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}\bigg|_{\mathbf{x}^*} = 0$$

### Tier 2: Second-Order Necessary Condition (Indefiniteness)

The Hessian matrix evaluated at $\mathbf{x}^*$ must be **indefinite**. Formally:

$$\exists \; \mathbf{d} \in \mathbb{R}^n : \mathbf{d}^T H(\mathbf{x}^*) \mathbf{d} < 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad \exists \; \mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^n : \mathbf{p}^T H(\mathbf{x}^*) \mathbf{p} > 0$$

### Tier 3: Sufficient Condition

Conversely, if $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ **AND** the Hessian $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **indefinite**, then $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **saddle point**. This is both necessary and sufficient under the assumption that $f$ is twice continuously differentiable ($C^2$).

## 2.2 The Lagrangian Saddle Point (KTU High-Yield Concept)

For a constrained optimization problem of the form:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x}} f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

we construct the **Lagrangian function**:

$$L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j g_j(\mathbf{x})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Saddle Point Theorem (Kuhn-Tucker Framework):**
> A point $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **local minimum** of $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ if and only if there exists a multiplier vector $\boldsymbol{\lambda}^*$ such that $(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*)$ is a **saddle point** of $L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda})$ with respect to $(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda})$:
>
> $$L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) \leq L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) \leq L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) \quad \forall \; \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n, \; \forall \; \boldsymbol{\lambda} \geq \mathbf{0}$$

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Condition

| Condition | Physical / Geometric Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$ | The point lies on a level set with **zero slope** in every direction. |
| $H$ indefinite | The curvature of $f$ **bends up** in some directions and **bends down** in others. |
| Lagrangian saddle form | Encodes **primal feasibility** and **dual feasibility** simultaneously. |
| $\lambda \geq 0$ | Ensures **complementary slackness** — active constraints carry positive multipliers. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Code)

| S.No. | Condition | Mathematical Form | Test Type |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | First-Order Stationarity | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | Necessary for all critical points |
| 2 | Hessian Indefiniteness | $\det(H)$ and principal minors of mixed sign | Necessary & Sufficient for saddle |
| 3 | Local Min Criterion | $H \succ 0$ (positive definite) | Strict minimum |
| 4 | Local Max Criterion | $H \prec 0$ (negative definite) | Strict maximum |
| 5 | Eigenvalue Test | $\lambda_i(H) > 0$ and $\lambda_j(H) < 0$ for some $i,j$ | Indefiniteness confirmed |
| 6 | Lagrangian Saddle Form | $L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) \leq L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) \leq L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*)$ | Constrained optima |
| 7 | Complementary Slackness | $\lambda_j^* g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ for all $j$ | Active constraint identification |
| 8 | Dual Feasibility | $\lambda_j^* \geq 0$ for all $j$ | Multiplier sign constraint |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Engineering:** Saddle point formulations are the cornerstone of **finite element analysis** (mixed formulations), **optimal control** (Pontryagin's minimum principle), and **interior point methods** in convex optimization. The identification of saddle points prevents convergence to **degenerate equilibria** in power systems and robotic manipulator trajectories.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Hessian Must Be Indefinite

Consider a twice continuously differentiable scalar function $f(\mathbf{x})$ near a critical point $\mathbf{x}^*$ where $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$. We use a second-order **Taylor expansion** to characterize the local behavior:

$$f(\mathbf{x}^* + \mathbf{h}) = f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)^T \mathbf{h} + \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{h}^T H(\mathbf{x}^*) \mathbf{h} + O(\|\mathbf{h}\|^3)$$

Since $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$, the first-order term vanishes:

$$f(\mathbf{x}^* + \mathbf{h}) - f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{h}^T H(\mathbf{x}^*) \mathbf{h} + O(\|\mathbf{h}\|^3)$$

For sufficiently small $\mathbf{h}$ (so that the cubic remainder $O(\|\mathbf{h}\|^3)$ is negligible), the sign of the difference is governed entirely by the **quadratic form** $\mathbf{h}^T H \mathbf{h}$.

**Step 1:** Suppose $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **positive definite**. Then for all $\mathbf{h} \neq \mathbf{0}$:
$$\mathbf{h}^T H \mathbf{h} > 0 \implies f(\mathbf{x}^* + \mathbf{h}) > f(\mathbf{x}^*)$$
This implies $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **strict local minimum** — **NOT** a saddle point.

**Step 2:** Suppose $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **negative definite**. Then for all $\mathbf{h} \neq \mathbf{0}$:
$$\mathbf{h}^T H \mathbf{h} < 0 \implies f(\mathbf{x}^* + \mathbf{h}) < f(\mathbf{x}^*)$$
This implies $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **strict local maximum** — **NOT** a saddle point.

**Step 3:** Suppose $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **indefinite** (eigenvalues of both signs exist). Then there exist directions $\mathbf{h}_+$ and $\mathbf{h}_-$ such that:
$$\mathbf{h}_+^T H \mathbf{h}_+ > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \mathbf{h}_-^T H \mathbf{h}_- < 0$$

Therefore, moving along $\mathbf{h}_+$ **increases** $f$ (locally maximizing), and moving along $\mathbf{h}_-$ **decreases** $f$ (locally minimizing). By definition, $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **saddle point**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Example: Classifying a Critical Point

**Problem:** Find and classify the critical point of $f(x, y) = x^2 - y^2 + 3xy - 6x + 2y$ using saddle point conditions.

### Step 1: Compute First Partial Derivatives

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2x + 3y - 6$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = -2y + 3x + 2$$

### Step 2: Set Gradient to Zero and Solve

We solve the linear system:

$$2x + 3y - 6 = 0$$
$$3x - 2y + 2 = 0$$

From the second equation: $y = \frac{3x + 2}{2}$. Substitute into the first:

$$2x + 3 \left( \frac{3x + 2}{2} \right) - 6 = 0$$

$$2x + \frac{9x + 6}{2} - 6 = 0$$

$$4x + 9x + 6 - 12 = 0$$

$$13x = 6 \implies x = \frac{6}{13}$$

Substituting back:

$$y = \frac{3 \cdot \frac{6}{13} + 2}{2} = \frac{\frac{18}{13} + \frac{26}{13}}{2} = \frac{44}{26} = \frac{22}{13}$$

So the critical point is $\left( \frac{6}{13}, \frac{22}{13} \right)$.

### Step 3: Compute the Hessian Matrix

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \partial y} \\[10pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y \partial x} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 3 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: Test for Definiteness

Compute the leading principal minors:

$$D_1 = 2 > 0$$
$$D_2 = \det(H) = (2)(-2) - (3)(3) = -4 - 9 = -13 < 0$$

Because $D_1 > 0$ and $D_2 < 0$, the Hessian is **neither positive definite nor negative definite**. By the **Sylvester Criterion**, the Hessian is **indefinite**.

### Step 5: Classification

Since $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$ and $H$ is indefinite, the critical point $\left( \frac{6}{13}, \frac{22}{13} \right)$ is a **saddle point** of $f(x, y)$.

### Step 6: Eigenvalue Verification (Optional but Awarded in KTU Valuation)

Characteristic equation: $\det(H - \mu I) = 0$:

$$(2 - \mu)(-2 - \mu) - 9 = 0$$
$$\mu^2 - 4 - 9 = 0 \implies \mu^2 = 13 \implies \mu = \pm\sqrt{13}$$

Since eigenvalues are $\sqrt{13} > 0$ and $-\sqrt{13} < 0$, **indefiniteness is confirmed**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation: Saddle Point Theorem (Lagrangian Form)

**Theorem:** Let $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ and $g_j: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$. Suppose $f$ and all $g_j$ are continuously differentiable. If $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a local minimum of $f$ subject to $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$, then there exist multipliers $\lambda_j^*$ such that $(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*)$ is a saddle point of $L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_j \lambda_j g_j(\mathbf{x})$.

**Proof Sketch:**

**Part A (Upper Bound):** We must show $L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) \leq L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*)$ for all $\mathbf{x}$.

$$L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_j \lambda_j^* g_j(\mathbf{x}) \geq f(\mathbf{x}) \geq f(\mathbf{x}^*)$$

The first inequality holds because $\lambda_j^* \geq 0$ and $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ (feasibility). The second inequality holds because $\mathbf{x}^*$ is the local minimum. Now, since $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq 0$ and $\lambda_j^* g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ (complementary slackness):

$$L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) = f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_j \lambda_j^* g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = f(\mathbf{x}^*)$$

Combining: $L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) = f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x}) \leq L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*)$. $\checkmark$

**Part B (Lower Bound):** We must show $L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) \leq L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*)$ for all $\boldsymbol{\lambda} \geq \mathbf{0}$.

$$L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_j \lambda_j g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x}^*) = L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*)$$

The inequality holds because $\lambda_j \geq 0$ and $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq 0$, so each summand is $\leq 0$. $\checkmark$

Combining both bounds yields the saddle point inequality. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Saddle Point Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given f of x] --> B[Compute Gradient nabla f of x]
    B --> C{Is nabla f equals zero vector?}
    C -->|No| D[NOT a critical point]
    C -->|Yes| E[Compute Hessian Matrix H of x star]
    E --> F{Is H positive definite?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Classification: STRICT LOCAL MINIMUM]
    F -->|No| H{Is H negative definite?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Classification: STRICT LOCAL MAXIMUM]
    H -->|No| J{Is H indefinite?}
    J -->|No| K{Is H positive semidefinite?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Classification: DEGENERATE / INCONCLUSIVE]
    K -->|No| M{Is H negative semidefinite?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Classification: DEGENERATE / INCONCLUSIVE]
    M -->|No| O[Classification: SADDLE POINT]
    J -->|Yes| O
    O --> P[Verify with eigenvalue analysis]
    P --> Q{Eigenvalues of both signs?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[CONFIRMED: SADDLE POINT]
    Q -->|No| S[RECHECK calculation]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Lagrangian Saddle Point Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Problem Input Subsystem]
        P1[Objective Function f of x] --> P3
        P2[Constraint Functions g sub j of x] --> P3
        P3[Construct Lagrangian L of x and lambda] --> P4
    end

    P4 --> Q1{Lower Bound Holds for all lambda nonnegative?}
    P4 --> Q2{Upper Bound Holds for all x feasible?}

    Q1 -->|Yes| R1[Pass Condition 1]
    Q1 -->|No| R2[Fail Condition 1]
    Q2 -->|Yes| S1[Pass Condition 2]
    Q2 -->|No| S2[Fail Condition 2]

    R1 --> T1
    S1 --> T1
    R2 --> T2
    S2 --> T2

    T1[Verified: SADDLE POINT OF LAGRANGIAN] --> U[Extract optimal x star and lambda star]
    T2[Refute: NOT a saddle point] --> V[Adjust formulation]
```

## 4.3 Eigenvalue Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hessian Matrix H] --> B[Eigenvalue Spectrum Analysis]
    B --> C{All eigenvalues positive?}
    C -->|Yes| D[POSITIVE DEFINITE: Local Min]
    C -->|No| E{All eigenvalues negative?}
    E -->|Yes| F[NEGATIVE DEFINITE: Local Max]
    E -->|No| G{Mixed signs in eigenvalues?}
    G -->|Yes| H[INDEFINITE: SADDLE POINT]
    G -->|No| I[Degenerate Case]
    I --> J{Some eigenvalues zero?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Higher order test required]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a saddle point. What conditions must the Hessian matrix satisfy at a saddle point of a twice differentiable function?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> A **saddle point** of a function $f(\mathbf{x})$ is a point $\mathbf{x}^*$ that is a local maximum along at least one direction and a local minimum along at least one other direction, but is **neither** a local maximum **nor** a local minimum in the full parameter space.
>
> **Conditions:**
> 1. **First-order (necessary):** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ (gradient vanishes).
> 2. **Second-order (necessary & sufficient):** The Hessian matrix $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **indefinite**, meaning it possesses at least one positive eigenvalue and at least one negative eigenvalue, OR equivalently, the quadratic form $\mathbf{d}^T H \mathbf{x}^*$ changes sign for different directions $\mathbf{d}$.
>
> *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for first-order condition, 1 mark for Hessian indefiniteness.)*

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Lagrangian Saddle Point Theorem. Why is it significant in constrained optimization?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Statement:** A point $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a local minimum of $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to constraints $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ if and only if there exists a multiplier vector $\boldsymbol{\lambda}^* \geq \mathbf{0}$ such that $(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*)$ is a **saddle point** of the Lagrangian $L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \lambda_j g_j(\mathbf{x})$, satisfying:
> $$L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) \leq L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) \leq L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) \quad \forall \, \mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda} \geq \mathbf{0}$$
>
> **Significance:** This theorem establishes the **duality** between the primal (constrained) and dual (unconstrained) problems. It is the theoretical foundation of the **KKT conditions** and enables solving constrained optimization problems via unconstrained techniques on the Lagrangian.
>
> *(Award 1.5 marks for statement, 1.5 marks for significance.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.A. (a)** [7 Marks] Find the critical points of the function $f(x, y) = x^3 - 3x^2 y + 3y^2$ and classify each using the second-order Hessian test.

#### Model Solution for Q.A. (a):

**Step 1: First Partial Derivatives**

$$f_x = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 3x^2 - 6xy$$

$$f_y = \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = -3x^2 + 6y$$

**Step 2: Solve the Stationarity System $f_x = 0$ and $f_y = 0$**

From $f_y = 0$: $-3x^2 + 6y = 0 \implies y = \frac{x^2}{2}$.

Substitute into $f_x = 0$:

$$3x^2 - 6x \left( \frac{x^2}{2} \right) = 0 \implies 3x^2 - 3x^3 = 0 \implies 3x^2(1 - x) = 0$$

This yields $x = 0$ (double root) and $x = 1$.

- For $x = 0$: $y = 0/2 = 0$. Critical point: $(0, 0)$.
- For $x = 1$: $y = 1/2$. Critical point: $(1, 1/2)$.

**Step 3: Hessian Matrix**

$$f_{xx} = 6x - 6y, \quad f_{yy} = 6, \quad f_{xy} = -6x$$

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 6x - 6y & -6x \\ -6x & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Classification at $(0, 0)$**

$$H(0, 0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Principal minors: $D_1 = 0$, $D_2 = 0 \cdot 6 - 0 = 0$. The Hessian is **positive semidefinite** (eigenvalues are $0$ and $6$). The standard test is **inconclusive** — higher-order analysis required. By inspection, $f(x, 0) = x^3$, which has an inflection at origin. So $(0, 0)$ is a **degenerate saddle**.

**Step 5: Classification at $(1, 1/2)$**

$$H(1, 1/2) = \begin{bmatrix} 6 - 3 & -6 \\ -6 & 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -6 \\ -6 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Principal minors: $D_1 = 3 > 0$, $D_2 = (3)(6) - (-6)(-6) = 18 - 36 = -18 < 0$.

Since $D_1 > 0$ but $D_2 < 0$, the Hessian is **indefinite**. Therefore, $(1, 1/2)$ is a **saddle point**.

**Final Answer:** The critical point $(1, 1/2)$ is a **saddle point**, while $(0, 0)$ is a **degenerate critical point** (inconclusive via Hessian test).

---

**Q.A. (b)** [7 Marks] For the constrained optimization problem $\min f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2$ subject to $x + y \geq 4$, formulate the Lagrangian and verify whether the solution forms a saddle point.

#### Model Solution for Q.A. (b):

**Step 1: Convert Inequality to Standard Form**

The constraint $x + y \geq 4$ is equivalent to $4 - x - y \leq 0$, i.e., $g(x, y) = 4 - x - y \leq 0$.

**Step 2: Construct the Lagrangian**

$$L(x, y, \lambda) = x^2 + y^2 + \lambda(4 - x - y)$$

**Step 3: Apply KKT Stationarity Conditions**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = 2x - \lambda = 0 \implies \lambda = 2x$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial y} = 2y - \lambda = 0 \implies \lambda = 2y$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \lambda} = 4 - x - y = 0 \implies x + y = 4$$

From the first two: $2x = 2y \implies x = y$. Combined with $x + y = 4$: $2x = 4 \implies x = 2, y = 2$.

Then $\lambda = 2(2) = 4 \geq 0$. $\checkmark$

**Step 4: Verify Saddle Point Form**

We need to confirm:

$$L(2, 2, \lambda) \leq L(2, 2, 4) \leq L(x, y, 4) \quad \forall \, x, y \in \mathbb{R}, \lambda \geq 0$$

**Lower Bound Check:** 

$$L(2, 2, \lambda) = 4 + 4 + \lambda(4 - 2 - 2) = 8 + 0 = 8$$

$$L(2, 2, 4) = 4 + 4 + 4(4 - 4) = 8$$

Thus $L(2, 2, \lambda) = 8 \leq 8 = L(2, 2, 4)$. $\checkmark$

**Upper Bound Check:** 

$$L(x, y, 4) = x^2 + y^2 + 4(4 - x - y) = x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 4y + 16$$

$$= (x-2)^2 + (y-2)^2 + 8 \geq 8 = L(2, 2, 4)$$

Thus $L(2, 2, 4) \leq L(x, y, 4)$. $\checkmark$

**Final Answer:** Both saddle point inequalities hold. Therefore, $(x^*, y^*, \lambda^*) = (2, 2, 4)$ is a **saddle point** of the Lagrangian, confirming that $(2, 2)$ is the constrained minimum with $f^* = 8$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.B. (a)** [7 Marks] Consider $f(x, y) = x^2 - 2xy + 3y^2 - 2x + y$. Find all critical points and identify which (if any) are saddle points.

#### Model Solution for Q.B. (a):

**Step 1: Compute First Partial Derivatives**

$$f_x = 2x - 2y - 2$$

$$f_y = -2x + 6y + 1$$

**Step 2: Solve the Stationarity System**

From $f_x = 0$: $2x - 2y = 2 \implies x - y = 1 \implies x = y + 1$.

Substitute into $f_y = 0$:

$$-2(y + 1) + 6y + 1 = 0 \implies -2y - 2 + 6y + 1 = 0 \implies 4y - 1 = 0 \implies y = \frac{1}{4}$$

Then $x = \frac{1}{4} + 1 = \frac{5}{4}$.

**Critical point:** $\left( \frac{5}{4}, \frac{1}{4} \right)$.

**Step 3: Hessian Matrix**

$$f_{xx} = 2, \quad f_{yy} = 6, \quad f_{xy} = -2$$

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 \\ -2 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Definiteness Test**

$D_1 = 2 > 0$. 

$D_2 = (2)(6) - (-2)(-2) = 12 - 4 = 8 > 0$.

Since $D_1 > 0$ and $D_2 > 0$, $H$ is **positive definite**.

**Classification:** The critical point $\left( \frac{5}{4}, \frac{1}{4} \right)$ is a **strict local minimum** — **NOT a saddle point**.

**Final Answer:** There is **one unique critical point** $\left( \frac{5}{4}, \frac{1}{4} \right)$ and it is a **local minimum**, not a saddle point.

---

**Q.B. (b)** [7 Marks] Using the saddle point theorem, prove that for a convex objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ and convex feasible region, any saddle point of the Lagrangian corresponds to a global minimum.

#### Model Solution for Q.B. (b):

**Given:**
- $f(\mathbf{x})$ is convex, i.e., $f(\theta \mathbf{x}_1 + (1-\theta) \mathbf{x}_2) \leq \theta f(\mathbf{x}_1) + (1-\theta) f(\mathbf{x}_2)$.
- $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ defines a convex feasible set.
- $(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*)$ is a saddle point of $L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \lambda_j^* g_j(\mathbf{x})$.

**To Prove:** $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a global minimum of $f$ over the feasible region.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** By the saddle point definition, for all $\mathbf{x}$ and all $\boldsymbol{\lambda} \geq \mathbf{0}$:

$$L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) \leq L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) \leq L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*)$$

**Step 2:** Consider the right inequality: $L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) \leq L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*)$.

Substituting the Lagrangian form:

$$f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_j \lambda_j^* g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_j \lambda_j^* g_j(\mathbf{x})$$

**Step 3:** Apply complementary slackness. Since $(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*)$ is a saddle point, $\lambda_j^* g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ for all $j$. Therefore:

$$f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_j \lambda_j^* g_j(\mathbf{x})$$

**Step 4:** Because $\lambda_j^* \geq 0$ and $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ for all feasible $\mathbf{x}$, the term $\sum_j \lambda_j^* g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$.

Therefore: $f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_j \lambda_j^* g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq f(\mathbf{x})$.

Combining:

$$f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_j \lambda_j^* g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq f(\mathbf{x})$$

**Step 5:** Conclusion. The inequality $f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x})$ holds for **all** feasible $\mathbf{x}$. Hence $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **global minimum** of $f$ over the feasible region. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deduction Zones:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the second-order test:** Students often stop after finding $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$ and assume the critical point is automatically a maximum or minimum. KTU examiners deduct **2 to 3 marks** if the Hessian test or eigenvalue verification is missing.
> 2. **Misinterpreting semidefiniteness:** A Hessian that is positive **semi**definite (with at least one zero eigenvalue) is **NOT** automatically a minimum. Higher-order analysis is required. Confusing this leads to a **2-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Sign errors in the Lagrangian formulation:** A common mistake is writing $L = f - \lambda g$ instead of $L = f + \lambda g$. This single sign error invalidates the entire KKT derivation and costs **3 marks**.
> 4. **Ignoring complementary slackness:** When verifying a saddle point of the Lagrangian, students often forget to verify $\lambda_j^* g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$. This is a mandatory condition worth **1 mark** in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Failing to verify both saddle inequalities:** A complete proof requires verifying BOTH the upper and lower bounds. Partial verification yields only **half marks** in Part B questions.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Saddle Point Conditions**

- **Definition:** A saddle point is a critical point that is a local max in some directions and a local min in others.

- **First-Order Test:** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ is a prerequisite for **any** unconstrained critical point.

- **Second-Order Test:** Saddle point exists $\iff$ Hessian $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **indefinite** (has both positive and negative eigenvalues).

- **Eigenvalue Shortcut:** Compute eigenvalues $\mu_i$ of $H$. Saddle point $\iff$ $\exists \, \mu_i > 0$ AND $\exists \, \mu_j < 0$.

- **Sylvester Criterion Recap:** For $2 \times 2$ Hessian, indefinite $\iff$ $D_1 D_2 < 0$ (principal minors of opposite sign).

- **Lagrangian Form:** $L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \lambda_j g_j(\mathbf{x})$.

- **Saddle Point Inequalities (must satisfy BOTH):**
  - $L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) \leq L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*)$ for all $\boldsymbol{\lambda} \geq \mathbf{0}$.
  - $L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) \leq L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*)$ for all feasible $\mathbf{x}$.

- **KKT Conditions (must satisfy ALL):** Stationarity, Primal Feasibility, Dual Feasibility, Complementary Slackness.

- **Game Theory Connection:** In two-player zero-sum games, the **saddle point** of the payoff matrix corresponds to a **pure strategy Nash equilibrium**.

- **Convexity Link:** Saddle points of the Lagrangian always exist under convexity assumptions (Slater's condition).

- **Engineering Applications:** Structural bifurcation, optimal control, finite element mixed methods, power system stability, and economic equilibrium modeling.

- **Common Error to Avoid:** Do **not** confuse a saddle point with a "max-min" or "min-max" — the saddle point is a single point, not a strategy pair.

- **Determinant Shortcut:** For $2 \times 2$ matrices, $H$ is indefinite $\iff$ $\det(H) < 0$ AND $D_1 \neq 0$.

- **Existence:** A saddle point may or may not exist; its existence is guaranteed for **strictly convex-concave** Lagrangians.

- **Uniqueness:** The primal solution $\mathbf{x}^*$ is unique, but the dual multipliers $\boldsymbol{\lambda}^*$ may be non-unique.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
