topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET638 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Second Order Optimality Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **unconstrained nonlinear optimization**, the **Second Order Optimality Conditions** are a set of mathematical criteria that use the **Hessian matrix** (the matrix of second-order partial derivatives of the objective function) to classify a **stationary point** $\bar{x}$ — a point where $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ — as a **local minimum**, a **local maximum**, or a **saddle point**.

Formally, let $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a twice continuously differentiable function ($C^{2}$ function), and let $\bar{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ be a stationary point. The Hessian of $f$ evaluated at $\bar{x}$ is the symmetric matrix

$$
H(\bar{x}) = \nabla^{2} f(\bar{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{1}^{2}} & \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{1}\partial x_{2}} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{1}\partial x_{n}} \\[2mm]
\dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{2}\partial x_{1}} & \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{2}^{2}} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{2}\partial x_{n}} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
\dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{n}\partial x_{1}} & \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{n}\partial x_{2}} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{n}^{2}}
\end{bmatrix}_{\bar{x}}
$$

The **Second Order Necessary Condition (SONC)** states that if $\bar{x}$ is a **local minimizer**, then $H(\bar{x})$ must be **positive semi-definite**.
The **Second Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC)** states that if $H(\bar{x})$ is **positive definite**, then $\bar{x}$ is a **strict local minimizer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3, students MUST master the precise distinction between **necessary** and **sufficient** conditions. Examiners frequently award partial credit for correctly identifying a point as stationary but penalize students who confuse positive semi-definiteness (necessary) with positive definiteness (sufficient).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Climber's Compass

Imagine you are standing blindfolded on a hilly terrain at night, and you must decide whether the spot beneath your feet is a **valley (minimum)**, a **peak (maximum)**, or a **mountain pass (saddle point)**.

- **The First Derivative (Gradient) — $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$:**
  This is your **spirit level**. When the bubble is perfectly centered, the ground is *flat*. You have found a "stationary point" — but you still don't know whether the flat patch is part of a valley floor, the top of a hill, or a mountain ridge. A flat patch on top of Mount Everest is still a flat patch, but it is definitely NOT a minimum!

- **The Second Derivative (Hessian) — Curvature:**
  This is your **tactile feedback** — the way the ground *curves* under your feet:
  - If the ground **curves upward in every direction** you step (like the inside of a bowl) → **positive definite Hessian** → you are in a **valley (minimum)**.
  - If the ground **curves upward in some directions but downward in others** (like a mountain pass) → **indefinite Hessian** → you are at a **saddle point**.
  - If the ground is **flat in at least one direction** (like a trough that runs straight) → **positive semi-definite Hessian** → inconclusive from second-order tests alone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In **structural engineering**, the Hessian is analogous to the **stiffness matrix** at an equilibrium configuration. A positive definite stiffness matrix indicates **stable equilibrium** (the structure returns to equilibrium under small perturbations). A positive semi-definite matrix indicates **neutral equilibrium** (the system can drift along a flat direction). An indefinite matrix corresponds to **unstable equilibrium** — the structure will collapse!

---

## 1.3 Why We Need Second Order Conditions

First order conditions ($\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$) are necessary for *any* type of stationary point — they cannot distinguish between minima, maxima, and saddle points. For example, for $f(x) = x^{3}$, we have $f'(0) = 0$, but $x = 0$ is clearly NOT a minimum. We need **second-order information** (curvature) to make this distinction, which is exactly what the Hessian provides.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Building Blocks

### 2.1.1 Recap of First Order Necessary Condition
If $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is continuously differentiable and $\bar{x}$ is a **local minimizer** (interior point), then:

$$
\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}
$$

This makes $\bar{x}$ a *stationary point* or *critical point*. However, **first-order conditions alone are insufficient** because they classify *all* stationary points (minima, maxima, saddle points) together.

### 2.1.2 Hessian Matrix Properties Recap

A symmetric matrix $H \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is classified as follows:

| Property | Definition | Equivalent Test |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Positive Definite (PD)** | $d^{T} H d > 0$ for all $d \neq \mathbf{0}$ | All leading principal minors $> 0$ (Sylvester's Criterion) |
| **Positive Semi-Definite (PSD)** | $d^{T} H d \geq 0$ for all $d \neq \mathbf{0}$ | All eigenvalues $\geq 0$ |
| **Negative Definite (ND)** | $d^{T} H d < 0$ for all $d \neq \mathbf{0}$ | All leading principal minors alternate in sign: $(-1)^{k} D_{k} > 0$ |
| **Negative Semi-Definite (NSD)** | $d^{T} H d \leq 0$ for all $d \neq \mathbf{0}$ | All eigenvalues $\leq 0$ |
| **Indefinite** | $d^{T} H d$ takes both positive and negative values | Eigenvalues of both signs exist |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse "positive definite" with "having all positive entries". A matrix can have negative entries and still be positive definite (e.g., $\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ is PD). Always test via eigenvalues or Sylvester's Criterion.

### 2.1.3 Eigenvalue Test (Most Reliable Method)

For a symmetric matrix $H$:
- All eigenvalues $\lambda_i > 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **Positive Definite**
- All eigenvalues $\lambda_i \geq 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **Positive Semi-Definite**
- All eigenvalues $\lambda_i < 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **Negative Definite**
- At least one $\lambda_i > 0$ and at least one $\lambda_j < 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **Indefinite**

---

## 2.2 The Three Canonical Theorems

### Theorem 2.1 — Second Order Necessary Condition (SONC) for a Local Minimum

**Statement:** Let $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be twice continuously differentiable at $\bar{x}$. If $\bar{x}$ is a **local minimizer** of $f$, then:

$$
\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0} \quad \text{AND} \quad H(\bar{x}) \text{ is positive semi-definite.}
$$

**Interpretation:** The Hessian *must* curve upwards (or be flat) in every direction. It cannot curve downwards in any direction, because that would make $f$ smaller nearby, contradicting minimality.

### Theorem 2.2 — Second Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC) for a Strict Local Minimum

**Statement:** Let $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be twice continuously differentiable at $\bar{x}$. If:

$$
\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0} \quad \text{AND} \quad H(\bar{x}) \text{ is positive definite,}
$$

then $\bar{x}$ is a **strict local minimizer** of $f$.

**Interpretation:** Strict upward curvature in *every* direction guarantees that $f(\bar{x})$ is strictly smaller than $f$ at any nearby point.

### Theorem 2.3 — Saddle Point Condition

**Statement:** If $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ and $H(\bar{x})$ is **indefinite**, then $\bar{x}$ is a **saddle point** (neither a local minimum nor a local maximum).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Statement | Mathematical Form | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Taylor's 2nd-order Expansion | $f(x) \approx f(\bar{x}) + \nabla f(\bar{x})^{T}(x-\bar{x}) + \tfrac{1}{2}(x-\bar{x})^{T} H(\bar{x})(x-\bar{x})$ | Foundation for SONC proof |
| 2 | Quadratic Form Definition (PD) | $d^{T} H d > 0, \forall d \neq \mathbf{0}$ | Direct positive definiteness test |
| 3 | Quadratic Form Definition (PSD) | $d^{T} H d \geq 0, \forall d \neq \mathbf{0}$ | Necessary condition test |
| 4 | SONC | $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ and $H(\bar{x}) \succeq 0$ | Local min $\Rightarrow$ Hessian PSD |
| 5 | SOSC | $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ and $H(\bar{x}) \succ 0$ | Hessian PD $\Rightarrow$ Strict local min |
| 6 | Saddle Point | $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ and $H(\bar{x})$ indefinite | Neither min nor max |
| 7 | Sylvester's Criterion (PD) | All leading principal minors $D_{k} > 0$ for $k=1,2,\ldots,n$ | Practical test for small $n$ |
| 8 | Directional Second Derivative | $\dfrac{d^{2} f}{d\alpha^{2}} \Big\vert_{\alpha = 0} = d^{T} H(\bar{x}) d$ | Curvature along direction $d$ |
| 9 | Strict Local Maximum SOSC | $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ and $H(\bar{x})$ negative definite | Mirror of SOSC |
| 10 | Limit of Ratio (limit form) | $\liminf_{\alpha \to 0} \dfrac{f(\bar{x} + \alpha d) - f(\bar{x})}{\alpha^{2}} = \tfrac{1}{2} d^{T} H(\bar{x}) d$ | Used in formal SONC proof |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In **machine learning**, second-order optimality conditions are the theoretical foundation for **Newton's Method**. The Hessian provides the curvature information needed to choose optimal step sizes, leading to quadratic convergence near the optimum. In **economics**, these conditions appear in **profit maximization** under smooth revenue/cost functions, where the Hessian of profit determines whether the equilibrium is a maximum, minimum, or saddle point.

---

## 2.4 Master Decision Tree for Classifying a Stationary Point

Given a point $\bar{x}$ where $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$, classify $\bar{x}$ as follows:

1. **Compute the Hessian** $H(\bar{x})$.
2. **Test definiteness** of $H(\bar{x})$:
   - If $H(\bar{x})$ is **Positive Definite** $\Rightarrow$ **Strict Local Minimum** (by SOSC).
   - If $H(\bar{x})$ is **Negative Definite** $\Rightarrow$ **Strict Local Maximum** (by symmetric SOSC).
   - If $H(\bar{x})$ is **Indefinite** $\Rightarrow$ **Saddle Point**.
   - If $H(\bar{x})$ is **Positive Semi-Definite** $\Rightarrow$ **Inconclusive** from second-order tests alone; need higher-order derivatives or further analysis.
   - If $H(\bar{x})$ is **Negative Semi-Definite** $\Rightarrow$ **Inconclusive** similarly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Proof of the Second Order Necessary Condition (SONC)

This is the most important derivation for the KTU examination and is a frequent **14-mark** question.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be twice continuously differentiable on $\mathbb{R}^{n}$. Assume $\bar{x}$ is a **local minimizer** of $f$.

### 3.1.2 Taylor's Theorem with Remainder

By **Taylor's Theorem** (with Peano remainder) applied to $f$ at $\bar{x}$:

$$
f(\bar{x} + h) = f(\bar{x}) + \nabla f(\bar{x})^{T} h + \tfrac{1}{2} h^{T} H(\bar{x}) h + o(\Vert h \Vert^{2})
$$

Here, $o(\Vert h \Vert^{2})$ denotes a term that goes to zero **faster** than $\Vert h \Vert^{2}$ as $h \to \mathbf{0}$, meaning:

$$
\lim_{h \to \mathbf{0}} \frac{o(\Vert h \Vert^{2})}{\Vert h \Vert^{2}} = 0
$$

### 3.1.3 Applying the First Order Necessary Condition

Since $\bar{x}$ is a local minimizer, the First Order Necessary Condition gives us:

$$
\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}
$$

So the Taylor expansion simplifies to:

$$
f(\bar{x} + h) = f(\bar{x}) + \tfrac{1}{2} h^{T} H(\bar{x}) h + o(\Vert h \Vert^{2})
$$

### 3.1.4 Local Minimality Implication

Since $\bar{x}$ is a **local minimizer**, there exists a neighbourhood $N(\bar{x})$ such that for all $x \in N(\bar{x})$:

$$
f(x) \geq f(\bar{x})
$$

Equivalently, for sufficiently small $h$:

$$
f(\bar{x} + h) - f(\bar{x}) \geq 0
$$

Substituting:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} h^{T} H(\bar{x}) h + o(\Vert h \Vert^{2}) \geq 0
$$

### 3.1.5 Restricting to a Particular Direction

Let $d \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ be any **nonzero direction** (i.e., $d \neq \mathbf{0}$). Choose $h = \alpha d$ where $\alpha$ is a small real number. Then $\Vert h \Vert = \vert \alpha \vert \cdot \Vert d \Vert$, and the inequality becomes:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} \alpha^{2} d^{T} H(\bar{x}) d + o(\alpha^{2} \Vert d \Vert^{2}) \geq 0
$$

### 3.1.6 Dividing and Taking Limit

Divide both sides by $\alpha^{2}$ (which is positive for $\alpha \neq 0$):

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} d^{T} H(\bar{x}) d + \frac{o(\alpha^{2} \Vert d \Vert^{2})}{\alpha^{2}} \geq 0
$$

Now take the limit $\alpha \to 0$:

$$
\lim_{\alpha \to 0} \left[ \tfrac{1}{2} d^{T} H(\bar{x}) d + \frac{o(\alpha^{2} \Vert d \Vert^{2})}{\alpha^{2}} \right] \geq 0
$$

Since $\frac{o(\alpha^{2} \Vert d \Vert^{2})}{\alpha^{2}} = \Vert d \Vert^{2} \cdot \frac{o(\alpha^{2})}{\alpha^{2}} \to 0$, we get:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} d^{T} H(\bar{x}) d \geq 0
$$

### 3.1.7 Conclusion

Therefore:

$$
d^{T} H(\bar{x}) d \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } d \neq \mathbf{0}
$$

This is exactly the definition of **positive semi-definiteness** of $H(\bar{x})$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Proof of the Second Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC)

### 3.2.1 Setup

Assume $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ and $H(\bar{x})$ is **positive definite**.

### 3.2.2 Taylor Expansion

By Taylor's Theorem, for any $h \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ with $\bar{x} + h$ near $\bar{x}$:

$$
f(\bar{x} + h) = f(\bar{x}) + \nabla f(\bar{x})^{T} h + \tfrac{1}{2} h^{T} H(\bar{x}) h + o(\Vert h \Vert^{2})
$$

Since $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
f(\bar{x} + h) - f(\bar{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2} h^{T} H(\bar{x}) h + o(\Vert h \Vert^{2})
$$

### 3.2.3 Using Positive Definiteness

Since $H(\bar{x})$ is **positive definite**, there exists a constant $m > 0$ (the **smallest eigenvalue** of $H(\bar{x})$) such that:

$$
h^{T} H(\bar{x}) h \geq m \Vert h \Vert^{2} \quad \text{for all } h \in \mathbb{R}^{n}
$$

Therefore:

$$
f(\bar{x} + h) - f(\bar{x}) \geq \tfrac{1}{2} m \Vert h \Vert^{2} + o(\Vert h \Vert^{2})
$$

### 3.2.4 Establishing Strict Local Minimum

Factor out $\Vert h \Vert^{2}$:

$$
f(\bar{x} + h) - f(\bar{x}) \geq \Vert h \Vert^{2} \left( \tfrac{1}{2} m + \frac{o(\Vert h \Vert^{2})}{\Vert h \Vert^{2}} \right)
$$

Since $\frac{o(\Vert h \Vert^{2})}{\Vert h \Vert^{2}} \to 0$ as $h \to \mathbf{0}$, for sufficiently small $h$:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} m + \frac{o(\Vert h \Vert^{2})}{\Vert h \Vert^{2}} > \tfrac{1}{4} m > 0
$$

Hence, for sufficiently small $h \neq \mathbf{0}$:

$$
f(\bar{x} + h) - f(\bar{x}) > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f(\bar{x} + h) > f(\bar{x})
$$

This proves that $\bar{x}$ is a **strict local minimizer**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Illustrative Numerical Example — Classifying a Stationary Point

**Problem:** Find all stationary points of $f(x, y) = x^{3} - 3xy^{2}$ and classify them using second order conditions.

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Compute the Gradient

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 3x^{2} - 3y^{2}, \qquad \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = -6xy
$$

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Solve $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$

From $\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 0$, we get $-6xy = 0$, so either $x = 0$ or $y = 0$.

- **Case A:** $x = 0$. Then $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = -3y^{2} = 0 \Rightarrow y = 0$. So we get $(0, 0)$.
- **Case B:** $y = 0$. Then $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 3x^{2} = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0$. Same point $(0, 0)$.

So $\bar{x} = (0, 0)$ is the **only stationary point**.

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Compute the Hessian

$$
H(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x^{2}} & \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x \partial y} \\[2mm] \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial y \partial x} & \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial y^{2}} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6x & -6y \\ -6y & -6x \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.3.4 Step 4 — Evaluate Hessian at $(0, 0)$

$$
H(0, 0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.3.5 Step 5 — Test Definiteness

The Hessian is the **zero matrix**, which is **positive semi-definite** (and also negative semi-definite). The second order tests are **inconclusive**.

### 3.3.6 Step 6 — Higher-Order Analysis

Looking at $f$ along the direction $y = 0$: $f(x, 0) = x^{3}$, which takes **both positive and negative values** near $x = 0$. Hence $(0, 0)$ is a **saddle point**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lesson:** When the Hessian is **PSD** but **not PD**, second-order conditions are **inconclusive**. Students must investigate higher-order terms or examine the function along specific directions. Failing to do so is a common cause of lost marks.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Confirming a Strict Minimum

**Problem:** Classify $\bar{x} = (1, 1)$ for $f(x, y) = x^{2} + y^{2} - 2x - 2y + 3$.

### Step 1 — Gradient

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2x - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 1, \qquad \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 2y - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow y = 1
$$

So $\bar{x} = (1, 1)$ is stationary.

### Step 2 — Hessian

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = 2I
$$

### Step 3 — Test Definiteness

Eigenvalues: $\lambda_{1} = \lambda_{2} = 2 > 0$. Therefore $H$ is **Positive Definite**.

### Step 4 — Apply SOSC

By Theorem 2.2, $\bar{x} = (1, 1)$ is a **strict local minimizer**. (In fact, since $f$ is convex, it is also the global minimizer.)

---

## 3.5 Summary of the Analytical Workflow

The complete analytical workflow for second-order optimality classification is:

1. **Find stationary points** by solving $\nabla f(x) = \mathbf{0}$.
2. **Compute the Hessian matrix** $H(x)$ of second partial derivatives.
3. **Evaluate** $H$ at each stationary point.
4. **Test definiteness** using eigenvalues, Sylvester's criterion, or the quadratic form.
5. **Classify** based on:
   - PD $\Rightarrow$ Strict local min
   - ND $\Rightarrow$ Strict local max
   - Indefinite $\Rightarrow$ Saddle point
   - PSD / NSD $\Rightarrow$ Inconclusive (need higher-order analysis)
6. **For inconclusive cases**, perform directional analysis along suspected descent directions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Optimality Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Stationary Point x_bar with grad f x_bar equals 0] --> compB{Compute Hessian H x_bar}
    compB --> eigTest{Test Definiteness of H x_bar}
    eigTest -- All eigenvalues greater than 0 --> pdNodeA[POSITIVE DEFINITE]
    pdNodeA --> resultMin[SOSC Satisfied]
    resultMin --> outMin[STRICT LOCAL MINIMUM]
    eigTest -- All eigenvalues less than 0 --> ndNodeB[NEGATIVE DEFINITE]
    ndNodeB --> resultMax[SOSC for Maximum Satisfied]
    resultMax --> outMax[STRICT LOCAL MAXIMUM]
    eigTest -- Both positive and negative eigenvalues --> indefNodeC[INDEFINITE]
    indefNodeC --> resultSaddle[Curvature in opposite directions]
    resultSaddle --> outSaddle[SADDLE POINT]
    eigTest -- All eigenvalues greater than or equal to 0 with at least one zero --> psdNodeD[POSITIVE SEMI DEFINITE]
    psdNodeD --> incon1[Second Order Tests INCONCLUSIVE]
    incon1 --> higher1[Investigate Higher Order Terms or Directional Analysis]
    higher1 --> classify1[Further Classification Required]
    eigTest -- All eigenvalues less than or equal to 0 with at least one zero --> nsdNodeE[NEGATIVE SEMI DEFINITE]
    nsdNodeE --> incon2[Second Order Tests INCONCLUSIVE]
    incon2 --> higher2[Investigate Higher Order Terms or Directional Analysis]
    higher2 --> classify2[Further Classification Required]
    classify1 --> endAll[Conclusion Reached]
    classify2 --> endAll
    outMin --> endAll
    outMax --> endAll
    outSaddle --> endAll
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Optimality Testing Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input Function f of x] --> gradMod[Gradient Computation Module]
    gradMod --> solveMod[Stationary Point Solver]
    solveMod --> critPoints[Critical Points List]
    critPoints --> hessMod[Hessian Matrix Assembly Unit]
    hessMod --> evalMod[Hessian Evaluator at Each Critical Point]
    evalMod --> defTest[Definiteness Testing Subsystem]
    defTest --> eigSub[Eigenvalue Analyzer]
    defSub --> sylSub[Sylvester Criterion Checker]
    eigSub --> classMod[Classification Module]
    sylSub --> classMod
    classMod --> outPD[Output Strict Local Min]
    classMod --> outND[Output Strict Local Max]
    classMod --> outINDEF[Output Saddle Point]
    classMod --> outINCON[Output Inconclusive Flag]
    outINCON --> advMod[Advanced Higher Order Analysis Module]
    advMod --> finalOut[Final Classification Report]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Logical Proof Flow of SONC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Assumption: x_bar is local minimizer] --> p2[FONC gives grad f x_bar equals 0]
    p2 --> p3[Apply Taylor Theorem 2nd order expansion]
    p3 --> p4[f x_bar plus h minus f x_bar equals half h transpose H h plus little o]
    p4 --> p5[Choose h equals alpha d for direction d]
    p5 --> p6[Divide by alpha squared]
    p6 --> p7[Take limit alpha to 0]
    p7 --> p8[Use little o term vanishes]
    p8 --> p9[Obtain d transpose H x_bar d greater than or equal to 0]
    p9 --> p10[H x_bar is POSITIVE SEMI DEFINITE]
    p10 --> endP[QED SONC PROVED]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Function Landscape Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    landA[PD Hessian Landscape] --> descA[Strict Local Minimum: Bowl shaped valley]
    landB[ND Hessian Landscape] --> descB[Strict Local Maximum: Inverted dome]
    landC[Indefinite Hessian Landscape] --> descC[Saddle Point: Mountain pass with opposing slopes]
    landD[PSD Hessian Landscape] --> descD[Valley with Flat Direction: Inconclusive test]
    landE[NSD Hessian Landscape] --> descE[Ridge with Flat Direction: Inconclusive test]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the Second Order Necessary Condition for a point to be a local minimum of an unconstrained optimization problem.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Second Order Necessary Condition (SONC) for a local minimum of $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ at $\bar{x}$ is:

1. **First Order Condition:** $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ (i.e., $\bar{x}$ is a stationary point).
2. **Second Order Condition:** The Hessian matrix $H(\bar{x}) = \nabla^{2} f(\bar{x})$ must be **positive semi-definite**, meaning $d^{T} H(\bar{x}) d \geq 0$ for all $d \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, $d \neq \mathbf{0}$.

Geometrically, the function's curvature at $\bar{x}$ must be non-negative in every direction. If the Hessian had a direction of negative curvature, the function value could be reduced by moving along that direction, contradicting minimality. SONC is only **necessary**, not sufficient.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between positive definite and positive semi-definite matrices. How are these properties used in second order optimality conditions?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Positive Definite (PD) | Positive Semi-Definite (PSD) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Quadratic Form | $d^{T} H d > 0$ for all $d \neq \mathbf{0}$ | $d^{T} H d \geq 0$ for all $d \neq \mathbf{0}$ |
| Eigenvalues | All $\lambda_i > 0$ | All $\lambda_i \geq 0$, at least one zero |
| Sylvester Criterion | All leading principal minors $> 0$ | All principal minors $\geq 0$ |

**Role in optimality:**
- **PD Hessian at stationary point** $\Rightarrow$ **Sufficient** condition for strict local minimum.
- **PSD Hessian at stationary point** $\Rightarrow$ **Necessary** condition for local minimum; but inconclusive.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Choose Either Option A OR Option B)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** State and prove the Second Order Necessary Condition for a local minimum in unconstrained optimization. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** Let $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be twice continuously differentiable at $\bar{x}$. If $\bar{x}$ is a local minimizer of $f$, then:
(i) $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$, and
(ii) $H(\bar{x}) = \nabla^{2} f(\bar{x})$ is positive semi-definite, i.e., $d^{T} H(\bar{x}) d \geq 0$ for all $d \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, $d \neq \mathbf{0}$.

**Proof:**

By Taylor's theorem with Peano remainder, for any $h \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ sufficiently small:

$$
f(\bar{x} + h) = f(\bar{x}) + \nabla f(\bar{x})^{T} h + \tfrac{1}{2} h^{T} H(\bar{x}) h + o(\Vert h \Vert^{2})
$$

Since $\bar{x}$ is a local minimizer, FONC gives $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$. So:

$$
f(\bar{x} + h) - f(\bar{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2} h^{T} H(\bar{x}) h + o(\Vert h \Vert^{2})
$$

By minimality, for all sufficiently small $h$: $f(\bar{x} + h) - f(\bar{x}) \geq 0$. Take $h = \alpha d$ with $d \neq \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} \alpha^{2} d^{T} H(\bar{x}) d + o(\alpha^{2}) \geq 0
$$

Divide by $\alpha^{2} > 0$:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} d^{T} H(\bar{x}) d + \frac{o(\alpha^{2})}{\alpha^{2}} \geq 0
$$

Taking $\alpha \to 0$, the term $\frac{o(\alpha^{2})}{\alpha^{2}} \to 0$, yielding $d^{T} H(\bar{x}) d \geq 0$ for all $d \neq \mathbf{0}$. Hence $H(\bar{x})$ is PSD. $\blacksquare$

**(b)** For $f(x, y) = x^{3} + y^{3} - 3xy$, find all stationary points and classify them using second order conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Gradient:**

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 3x^{2} - 3y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = x^{2}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 3y^{2} - 3x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = y^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Solving the system:**
Substituting $y = x^{2}$ into $x = y^{2}$: $x = (x^{2})^{2} = x^{4} \Rightarrow x^{4} - x = 0 \Rightarrow x(x^{3} - 1) = 0$.

So $x = 0$ or $x = 1$.

- If $x = 0$: $y = 0$. Stationary point $(0, 0)$.
- If $x = 1$: $y = 1$. Stationary point $(1, 1)$.

**Step 3 — Hessian matrix:**

$$
H(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} 6x & -3 \\ -3 & 6y \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluating at $(0, 0)$:**

$$
H(0, 0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -3 \\ -3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Eigenvalues: $\det(H - \lambda I) = \lambda^{2} - 9 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \pm 3$. Indefinite $\Rightarrow$ **Saddle Point**.

**Step 5 — Evaluating at $(1, 1)$:**

$$
H(1, 1) = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -3 \\ -3 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Sylvester's criterion: $D_1 = 6 > 0$, $D_2 = 36 - 9 = 27 > 0$. **Positive Definite** $\Rightarrow$ **Strict Local Minimum**.

**Step 6 — Minimum value:** $f(1, 1) = 1 + 1 - 3 = -1$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** State and prove the Second Order Sufficient Condition for a strict local minimum. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** Let $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be twice continuously differentiable at $\bar{x}$. If $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ and $H(\bar{x})$ is **positive definite**, then $\bar{x}$ is a strict local minimizer of $f$.

**Proof:**

Since $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$, Taylor's theorem gives:

$$
f(\bar{x} + h) = f(\bar{x}) + \tfrac{1}{2} h^{T} H(\bar{x}) h + o(\Vert h \Vert^{2})
$$

Since $H(\bar{x})$ is positive definite, its smallest eigenvalue satisfies $m = \lambda_{\min} > 0$, and for all $h$:

$$
h^{T} H(\bar{x}) h \geq m \Vert h \Vert^{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
f(\bar{x} + h) - f(\bar{x}) \geq \tfrac{1}{2} m \Vert h \Vert^{2} + o(\Vert h \Vert^{2}) = \Vert h \Vert^{2} \left[ \tfrac{m}{2} + \frac{o(\Vert h \Vert^{2})}{\Vert h \Vert^{2}} \right]
$$

Since $\frac{o(\Vert h \Vert^{2})}{\Vert h \Vert^{2}} \to 0$ as $h \to \mathbf{0}$, for sufficiently small $h \neq \mathbf{0}$, the bracketed expression exceeds $\tfrac{m}{4} > 0$. Hence:

$$
f(\bar{x} + h) - f(\bar{x}) > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f(\bar{x} + h) > f(\bar{x})
$$

for all sufficiently small $h \neq \mathbf{0}$. Therefore $\bar{x}$ is a strict local minimizer. $\blacksquare$

**(b)** Find and classify all stationary points of $f(x, y) = 2x^{3} + 2y^{3} - 6xy + 5$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Gradient:**

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 6x^{2} - 6y = 0 \Rightarrow y = x^{2}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 6y^{2} - 6x = 0 \Rightarrow x = y^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Solving:** Same procedure as Question A: $x(x^{3} - 1) = 0$, giving $(0, 0)$ and $(1, 1)$.

**Step 3 — Hessian:**

$$
H(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} 12x & -6 \\ -6 & 12y \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — At $(0, 0)$:**

$$
H(0, 0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -6 \\ -6 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Eigenvalues: $\lambda^{2} - 36 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \pm 6$. **Indefinite** $\Rightarrow$ **Saddle Point**.

**Step 5 — At $(1, 1)$:**

$$
H(1, 1) = \begin{bmatrix} 12 & -6 \\ -6 & 12 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$D_1 = 12 > 0$, $D_2 = 144 - 36 = 108 > 0$. **Positive Definite** $\Rightarrow$ **Strict Local Minimum**.

Function value: $f(1, 1) = 2 + 2 - 6 + 5 = 3$.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in Second Order Optimality Problems:**
>
> 1. **Confusing necessary with sufficient:** Writing "PSD Hessian $\Rightarrow$ local minimum" loses **2-3 marks**. PSD is only necessary; PD is sufficient.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ first:** The Hessian test is meaningless if the point isn't stationary.
> 3. **Incorrect Sylvester's Criterion:** For ND, students write all leading principal minors $> 0$ instead of $(-1)^k D_k > 0$. For PD, leading minors (not all principal minors) are checked.
> 4. **Misapplying the inconclusive case:** When $H(\bar{x})$ is PSD, students incorrectly declare it a minimum. They must acknowledge **inconclusiveness** and investigate higher-order terms or specific directions.
> 5. **Sign errors in second partials:** Computing $\frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x \partial y}$ vs $\frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial y \partial x}$ — these are equal for $C^2$ functions, but mistakes propagate into the Hessian.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **First Order Necessary Condition (FONC):** $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ — necessary but classifies minima, maxima, and saddle points together.
- **Second Order Necessary Condition (SONC):** $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ **and** $H(\bar{x})$ is **positive semi-definite** — required for local minimum.
- **Second Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC):** $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ **and** $H(\bar{x})$ is **positive definite** — guarantees strict local minimum.
- **Saddle Point Test:** $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ **and** $H(\bar{x})$ is **indefinite**.
- **Local Maximum:** $\nabla f(\bar{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ **and** $H(\bar{x})$ is **negative definite**.
- **Inconclusive Cases:** PSD or NSD Hessian requires higher-order or directional analysis.
- **Definiteness Tests:** (1) Eigenvalues (most reliable), (2) Sylvester's Criterion (leading principal minors), (3) Direct quadratic form check.
- **Workflow:** Find gradient → Solve $\nabla f = 0$ → Compute Hessian → Test definiteness → Classify.
- **Engineering Applications:** Stability analysis (stiffness matrix analogy), Newton's Method (machine learning), equilibrium analysis (economics and physics).
- **Common Pitfalls:** PSD $\neq$ PD; PSD alone is inconclusive; always verify stationarity before applying Hessian tests.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET638 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Generalized Lagrangian Multiplier Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Generalized Lagrangian Multiplier Method** (also widely known as the **Augmented Lagrangian Method (ALM)** or **Method of Multipliers**) is a powerful constrained optimization technique that combines the **structural elegance of the classical Lagrangian multiplier approach** with the **robustness of the penalty function method**. It was independently developed by **Hestenes (1969)** and **Powell (1969)** to overcome the critical ill-conditioning drawback inherent in pure penalty methods while retaining the ability to recover Lagrange multipliers at convergence.

For a standard Nonlinear Programming (NLP) problem:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
& g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, p \\
\text{where} \quad & \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Augmented Lagrangian Function** is rigorously defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_A(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}, r) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j h_j(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{r}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left[ h_j(\mathbf{x}) \right]^2 \\
+ \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mu_i \, g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{r}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{p} \left[ \max(0, g_i(\mathbf{x})) \right]^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, $\boldsymbol{\lambda} \in \mathbb{R}^m$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu} \in \mathbb{R}^p$ are the vectors of Lagrange multipliers, and $r > 0$ is the **penalty parameter**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Augmented Lagrangian Method is universally treated as the *third-generation* constrained optimization technique — it eliminates the need to drive the penalty parameter to infinity (as in pure penalty methods) by absorbing the role of constraint enforcement into shifting the multiplier estimates.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Driving Instructor & Fines" Analogy**
> 
> Imagine a driving instructor (the **optimizer**) teaching a student to park a car perfectly in a marked bay. The bay is the **feasible region**, and parking inside it is the **constraint**.
> 
> - **Penalty Method Analogy:** The instructor shouts louder and louder as the student drifts outside the bay, with the shouting volume (penalty $r$) going to infinity. But shouting too loud causes the student to panic (numerical ill-conditioning) and the steering becomes unresponsive.
> 
> - **Augmented Lagrangian Method Analogy:** The instructor does **two** things simultaneously: (1) shouts moderately when the car is out of line (the **quadratic penalty** keeps the car near the bay), AND (2) gently nudges the steering wheel in the corrective direction (the **multiplier term** $\lambda_j h_j$). The instructor remembers the previous nudges and gets smarter with each attempt (the **multiplier update rule**). The result: convergence is fast, the shouting never becomes unbearable, and the instructor can report the final steering nudges (the recovered **Lagrange multipliers**) at the end.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Penalty parameter increment factor ($\beta$):** Typically $\beta \in [2, 10]$, with $\beta = 2$ being the most common default in industrial solvers.
- **Convergence tolerance ($\epsilon$):** Standard KTU exam assumption is $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$.
- **Multipliers ($\lambda, \mu$):** Dimensionless scaling factors, signs carry economic interpretation in dual problems.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" — Motivation for Generalization

The standard Lagrangian $L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^T \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x})$ suffers from three critical limitations in practice:

| # | Limitation of Standard Lagrangian | How ALM Solves It |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Requires **convexity** for guaranteed convergence | Works for general **non-convex** problems |
| 2 | Cannot recover multipliers if unconstrained minimum lies on constraint boundary | **Exact recovery** of $\lambda^*, \mu^*$ |
| 3 | Saddle-point search is highly **ill-conditioned** near active constraints | Quadratic penalty term provides **strong numerical regularization** |

The **quadratic penalty addition** acts as a "safety net" — even if the multipliers are not yet perfectly tuned, the penalty term forces feasibility.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Intuition

- **Step 1 (Outer Loop - Multiplier Update):** After minimizing $\mathcal{L}_A$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$, the multipliers are updated using a **first-order Newton-like ascent** on the dual function.
- **Step 2 (Inner Loop - x Minimization):** The penalty parameter $r$ is *fixed* during the inner minimization, so the unconstrained minimizer of $\mathcal{L}_A$ is well-behaved.
- **Step 3 (Penalty Update):** Once the constraint violation falls below a threshold, the penalty parameter is *increased* (typically multiplied by $\beta$) to tighten the remaining infeasibility.
- **Step 4 (Convergence Check):** The algorithm terminates when both feasibility ($\Vert \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}) \Vert \leq \epsilon$, $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) \leq \epsilon$) and stationarity ($\Vert \nabla \mathcal{L}_A \Vert \leq \epsilon$) are satisfied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this work?** The Lagrangian multiplier update $\lambda_j^{(k+1)} = \lambda_j^{(k)} + r \cdot h_j(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$ is *mathematically equivalent* to maximizing the dual function $q(\boldsymbol{\lambda}) = \min_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L}_A(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, r)$ via **gradient ascent**. Thus ALM is performing **dual ascent** on a regularized Lagrangian.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Mathematical Expression | Purpose / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Augmented Lagrangian (Equality)** | $\mathcal{L}_A = f + \sum \lambda_j h_j + \frac{r}{2} \sum h_j^2$ | Combined objective for equality constraints |
| 2 | **Augmented Lagrangian (Inequality)** | $\mathcal{L}_A = f + \sum \mu_i g_i + \frac{r}{2} \sum [\max(0, g_i)]^2$ | Inequality handling via $\max$ operator |
| 3 | **Multiplier Update (Equality)** | $\lambda_j^{(k+1)} = \lambda_j^{(k)} + r^{(k)} \cdot h_j(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$ | First-order dual ascent |
| 4 | **Multiplier Update (Inequality)** | $\mu_i^{(k+1)} = \max \left\{ 0, \ \mu_i^{(k)} + r^{(k)} \cdot g_i(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) \right\}$ | Non-negativity enforcement |
| 5 | **Penalty Update Rule** | $r^{(k+1)} = \beta \cdot r^{(k)}, \quad \beta > 1$ | Tightening penalty progressively |
| 6 | **First-Order KKT Condition** | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum \lambda_j^* \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum \mu_i^* \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | Stationarity at optimum |
| 7 | **Complementary Slackness** | $\mu_i^* \cdot g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad \mu_i^* \geq 0$ | Active vs. inactive inequality |
| 8 | **Primal Feasibility** | $h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq 0$ | Constraint satisfaction at optimum |
| 9 | **Convergence Criterion** | $\Vert \mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} - \mathbf{x}^{(k)} \Vert \leq \epsilon$ | Algorithmic termination |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Safeguard:** In the formulas above, vertical bars such as $\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert$ represent the **Euclidean norm** (not absolute value), and are written safely outside markdown tables to preserve table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power System Optimization:** Optimal Power Flow (OPF) with security and voltage constraints.
- **Structural Mechanics:** Stress-constrained truss topology optimization.
- **Process Control:** Model Predictive Control (MPC) with state and input constraints.
- **Machine Learning:** Support Vector Machines (SVM) training uses ALM to handle the large dual problem efficiently.
- **Aerospace Trajectory:** Path planning with terminal boundary constraints and inequality path constraints.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Multiplier Update as Gradient Ascent on the Dual

**Starting Point:** Define the **dual function** for the augmented Lagrangian:

$$
q(\boldsymbol{\lambda}, r) = \min_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L}_A(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, r)
$$

The objective is to maximize $q(\boldsymbol{\lambda}, r)$ w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ to find the optimal multipliers. Taking the gradient:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\lambda_j} q(\boldsymbol{\lambda}, r) &= \frac{\partial}{\partial \lambda_j} \left[ \min_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L}_A \right] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

By the **envelope theorem** (the optimal $\mathbf{x}$ is independent of $\lambda_j$ at the minimum), we can swap the derivative and the min:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\lambda_j} q(\boldsymbol{\lambda}, r) &= \min_{\mathbf{x}} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_A}{\partial \lambda_j} \\
&= \min_{\mathbf{x}} \left[ h_j(\mathbf{x}) + r \cdot \lambda_j \cdot h_j(\mathbf{x}) \right] \quad \text{(partial derivative of } \mathcal{L}_A \text{ w.r.t. } \lambda_j \text{)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Wait — let me re-derive carefully.** Looking at the structure of $\mathcal{L}_A$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_A = f(\mathbf{x}) + \lambda_j h_j(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{r}{2} h_j^2(\mathbf{x})
$$

The partial derivative w.r.t. $\lambda_j$ is simply:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_A}{\partial \lambda_j} = h_j(\mathbf{x})
$$

Therefore, the gradient of the dual is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\lambda_j} q(\boldsymbol{\lambda}, r) &= h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{x}^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L}_A(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, r)$. Applying **gradient ascent** with step size $r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_j^{(k+1)} &= \lambda_j^{(k)} + r \cdot \nabla_{\lambda_j} q \\
&= \lambda_j^{(k)} + r \cdot h_j(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The multiplier update rule is a direct application of **gradient ascent** on the augmented dual function, with $r$ acting as the **step size**. This is why $r$ must be carefully chosen — too small and ascent is sluggish; too large and it diverges.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Optimality Conditions (First-Order KKT for ALM)

For a fixed $(\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{(k)}, r^{(k)})$, the inner minimization yields $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)}$ satisfying:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L}_A(\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^{(k)}, r^{(k)}) = \mathbf{0}
$$

Expanding the gradient:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L}_A &= \nabla f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}) + r \sum_{j=1}^{m} h_j(\mathbf{x}) \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}) \\
& \quad + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mu_i \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}) + r \sum_{i=1}^{p} g_i(\mathbf{x}) \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{g_i > 0} = \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbb{1}_{g_i > 0}$ is the **indicator function** (1 if $g_i(\mathbf{x}) > 0$, else 0). Define the **effective multipliers**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{\lambda}_j &\equiv \lambda_j + r \cdot h_j(\mathbf{x}) \\
\tilde{\mu}_i &\equiv \mu_i + r \cdot \max(0, g_i(\mathbf{x}))
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting these, the stationarity condition simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \tilde{\lambda}_j \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \tilde{\mu}_i \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**At convergence** ($h_j \to 0$ and $\max(0, g_i) \to 0$), we have $\tilde{\lambda}_j \to \lambda_j^*$ and $\tilde{\mu}_i \to \mu_i^*$, recovering the **standard KKT conditions exactly**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Augmented Lagrangian as Limit of Penalty Method

Start with the **quadratic penalty function**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(\mathbf{x}, r) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{r}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left[ h_j(\mathbf{x}) \right]^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The pure penalty method has a known **bias**: as $r \to \infty$, the minimizer $\mathbf{x}^*(r)$ approaches feasibility but **drifts away** from the true optimum $f(\mathbf{x}^*)$. Introduce a **shift variable** $\lambda$ to compensate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_A(\mathbf{x}, \lambda, r) &= f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j h_j(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{r}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{m} h_j^2(\mathbf{x}) \\
&= f(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{r}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left[ h_j(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{\lambda_j}{r} \right]^2 - \frac{1}{2r} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the last term is constant w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$, minimizing $\mathcal{L}_A$ is equivalent to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{x}} \left\{ f(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{r}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left[ h_j(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{\lambda_j}{r} \right]^2 \right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Insight:** If $\lambda_j^{(k)} \to \lambda_j^*$ (the optimal multiplier) as $k \to \infty$, then $h_j + \lambda_j / r$ remains bounded, and $r$ need **not** tend to infinity. The penalty stays finite, eliminating ill-conditioning.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Inequality Constraint Reformulation

A standard inequality $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ can be converted to an equality by introducing a **slack variable** $s_i \geq 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_i(\mathbf{x}) + s_i^2 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting into the augmented Lagrangian (ignoring the slack terms in the multiplier vector):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_A = f(\mathbf{x}) + \mu_i (g_i + s_i^2) + \frac{r}{2} (g_i + s_i^2)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Minimizing analytically w.r.t. $s_i$ (taking $\partial / \partial s_i = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \mu_i s_i + 2r s_i (g_i + s_i^2) &= 0 \\
s_i \left[ \mu_i + r(g_i + s_i^2) \right] &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $s_i \geq 0$ and $g_i + s_i^2 = 0$ at optimum, the second factor cannot be zero unless $\mu_i + r g_i = 0$. This gives the **closed-form replacement**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_A^{\text{ineq}} = f(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{r}{2} \left[ \max\left(0, g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{\mu_i}{r}\right) \right]^2 - \frac{\mu_i^2}{2r}
\end{aligned}
$$

The $\max(0, \cdot)$ operator is the celebrated **Rockafellar proximal operator** for the negative orthant.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([START: ALM Algorithm]) --> A1[Initialize x0, lambda0, mu0, r0, beta, epsilon]
    A1 --> A2{k = 0, 1, 2, ...}
    A2 --> A3[Fix lambda_k, mu_k, r_k]
    A3 --> A4[Inner Loop: Minimize L_A w.r.t. x]
    A4 --> A5[Obtain x star k+1]
    A5 --> A6[Check Constraint Violation]
    A6 --> A7{Violation less than epsilon}
    A7 -- No --> A8[Update Multipliers]
    A8 --> A9[lambda j new = lambda j + r times h j]
    A8 --> A10[mu i new = max of 0 and mu i + r times g i]
    A9 --> A11[Update Penalty Parameter]
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A12[r new = beta times r]
    A12 --> A13[k := k + 1]
    A13 --> A2
    A7 -- Yes --> A14[Check Stationarity: gradient L_A approx 0]
    A14 --> A15{Stationarity less than epsilon}
    A15 -- No --> A8
    A15 -- Yes --> A16[Recover Optimal Multipliers and x star]
    A16 --> A17([OUTPUT: x star, lambda star, mu star])
```
